recipes.name: Couscous Royal
recipes.description: Algeria's grandest couscous dish featuring fluffy steamed semolina topped with lamb, merguez sausage, and a fragrant vegetable broth loaded with chickpeas, turnips, and courgettes.
recipes.story: Couscous is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list, shared among the Maghreb nations. In Algeria, Friday couscous is a sacred family tradition. The Royal version brings together multiple meats and a rich vegetable broth, reserved for celebrations, weddings, and honoured guests. Steaming the couscous in a couscoussier above the simmering broth infuses every grain with flavour.
recipes.best_served_with: Buttermilk (lben) and harissa on the side
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.instruction: In the bottom of a couscoussier or large pot, brown the lamb in oil. Add the onion, tomato paste, 1 tsp each of ras el hanout, black pepper, and salt. Cover with 2 litres of water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer for 45 minutes.
steps.tip: Skim any foam that rises to the surface for a clear broth.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the chickpeas, turnips, and carrots to the broth. Continue simmering for 20 minutes. Then add the courgettes and cook for another 15 minutes until all vegetables are tender.
steps.tip: Add vegetables in stages so nothing overcooks.

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.instruction: While the broth simmers, place the couscous in a wide bowl. Sprinkle with salted water and rake with your fingers. Transfer to the steamer basket and steam over the broth for 20 minutes. Remove, fluff, and repeat the process once more.
steps.tip: Steaming twice gives the lightest, fluffiest couscous. Never let the couscous touch the broth directly.

steps.title: Cook the merguez
steps.instruction: Grill or pan-fry the merguez sausages until cooked through and charred on the outside, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Mound the couscous on a large serving platter. Arrange the lamb and merguez on top. Surround with the vegetables and ladle broth over everything. Serve with harissa on the side.
steps.tip: Serve the extra broth in a separate bowl so guests can add more to their liking.

recipes.story: Amiwo is a celebratory dish from southern Benin, always present at ceremonies and important gatherings. The red colour from the tomato sauce symbolises joy and festivity in Fon culture.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the tomato sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry onion until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add blended tomatoes and cook until reduced and thick, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Add crab
steps.instruction: Add crab meat to the sauce and stir. Cook for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Add cornmeal
steps.instruction: Add 3 cups water to the sauce. Bring to a boil. Gradually sprinkle in cornmeal while stirring constantly to prevent lumps.

steps.title: Cook the amiwo
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to low. Stir continuously for 15-20 minutes until the cornmeal is cooked through and pulls away from the pot sides.
steps.tip: The colour should be a vibrant red-orange.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape into a mound on a plate. Serve with fried chicken or grilled fish.

recipes.name: Kuli-Kuli
recipes.description: Crunchy groundnut crackers made by extracting the oil from peanut paste, then shaping and deep-frying the remaining cake. A beloved protein-rich snack.
recipes.story: Kuli-Kuli are the crunch of Beninese markets. Vendors shape the groundnut paste into sticks, rings, or balls before frying. They are eaten as snacks, crumbled into salads, or ground back into powder for soups.
recipes.best_served_with: On its own or crumbled over salads
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Grind the peanuts
steps.instruction: Blend roasted peanuts in a food processor until they form a thick paste, similar to peanut butter.

steps.title: Extract the oil
steps.instruction: Knead the paste vigorously, squeezing out as much oil as possible. The paste should become crumbly and dry. Reserve the extracted oil.
steps.tip: This step is crucial — the drier the paste, the crunchier the kuli-kuli.

steps.title: Season and shape
steps.instruction: Mix salt and cayenne into the paste. Shape into small sticks, rings, or flat rounds about 1cm thick.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C. Fry in batches until deep golden brown and very crunchy, about 4-5 minutes per batch.
steps.tip: Keep the heat moderate to fry through without burning.

steps.title: Drain and cool
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Let cool completely — they crisp up more as they cool. Store in an airtight container.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Seswaa
recipes.description: Botswana's national dish — beef slow-boiled until falling apart, then shredded and pounded to a tender, savory pulled-meat texture, seasoned simply with salt.
recipes.story: Seswaa is the pride of Botswana, served at weddings, funerals, national celebrations, and important family gatherings. The dish is traditionally prepared by men in a large three-legged cast-iron pot over an open fire. The simplicity of the recipe — just meat, water, and salt — allows the quality of Botswana's grass-fed beef to shine through.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the meat
steps.instruction: Place the beef pieces and quartered onion in a large heavy pot. Add enough water to cover the meat by about 5cm. Bring to a boil over high heat.
steps.tip: Bone-in cuts give the broth more flavor and body.

steps.title: Skim and simmer
steps.instruction: Skim off any foam that rises to the surface. Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer gently for about 3 hours until the meat is completely tender and falling off the bone.
steps.tip: Check occasionally and add more water if the level drops below the meat.

steps.title: Remove bones
steps.instruction: Remove the meat from the pot and discard all bones. Reserve the cooking broth. Place the meat on a large wooden board or in a mortar.

steps.title: Pound and shred
steps.instruction: Using two forks or a wooden pestle, pound and shred the meat until it has a fine, pulled texture. Season with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Traditionally this is done with a wooden pole in the pot itself — the pounding action is what gives seswaa its name.

steps.title: Moisten and serve
steps.instruction: Return the shredded meat to the pot and add a few ladles of the reserved broth to keep it moist. Heat through and serve with bogobe, pap, or dumplings.
steps.tip: The cooking broth is precious — serve some on the side as a gravy.

recipes.name: Riz Gras
recipes.description: Burkina Faso's rich one-pot rice cooked in a meat and tomato broth with vegetables. The rice is "fat" from absorbing all the flavours of the stock.
recipes.story: Riz Gras, meaning "fat rice," is the celebratory dish of Burkina Faso. The Mossi people prepare it for weddings, baptisms, and any occasion worthy of gathering. The rice cooks directly in the meat broth, absorbing every drop of flavour.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantain and a simple salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Season meat and brown on all sides. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Fry onions in the same pot until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add 5 cups water and the browned meat. Bring to a boil and simmer until meat is tender, about 25 minutes.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots and cabbage to the pot. Cook for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Add the washed rice to the pot, ensuring liquid covers it by about 2cm. Cover tightly and cook on low heat for 25 minutes without opening.
steps.tip: The rice should absorb all the flavoured broth.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fluff rice with a fork. Serve with the meat and vegetables arranged on top.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Koki Beans
recipes.description: Black-eyed peas blended into a smooth paste with palm oil and spices, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed into a savoury, custard-like cake. A Cameroonian classic.
recipes.story: Koki is a festive dish from the grasslands of western Cameroon, traditionally prepared by the Bamileke people for celebrations and rites of passage. The black-eyed peas are soaked, peeled, and blended into a smooth batter enriched with palm oil, then steamed in banana leaf parcels. The result is a dense, flavourful cake with a beautiful orange hue from the palm oil.
recipes.best_served_with: Ripe plantains, puff-puff, or on its own as a snack
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the beans
steps.instruction: Soak black-eyed peas overnight. Rub between your hands to loosen the skins, then rinse — the skins will float to the surface. Repeat until most skins are removed.
steps.tip: Peeling the beans is essential for a smooth texture. Patience here pays off.

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend the peeled beans with onion, scotch bonnet, and a splash of water until very smooth. Transfer to a large bowl and stir in palm oil and salt, mixing vigorously until the oil is fully incorporated.
steps.tip: The batter should be thick but pourable — add water sparingly.

steps.title: Wrap in banana leaves
steps.instruction: Soften banana leaves over a flame or in hot water. Place a ladleful of batter in the centre of each leaf and fold into a neat rectangular parcel, securing with string or toothpicks.
steps.tip: Make sure the parcels are sealed well to prevent water from getting in during steaming.

steps.title: Steam the koki
steps.instruction: Arrange parcels in a large steamer or pot with a raised platform and water at the bottom. Cover tightly and steam for 1 hour until firm and set.
steps.tip: Check water level every 20 minutes and top up if needed.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Unwrap the koki parcels and slice or serve whole. Enjoy warm with ripe plantains or on its own.
steps.tip: Koki can be reheated by re-steaming the next day.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Ndolé
recipes.description: Cameroon's national dish — bitter leaves cooked down with ground peanuts, crayfish, and prawns into a rich, complex stew. Earthy, nutty, and deeply satisfying.
recipes.story: Ndolé is the pride of the Douala people of coastal Cameroon and has been elevated to the status of national dish. The key ingredient — ndolé or bitter leaves — must be washed multiple times to reduce bitterness before being combined with ground peanuts and protein. The dish is labour-intensive but deeply rewarding, and no Cameroonian celebration is complete without it.
recipes.best_served_with: Boiled plantains, rice, or miondo (cassava sticks)
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Wash the bitter leaves
steps.instruction: Place bitter leaves in a large bowl of water, squeeze and wash vigorously to remove bitterness. Repeat at least 3 times until the water runs relatively clear. Boil the washed leaves for 15 minutes, then drain.
steps.tip: If using spinach as a substitute, skip the washing — just wilt briefly.

steps.title: Prepare the peanut paste
steps.instruction: Roast raw peanuts in a dry pan until golden. Grind in a blender with a little water to form a smooth paste.
steps.tip: Alternatively, use 200g of natural smooth peanut butter.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large pot. Sauté the onion until translucent. Add garlic and ground crayfish, cooking for 2 minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Build the stew
steps.instruction: Add the peanut paste to the pot and stir continuously for 5 minutes. Add the drained bitter leaves and stock cube. Stir to combine and cook for 20 minutes on medium-low heat.
steps.tip: Stir frequently to prevent the peanut paste from sticking to the bottom.

steps.title: Add the prawns
steps.instruction: Add the prawns to the stew and cook for 8-10 minutes until pink and cooked through. Taste and adjust seasoning.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with boiled plantains, rice, or miondo. The stew should be thick and glossy.
steps.tip: Ndolé tastes even better the next day as the flavours deepen overnight.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Cachupa Rica
recipes.description: Cape Verde's national dish — a rich, slow-cooked stew of hominy corn, beans, and multiple meats and sausages. The "rica" (rich) version is the celebratory feast edition.
recipes.story: Cachupa is to Cape Verde what the soul itself is to its people. Every island has its own version, and the dish is said to embody the melting pot of African, Portuguese, and Brazilian influences that define Cape Verdean culture. Cachupa Rica is the celebratory version with multiple meats.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried egg on top (for leftover cachupa)
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the corn and beans
steps.instruction: Drain soaked corn and beans. Place in a large pot with fresh water and boil for 45 minutes until beginning to soften.
steps.tip: The corn takes the longest — start with it.

steps.title: Add the meats
steps.instruction: Add pork ribs, beef, and sausage to the pot. Season with salt, garlic, and bay leaves. Continue simmering for 30 minutes.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potatoes, cabbage, onions, and any other vegetables. Cook for another 25-30 minutes until everything is tender.

steps.title: Adjust consistency
steps.instruction: The cachupa should be thick and hearty — more stew than soup. If too watery, cook uncovered to reduce. If too thick, add a little water.
steps.tip: Cachupa tastes even better the next day, reheated and fried.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into deep bowls. Each serving should have a generous mix of corn, beans, meats, and vegetables.

recipes.name: Gozo
recipes.description: A thick, stiff cassava flour porridge that serves as the staple base for most Central African meals. Smooth, stretchy, and perfect for scooping up stews and sauces.
recipes.story: Gozo is the daily bread of the Central African Republic. Made from cassava flour and water, this stiff porridge is shaped into balls and used to scoop up every sauce and stew in the country. Preparing gozo well requires strong arms and practice — the flour must be stirred vigorously and continuously to achieve the right consistency without lumps.
recipes.best_served_with: Any stew or sauce — especially peanut or okra-based sauces
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the water
steps.instruction: Bring 4 cups of water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add salt if using.
steps.tip: Use a heavy pot — you will be stirring vigorously.

steps.title: Add cassava flour gradually
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Begin adding cassava flour a little at a time, stirring constantly with a strong wooden spoon. Continue adding flour and stirring.
steps.tip: Add flour slowly to prevent lumps from forming.

steps.title: Stir vigorously
steps.instruction: Once all the flour is incorporated, stir vigorously for 10-12 minutes. The mixture should pull away from the sides of the pot and form a smooth, stretchy dough.
steps.tip: This requires significant arm strength — do not stop stirring or the bottom will scorch.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet your hands and shape the gozo into smooth balls. Place on a plate and serve immediately alongside your choice of stew or sauce.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in water before shaping to prevent sticking.

collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

recipes.name: Daraba
recipes.description: A thick, creamy stew of mixed greens, okra, tomatoes, and peanut butter, sometimes enriched with smoked fish. A beloved everyday dish across southern Chad.
recipes.story: Daraba is the everyday stew of southern Chad, where the Sara people have cultivated a rich agricultural tradition. The dish combines whatever leafy greens are available — spinach, sorrel, or sweet potato leaves — with okra for body and peanut butter for richness. It is simple, nourishing, and endlessly adaptable, making it the backbone of daily meals.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large pot over medium heat. Sauté the diced onion until soft and golden, about 5 minutes. Add the scotch bonnet and chopped tomatoes, cooking until the tomatoes break down.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Stir the peanut butter into the pot with 2 cups of water, mixing until smooth and well combined. Bring to a gentle simmer.
steps.tip: Dissolve the peanut butter in a little warm water first to prevent clumping.

steps.title: Add okra
steps.instruction: Add the sliced okra to the simmering sauce and cook for 10 minutes until the okra softens and thickens the stew.

steps.title: Add the greens
steps.instruction: Add the chopped greens to the pot and stir to combine. Cover and cook for 15 minutes until the greens are wilted and tender.
steps.tip: Do not overcook — the greens should be tender but still vibrant green.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste and adjust salt and pepper. Serve hot over boule or rice.
steps.tip: Daraba thickens as it cools — add a splash of water when reheating.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Moambe Chicken
recipes.description: Tender chicken simmered in a rich, earthy palm nut sauce with tomatoes, onions, and spices. Considered the national dish of the Democratic Republic of Congo.
recipes.story: Moambe chicken is the undisputed national dish of the DR Congo, rooted in the culinary heritage of the Mongo people of the Congo Basin. Palm nuts are pounded and boiled to extract the rich, orange cream that forms the heart of the sauce. Every Congolese family has their own variation, and it is the centrepiece of celebrations from weddings to Independence Day feasts.
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu, boiled plantains, or white rice
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken pieces generously with salt and pepper. Heat oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot and brown the chicken on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Browning adds deep flavour — do not skip this step.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: In the same pot, sauté onions until translucent, about 5 minutes. Add garlic and cook for 1 minute. Stir in tomato paste and cook for another minute.

steps.title: Add tomatoes and palm cream
steps.instruction: Add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down, about 5 minutes. Stir in the palm nut cream and water, mixing until smooth and combined.
steps.tip: If using canned palm cream, break up any lumps before adding.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Return the chicken to the pot and nestle pieces into the sauce. Add whole scotch bonnet peppers on top. Cover and simmer on low heat for 40 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce is thick and rich.
steps.tip: Do not puncture the scotch bonnets — they add gentle heat without making the dish fiery.

steps.title: Adjust and serve
steps.instruction: Remove the scotch bonnet peppers. Taste and adjust salt. The sauce should be thick and coat the back of a spoon. Serve hot over fufu or boiled plantains.
steps.tip: The oil from the palm cream will float to the top — this is normal and adds richness.

recipes.name: Koshari
recipes.description: Egypt's iconic street food — layers of rice, lentils, macaroni, and chickpeas topped with a spiced tomato sauce, garlicky vinegar dressing, and shatteringly crispy fried onions.
recipes.story: Koshari is the undisputed national dish of Egypt, sold from carts and dedicated shops on every street in Cairo, Alexandria, and beyond. Originally a humble dish of the working class, it has become a symbol of Egyptian identity. The magic is in the layering — each component is simple, but together with the tangy tomato sauce and crispy onions, koshari becomes greater than the sum of its parts.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra crispy onions and hot sauce
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the lentils
steps.instruction: Boil the brown lentils in salted water for about 20 minutes until tender but still holding their shape. Drain and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not overcook — the lentils should keep their shape.

steps.title: Cook rice and pasta
steps.instruction: Cook the rice according to package directions. Separately, boil the macaroni until al dente. Drain both and set aside.

steps.title: Fry the onions
steps.instruction: Thinly slice the onions and deep-fry in batches in vegetable oil until deeply golden and crispy, about 8 minutes per batch. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Fry low and slow for the crispiest onions — they will darken as they cool.

steps.title: Make the tomato sauce
steps.instruction: In a saucepan, saute a little garlic in oil, then add crushed tomatoes, vinegar, 1 tsp cumin, salt, and a pinch of chilli. Simmer for 15 minutes.

steps.title: Make the dakka
steps.instruction: In a small pan, fry the remaining garlic in 2 tbsp oil until golden. Add 3 tbsp white vinegar and 1 tsp cumin. Let it sizzle for 30 seconds.
steps.tip: The dakka should be sharp and garlicky.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Layer the rice, lentils, and macaroni in a deep bowl. Top with chickpeas, pour over the tomato sauce and dakka, then pile the crispy onions on top. Serve immediately.
steps.tip: The layers should be visible — do not mix before serving.

recipes.name: Succotash de Guinée
recipes.description: A hearty stew of black-eyed peas, corn, and vegetables cooked in a rich tomato and palm oil base. A nutritious and colourful Equatorial Guinean staple.
recipes.story: This succotash reflects the agricultural roots of Equatorial Guinea, where black-eyed peas and corn are staple crops. Unlike its American namesake, the Equatorial Guinean version is enriched with palm oil and spiced with local peppers. It is a daily staple that provides complete protein from the combination of legumes and corn.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantains, rice, or grilled fish
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the black-eyed peas
steps.instruction: Place the soaked and drained black-eyed peas in a pot with fresh water. Bring to a boil and cook for 25 minutes until tender but not mushy. Drain and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not add salt while boiling — it toughens the skins.

steps.title: Sauté the base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large pot. Sauté the onion until soft and golden. Add garlic and scotch bonnet, cooking for 1 minute. Add the chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down.

steps.title: Combine and simmer
steps.instruction: Add the cooked black-eyed peas, corn kernels, and 2 cups of water to the pot. Stir well and bring to a simmer. Cook for 15 minutes until the corn is tender and the flavours have melded.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt and pepper to taste. The stew should be thick and hearty. Serve hot with fried plantains or rice.
steps.tip: This dish tastes even better the next day after the flavours have developed.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Sishwala with Emasi
recipes.description: Eswatini's national dish — thick maize porridge paired with emasi, a tangy fermented milk similar to sour cream, creating a perfect balance of starch and tang.
recipes.story: Sishwala with emasi is the most traditional meal in Eswatini, served at everything from daily meals to royal ceremonies. Emasi is a cherished fermented milk product made by curing fresh milk in a calabash gourd for several days. In Swazi culture, cattle represent wealth and emasi is considered a food of honor, traditionally served to guests and at important ceremonies.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring the water and salt to a boil in a heavy-bottomed pot.

steps.title: Make thin porridge
steps.instruction: Mix about a quarter of the maize meal with a little cold water to form a paste. Stir this into the boiling water. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: Gradually add the remaining maize meal in batches, stirring vigorously after each addition. Use a strong wooden spoon and put your weight into it — the porridge will become very thick and stiff.
steps.tip: The sishwala should be firmer than regular porridge — thick enough to hold its shape when scooped.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to very low. Cover the pot tightly and let the sishwala steam for 10-15 minutes until fully cooked.

steps.title: Serve with emasi
steps.instruction: Scoop the sishwala onto plates, forming a mound. Pour generous amounts of emasi over the top or serve it in a bowl on the side. The cool, tangy emasi complements the warm, bland sishwala perfectly.
steps.tip: Traditionally, emasi is served at room temperature, not chilled, to complement the warm sishwala.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Umbhidvo Wetintsanga
recipes.description: Tender pumpkin leaves cooked with crushed groundnuts — a beloved Swazi vegetable dish rich in protein and vitamins, traditionally foraged from the garden.
recipes.story: Umbhidvo wetintsanga is one of the most traditional Swazi dishes, made from the tender young leaves of the pumpkin plant combined with groundnuts. In Eswatini, nothing from the pumpkin plant goes to waste — the fruit, seeds, and leaves are all used in cooking. This dish represents the resourcefulness and deep agricultural knowledge of the Swazi people.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: pumpkin leaves
ingredients.prep_note: young and tender, washed and chopped

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: Wash the pumpkin leaves thoroughly. Remove any tough stems and chop the leaves into pieces. Blanch in boiling water for 3 minutes, then drain.
steps.tip: Blanching removes the slight bitterness from the leaves and softens the fine hairs on them.

steps.title: Crush the groundnuts
steps.instruction: Place the shelled groundnuts in a mortar and pound until coarsely crushed — they should be broken but not turned into powder.
steps.tip: You want a coarse texture that adds crunch to the finished dish.

steps.title: Sauté the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Cook the onion until softened, then add the tomatoes and cook until they break down, about 5 minutes.

steps.title: Cook with groundnuts
steps.instruction: Add the blanched pumpkin leaves and crushed groundnuts to the pot. Stir well, season with salt, cover, and cook for 15 minutes until the leaves are very tender and the groundnuts have released their oils into the dish.
steps.tip: Stir occasionally to prevent sticking, adding a splash of water if it becomes too dry.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve warm alongside sishwala and stewed meat for a complete traditional Swazi meal.
steps.tip: The groundnuts should be soft but still have some texture when the dish is ready.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Doro Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopia's celebrated spicy chicken stew simmered in berbere spice and niter kibbeh, served with hard-boiled eggs on injera.
recipes.story: Doro Wat is the crown jewel of Ethiopian cuisine, reserved for holidays and special occasions. Traditionally prepared for Ethiopian Christmas (Genna) and Easter (Fasika), the dish requires patience — onions are slow-cooked for over an hour without oil until they form a rich base. Each hard-boiled egg is scored to absorb the fiery berbere sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate Chicken
steps.instruction: Rub chicken pieces with lemon juice and salt. Set aside for 30 minutes.
steps.tip: The lemon bath removes gamey flavors and tenderizes the meat.

steps.title: Dry-Cook Onions
steps.instruction: Place finely diced onions in a dry heavy pot over medium heat. Stir frequently until onions are deeply browned and reduced by half. Do not add oil or water.
steps.tip: This is the most important step — patience here builds the stew's depth of flavor.

steps.title: Build Sauce
steps.instruction: Add niter kibbeh, garlic, ginger, and berbere spice. Stir continuously for 5 minutes. Add tomato paste and water. Simmer until oil separates from sauce.

steps.title: Cook Chicken
steps.instruction: Add chicken pieces to the sauce, turning to coat. Cover and simmer on low heat until chicken is very tender and sauce is thick.

steps.title: Add Eggs
steps.instruction: Score each hard-boiled egg with a knife in a crosshatch pattern. Nestle eggs into the stew and simmer 10 more minutes so they absorb the sauce.
steps.tip: Scoring the eggs lets the berbere flavor penetrate — the deeper the cuts, the more flavorful.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle doro wat onto a large platter of injera. Place eggs on top. Serve with extra injera on the side for scooping.
steps.tip: Eat communally from a shared platter — it is called "gursha" when you hand-feed someone a morsel.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Mustard Chicken
recipes.description: Chicken marinated in a tangy mustard and citrus blend, then roasted until golden and crispy. A Gabonese dish that reflects the country's French culinary influence blended with local flavours.
recipes.story: Gabon's colonial history left a strong French culinary footprint, but Gabonese cooks never simply replicate — they adapt. This mustard chicken uses Dijon-style mustard combined with local citrus and chilli for a flavour profile that is uniquely Gabonese. It is a popular dish at gatherings where the ease of preparation belies its impressive taste.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, fried plantains, or a fresh green salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.instruction: In a large bowl, mix the Dijon mustard, lemon juice, minced garlic, oil, chilli flakes, salt, and pepper until well combined.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Coat the chicken pieces thoroughly in the mustard marinade. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes, or ideally 2 hours.
steps.tip: Overnight marination gives the best results.

steps.title: Roast the chicken
steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200C (400F). Arrange the chicken pieces skin-side up in a roasting tin. Scatter the onion rings around the chicken. Roast for 45-50 minutes until the skin is golden and crispy and the juices run clear.
steps.tip: Baste with pan juices halfway through for extra flavour and moisture.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Remove from the oven and let rest for 5 minutes. Serve with the roasted onions and pan juices spooned over the top.
steps.tip: The pan juices make an excellent sauce — do not discard them.

recipes.name: Nyembwe Chicken
recipes.description: Gabon's national dish — chicken slowly braised in a luscious palm nut sauce until the meat is falling off the bone. Rich, earthy, and deeply comforting.
recipes.story: Nyembwé refers to the thick cream extracted from palm nuts, and this dish is the crown jewel of Gabonese cooking. Among the Fang people, preparing nyembwe chicken well is a mark of culinary skill and hospitality. The sauce must achieve the perfect balance of richness without being oily, and the chicken must be tender enough to fall apart at the touch of a fork.
recipes.occasion: National celebrations, family feasts, Sunday lunch
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Season the chicken with salt, pepper, crushed garlic, and lemon juice. Let it marinate for at least 15 minutes at room temperature.
steps.tip: Longer marination — up to 2 hours in the fridge — yields deeper flavour.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Heat a little oil in a large heavy pot. Brown the chicken pieces on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the onions and tomatoes
steps.instruction: In the same pot, sauté sliced onions until softened. Add the chopped tomatoes and cook until they collapse into a paste, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Add palm nut cream
steps.instruction: Stir in the palm nut cream and water, mixing until smooth. Drop in the whole habanero pepper. Bring to a gentle simmer.
steps.tip: Do not break the habanero — it adds aroma without making the dish too spicy.

steps.title: Braise the chicken
steps.instruction: Return the chicken to the pot and nestle into the sauce. Cover and cook on low heat for 40-45 minutes until the chicken is very tender and the sauce is thick and rich.
steps.tip: Resist the urge to stir too often — let the sauce reduce naturally.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the habanero. Taste and adjust salt. Serve hot with boiled plantains or rice.
steps.tip: The sauce should coat the back of a spoon — if too thin, simmer uncovered for a few more minutes.

recipes.name: Domoda
recipes.description: Gambia's national dish — a creamy groundnut stew with beef, tomatoes, and pumpkin, served over white rice. Rich, nutty, and deeply comforting.
recipes.story: Domoda is to Gambia what jollof is to Nigeria — the dish that defines the nation's cuisine. This Mandinka groundnut stew is eaten across all ethnic groups and is the most requested dish at any Gambian gathering.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Season beef and brown in oil on all sides. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Fry onions until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add 3 cups water and the browned meat. Simmer 20 minutes.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in 1 cup warm water. Stir into the pot until smooth. Add pumpkin and scotch bonnet.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Cook on low heat for 25 minutes until pumpkin is tender and sauce is thick and creamy.
steps.tip: Stir regularly to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve generously over white rice.

recipes.name: Ghanaian Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Ghana's aromatic take on the famous rice dish, distinguished by basmati rice and a fragrant tomato sauce enriched with local spices.
recipes.story: Ghanaians stand firm that their Jollof is the world's best. The use of basmati rice gives a distinct fluffy texture, while shito on the side adds a uniquely Ghanaian dimension.
recipes.best_served_with: Shito, fried plantain, and grilled chicken
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the sauce
steps.instruction: Blend canned tomatoes, one onion, garlic, and ginger until smooth.

steps.title: Fry the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil. Fry sliced onions until golden. Add tomato paste, fry 3 minutes. Pour in blended tomato mixture.
steps.tip: Tomato paste must darken before adding the blend.

steps.title: Reduce the sauce
steps.instruction: Cook on medium heat until thickened and oil rises to surface. Season and place whole scotch bonnet on top.
steps.tip: Whole scotch bonnet adds flavour without overwhelming heat.

steps.title: Add rice and cook
steps.instruction: Add washed rice, stir to coat. Add water to sit 2cm above rice. Cover tightly with foil and lid. Cook on low 30-35 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not lift the lid during the first 25 minutes.

steps.title: Fluff and serve
steps.instruction: Fluff with a fork. Remove scotch bonnet. Serve with shito, fried plantain, and protein of choice.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Guinean Chicken Yassa
recipes.description: Guinea's version of the classic yassa, enriched with extra ginger and hot peppers, giving it a uniquely Guinean kick compared to the Senegalese original.
recipes.story: While yassa travelled across West Africa from the Casamance, Guinea's version has evolved its own identity. Guinean cooks add more ginger and their own varieties of hot pepper, making it distinctly spicier and more aromatic.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate
steps.instruction: Combine chicken with lime juice, half the onions, grated ginger, salt, and pepper. Refrigerate 2-4 hours.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Remove chicken from marinade. Heat oil and brown the chicken pieces on all sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the onions
steps.instruction: In the same pot, add all onions (fresh and from marinade). Cook on medium heat until very soft and golden, about 20 minutes.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Return chicken to pot. Add reserved marinade and whole piment peppers. Cover and simmer 25 minutes until chicken is tender.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve chicken and onion sauce over steamed white rice.

recipes.name: Caldo de Mancarra
recipes.description: Guinea-Bissau's groundnut soup — a thick peanut broth with chicken and vegetables, reflecting both Portuguese and West African culinary traditions.
recipes.story: Caldo de Mancarra blends Guinea-Bissau's African heritage with Portuguese colonial influences. "Mancarra" is the local Creole word for peanut, and this soup is the nation's most beloved comfort dish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken and boil with onions until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve stock.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: In the same pot, fry remaining onions. Add tomatoes and cook until softened.

steps.title: Add peanut paste
steps.instruction: Dissolve ground peanuts in 2 cups chicken stock. Add to the pot along with sweet potatoes and hot pepper.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add chicken back. Simmer 20 minutes until sweet potatoes are tender and soup is thick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot over white rice.

recipes.name: Attiéké with Grilled Fish
recipes.description: Fermented cassava couscous served alongside perfectly grilled fish, tomatoes, and onions. Ivory Coast's beloved street food lunch.
recipes.story: Attiéké is the pride of the Ébrié lagoon people of southern Ivory Coast. The fermented cassava couscous has a tangy flavour and fluffy texture unlike anything else in West African cuisine. It has become the national lunch dish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Rub fish inside and out with salt, pepper, garlic, and a squeeze of lime. Let marinate 15 minutes.

steps.title: Grill the fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish over charcoal or on a hot grill pan, turning once, until skin is crispy and flesh is cooked through, about 12 minutes per side.
steps.tip: Score the fish deeply so heat penetrates evenly.

steps.title: Steam the attiéké
steps.instruction: Steam the attiéké in a steamer basket or microwave for 5 minutes until fluffy and warm. Fluff with a fork and drizzle with a little oil.

steps.title: Prepare the garnish
steps.instruction: Slice tomatoes and onions into rings. Make a simple piment sauce by blending scotch bonnets with a little oil and salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place attiéké on a plate, lay the grilled fish alongside, and garnish with tomato and onion slices. Serve piment sauce on the side.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Kedjenou
recipes.description: A slow-cooked chicken stew made without any added water — the chicken and vegetables steam in their own juices with aromatic herbs and spices.
recipes.story: Kedjenou is the signature dish of the Baoulé people. Traditionally cooked in a sealed canari (clay pot) over low heat, the chicken releases its own juices creating an intensely flavoured broth without adding any water.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Layer the pot
steps.instruction: Place chicken in a heavy pot or Dutch oven. Add tomatoes, onions, eggplant, garlic, ginger, bay leaves, and whole scotch bonnet. Season with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Do not add any water — this is the key to kedjenou.

steps.title: Seal and cook
steps.instruction: Cover the pot tightly with foil and then the lid to create a perfect seal. Cook on the lowest heat possible for 45 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not open the lid during cooking — shake the pot occasionally instead.

steps.title: Check doneness
steps.instruction: After 45 minutes, carefully open the lid. The chicken should be falling off the bone in a rich, concentrated broth of its own juices.

steps.title: Simmer if needed
steps.instruction: If the chicken needs more time, reseal and cook another 15 minutes. The sauce should be thick and deeply flavoured.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with attiéké or fluffy white rice. The concentrated sauce is incredibly flavourful.

recipes.name: Ugali na Sukuma Wiki
recipes.description: Kenya's beloved staple of firm maize porridge served with sauteed collard greens, onions, and tomatoes.
recipes.story: Ugali na Sukuma Wiki is the heartbeat of Kenyan cuisine, eaten daily across all social classes. "Sukuma wiki" literally translates to "push the week," reflecting its role as an affordable green that stretches meals. No Kenyan table is complete without this humble yet satisfying combination.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Water
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add salt.
steps.tip: Use a heavy wooden spoon (mwiko) for stirring ugali — it makes a big difference.

steps.title: Make Ugali
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Gradually add maize flour while stirring vigorously to avoid lumps. Keep stirring until the mixture pulls away from the sides and forms a firm dough.
steps.tip: Press against the pot sides to test — it should be firm but not dry.

steps.title: Shape Ugali
steps.instruction: Wet a plate and turn the ugali onto it. Shape into a dome using a wet spoon. Cover to keep warm.

steps.title: Saute Aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Saute onions until golden, then add garlic and tomatoes. Cook until tomatoes break down.

steps.title: Cook Greens
steps.instruction: Add the sliced sukuma wiki and chili. Toss to coat with the tomato mixture. Cover and cook until wilted but still vibrant green.
steps.tip: Do not overcook — sukuma wiki should retain a slight bite and bright color.

steps.title: Season and Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt and squeeze lemon juice over the greens. Serve alongside the ugali dome.
steps.tip: Traditionally eaten with hands — pinch ugali with three fingers, make an indent, and scoop.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Papa le Moroho
recipes.description: Lesotho's everyday staple — thick maize meal porridge served with braised wild greens cooked with tomatoes and onions, a nourishing highland meal.
recipes.story: Papa le moroho is the most fundamental meal in Lesotho, eaten daily in homes across the mountain kingdom. Papa (maize porridge) is the starchy base of nearly every Basotho meal, while moroho (wild greens) provides essential vitamins and minerals. In a country where much of the terrain is mountainous, these hardy crops and foraged greens have sustained the Basotho people for generations.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the papa
steps.instruction: Bring the water to a boil in a heavy pot. Mix a quarter of the maize meal with cold water to make a thin paste. Stir this into the boiling water and cook for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken the papa
steps.instruction: Gradually add the remaining maize meal, stirring vigorously after each addition with a wooden spoon. Continue until the papa is very thick and pulls away from the sides of the pot. Cover and steam on low heat for 10 minutes.
steps.tip: The key to good papa is vigorous stirring — it prevents lumps and creates a smooth texture.

steps.title: Cook the moroho
steps.instruction: While the papa steams, heat oil in a separate pan. Sauté the onion until softened. Add the tomatoes and cook until they form a sauce.

steps.title: Add greens
steps.instruction: Add the chopped greens to the pan. Season with salt. Cover and cook for 8-10 minutes until the greens are tender and have absorbed the tomato flavor.
steps.tip: Wild spinach takes longer to cook than regular spinach — adjust your timing accordingly.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound the papa on a serving plate. Spoon the moroho alongside. Eat by pinching off a piece of papa and using it to scoop up the greens.
steps.tip: In Basotho culture, meals are shared from a communal plate as a sign of unity.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Palm Butter Soup
recipes.description: Liberia's national dish — a rich, thick soup made from palm fruit extract simmered with meat, fish, and crab. The deep orange colour and velvety texture are unmistakable.
recipes.story: Palm Butter Soup is the pride of Liberian cuisine. Made from the extract of boiled palm fruits, it is rich in vitamins and has a unique, silky texture. Every Liberian family has their recipe passed down through generations.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the meat
steps.instruction: Season meat with onions, salt, and pepper. Boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve stock.

steps.title: Prepare palm butter base
steps.instruction: In a large pot, combine palm fruit concentrate with 2 cups of meat stock. Bring to a boil, stirring well.

steps.title: Add proteins
steps.instruction: Add the cooked meat, smoked fish, and crab to the palm butter. Add scotch bonnets and okra if using.

steps.title: Simmer the soup
steps.instruction: Simmer on low heat for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the soup thickens and the oil separates on top.
steps.tip: The soup is ready when the palm oil pools on the surface.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot over white rice or with fufu.

recipes.name: Torborgee
recipes.description: A fiery Liberian stew of fermented locust beans, dried shrimp, and intense chilli peppers. One of the hottest dishes in West African cuisine.
recipes.story: Torborgee is the dish that separates the brave from the timid. Made with dawadawa and an abundance of hot peppers, it is an acquired taste that Liberians adore. It is said that once you develop a taste for torborgee, no other stew will satisfy.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Fry onions until softened. Add dawadawa and stir for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add peppers
steps.instruction: Add the blended habaneros and cook for 5 minutes, stirring frequently.
steps.tip: This will be very spicy — adjust peppers to your tolerance.

steps.title: Add seafood
steps.instruction: Add dried shrimp and soaked dried fish. Pour in 2 cups water. Bring to a simmer.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Cook on low heat for 25 minutes until the stew is thick and intensely flavoured.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over white rice. A small amount goes a long way due to the intensity.

recipes.name: Bazin
recipes.description: Libya's national dish — a smooth, dense dome of barley dough surrounded by a rich lamb and tomato stew, topped with hard-boiled eggs. Eaten communally by hand.
recipes.story: Bazin is the centrepiece of Libyan cuisine, a dish that defines national identity. The barley dough is cooked and pounded until smooth, then shaped into a dome and placed in the centre of a large round tray, surrounded by a slow-cooked lamb stew. Families gather around the tray and eat with their right hand, tearing off pieces of bazin and dipping them in the sauce. It is served at every major celebration and Friday lunch.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the stew
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a large pot. Brown the lamb on all sides. Add onions and cook until golden. Stir in tomato paste, turmeric, salt, pepper, and 1 tsp cayenne. Add enough water to cover the meat generously.

steps.title: Simmer until tender
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for about 60 minutes. Add potatoes in the last 20 minutes. The lamb should be falling-off-the-bone tender and the sauce thick.
steps.tip: The sauce needs to be rich and reduced — it is the dipping sauce for the bazin.

steps.title: Make the bazin dough
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of salted water to a boil. Gradually add the barley flour while stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon. Keep stirring and cooking over low heat for 15 minutes until you have a very thick, smooth dough that pulls away from the pot.
steps.tip: Stir constantly and use muscle — the dough should be very thick and smooth.

steps.title: Shape the dome
steps.instruction: Wet your hands and shape the barley dough into a smooth dome. Place it in the centre of a large round serving tray.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in cold water to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Arrange the lamb and potatoes around the bazin dome. Pour the stew sauce over and around it. Place the hard-boiled egg halves on top. Serve communally and eat by hand.
steps.tip: Tear small pieces of bazin and dip into the sauce — the combination is extraordinary.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Tigadegena
recipes.description: Mali's celebrated peanut sauce stew with tender meat, rich and creamy from slow-cooked onions and tomatoes blended with groundnut paste.
recipes.story: Tigadegena means "peanut butter sauce" in Bambara and is considered Mali's national dish. It reflects the central role of groundnuts in Sahelian cuisine.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the lamb
steps.instruction: Season lamb with salt. Heat oil and brown on all sides. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Fry the aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and fry until softened. Stir in tomato paste and cook 3 minutes.

steps.title: Create peanut sauce
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut paste in 3 cups warm water. Pour into pot with browned lamb. Simmer.

steps.title: Cook the stew
steps.instruction: Add potatoes and eggplant. Cover and simmer 40 minutes until meat is fork-tender and sauce thickened.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over white rice or alongside toh.

recipes.name: Mauritanian Fish Rice
recipes.description: Mauritania's version of the classic fish and rice dish, featuring Atlantic fish from the country's rich coastal waters, cooked in a tomato and vegetable sauce.
recipes.story: Mauritania's Atlantic coast provides some of the richest fishing grounds in West Africa. Their thieboudienne uses the freshest ocean fish, and the dish is shared communally on large platters among family and guests.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the fish
steps.instruction: Season fish with garlic, parsley, and lemon. Let marinate 15 minutes. Fry until browned. Set aside.

steps.title: Cook the sauce
steps.instruction: Fry onions. Add tomato paste and cook 5 minutes. Add 6 cups water.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots and cabbage. Simmer 15 minutes until tender. Remove vegetables and fish.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Add washed rice to the sauce. Cover tightly and cook on low 25 minutes.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Mound rice on a platter. Arrange fish and vegetables on top. Serve communally.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Chicken Tagine with Preserved Lemons & Olives
recipes.description: A fragrant Moroccan slow-cooked chicken stew with golden preserved lemons and briny olives, perfumed with saffron, ginger, and fresh cilantro.
recipes.story: The tagine is both a cooking vessel and the dish itself, central to Moroccan culinary identity for centuries. This particular combination of preserved lemons and olives is considered the quintessential Moroccan tagine, served at family lunches across the kingdom from Fez to Marrakech. The conical lid traps steam and returns it to the dish, creating tender meat in a concentrated sauce.
recipes.best_served_with: Fluffy couscous or crusty Moroccan bread (khobz)
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub the chicken pieces with garlic, ginger, saffron water, salt, and pepper. Let marinate for at least 30 minutes or overnight in the fridge.
steps.tip: Overnight marinating gives the deepest flavour.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Place the grated onions in the bottom of a tagine or heavy-bottomed pot. Arrange the chicken on top and pour over any remaining marinade. Add 1 cup of water.

steps.title: Slow cook
steps.instruction: Cover and cook over medium-low heat for about 60 minutes, turning the chicken once halfway through, until the meat is very tender and the sauce has reduced.
steps.tip: Keep the heat low to prevent burning — the onions should melt into the sauce.

steps.title: Add lemons and olives
steps.instruction: Add the preserved lemon rinds and olives to the tagine. Cook uncovered for another 15 minutes to let the sauce thicken and the flavours meld.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.instruction: Arrange the chicken on a serving platter, spoon the sauce over the top, and garnish generously with fresh cilantro. Serve with couscous or bread.
steps.tip: Present in the tagine pot for an authentic table centrepiece.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Potjiekos
recipes.description: A traditional Namibian layered stew cooked slowly in a cast-iron three-legged pot over coals — layers of meat, vegetables, and starches meld together without stirring.
recipes.story: Potjiekos, meaning "small pot food," is a centuries-old tradition brought to Southern Africa by Dutch settlers and embraced across Namibia. The dish is always cooked outdoors over a low fire in a round, cast-iron pot. The golden rule of potjiekos is never to stir — the ingredients are layered so that each element steams and braises in its own juices, creating complex flavors.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in the potjie pot over medium-hot coals. Brown the beef cubes in batches until well-seared on all sides. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Good browning creates the flavor base for the entire dish.

steps.title: Layer the onions
steps.instruction: Spread the onion rings over the bottom of the pot. Place the browned meat on top of the onions. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Layer the vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the carrots in an even layer over the meat. Follow with the green beans, then the potatoes on top. Season each layer lightly with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: The order matters — root vegetables near the bottom, delicate ones on top.

steps.title: Add liquid and seal
steps.instruction: Pour the beef stock and red wine gently down the side of the pot so as not to disturb the layers. Place the lid on tightly.
steps.tip: The lid should create a good seal — some cooks place foil under the lid for extra sealing.

steps.title: Slow cook
steps.instruction: Reduce the coals to maintain a very gentle simmer. Cook without lifting the lid for 2 to 2.5 hours. Resist the urge to stir — the layers must cook undisturbed.
steps.tip: If using a stovetop, keep the heat at the lowest possible setting.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the lid, check that the meat is tender, and gently ladle out portions, keeping the layers visible. Serve with rice or fresh bread.
steps.tip: The bottom layer will have the richest, most concentrated flavors.

recipes.name: Dambou
recipes.description: Steamed moringa leaf couscous — tiny granules of millet flour mixed with dried moringa powder, steamed until fluffy and served with a spicy sauce.
recipes.story: Dambou is a nutritional powerhouse from Niger, combining protein-rich moringa leaves with millet flour. Moringa, called "the miracle tree," grows abundantly in the Sahel and is central to Nigerien cuisine and health.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Mix the dambou
steps.instruction: In a large bowl, combine millet flour and moringa powder. Sprinkle water gradually while mixing with your fingers to form tiny couscous-like granules.
steps.tip: The granules should be small and uniform — not clumpy.

steps.title: First steaming
steps.instruction: Place the granules in a steamer basket lined with cheesecloth. Steam over boiling water for 15 minutes.

steps.title: Break and re-steam
steps.instruction: Remove and break up any clumps with a fork. Drizzle with a little oil and toss. Return to the steamer for another 10 minutes.
steps.tip: Breaking up clumps between steamings gives a fluffy result.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.instruction: Fluff the finished dambou with a fork. Season with salt and a drizzle of groundnut oil. Serve with a spicy tomato-onion sauce.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Nigeria's iconic one-pot rice dish cooked in a smoky tomato and pepper sauce. The rice absorbs layers of flavour from blended tomatoes, scotch bonnets, and a signature party smokiness.
recipes.story: Jollof Rice is the undisputed king of Nigerian party food. No owambe (Yoruba party) is complete without it. The debate over whether Nigerian or Ghanaian Jollof is superior has become one of West Africa's most passionate cultural rivalries.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantain, coleslaw, and grilled chicken
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Blend the base
steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes, red bell peppers, scotch bonnets, and one onion until smooth. Pour into a pot and cook on medium-high heat until reduced by half.
steps.tip: Frying down the tomato base is the most important step — it must lose all excess water.

steps.title: Fry the base
steps.instruction: Heat vegetable oil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Fry sliced onions until golden. Add tomato paste and fry for 2 minutes. Pour in the reduced tomato blend.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Add curry powder, thyme, bay leaves, seasoning cubes, and salt. Let the sauce fry until oil floats on top, about 10 minutes.
steps.tip: When the oil separates and sits on top, your base is ready for rice.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Pour in the washed rice and stir to coat every grain. Add stock or water to sit about 1cm above the rice. Cover tightly with foil then the lid.
steps.tip: The foil seal traps steam and helps the rice cook evenly.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Cook on low heat for 30 minutes without opening the lid. Stir gently from the bottom, re-cover and cook for another 10-15 minutes until tender.

steps.title: Create the party smokiness
steps.instruction: Increase heat to high for the last 2-3 minutes to create a slight burn at the bottom — this is the prized party jollof smoky flavour.
steps.tip: The bottom crust is the most coveted serving at any party.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Poulet à la Muambé
recipes.description: Chicken braised in a velvety palm butter sauce with garlic, okra, and chilli. The Congolese version features a lighter, more herbaceous sauce compared to its Kinshasa cousin.
recipes.story: In Brazzaville, muambé is more than a dish — it is an expression of Kongo identity. Palm fruit is harvested, boiled, and pressed to extract the rich cream that defines this stew. While similar to the DR Congo version, the Brazzaville style often includes okra for body and a touch more chilli. It is served at every important occasion from births to funerals.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper. Heat a large pot with a little oil and brown chicken pieces on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Sauté the aromatics
steps.instruction: In the same pot, cook the onion until soft. Add garlic and chilli flakes, stirring for 1 minute. Pour in the blended tomatoes and cook until reduced by half.

steps.title: Add palm butter
steps.instruction: Stir the palm butter into the tomato base until fully incorporated. Add bay leaves and 1 cup of water. Bring to a gentle simmer.
steps.tip: Palm butter can be thick — stir vigorously to avoid lumps.

steps.title: Braise the chicken
steps.instruction: Return the chicken pieces to the pot. Cover and cook on low heat for 35 minutes, turning the chicken halfway through.

steps.title: Add okra and finish
steps.instruction: Add the sliced okra and cook for another 10 minutes until tender. Remove bay leaves, taste for salt, and serve hot with chikwangue or rice.
steps.tip: Okra adds natural thickness to the sauce.

recipes.name: Calulu
recipes.description: Sao Tome's national dish — smoked and dried fish stewed with okra, tomatoes, and palm oil, layered with eggplant and served over mashed breadfruit or banana.
recipes.story: Calulu is the soul of Santomean cuisine, a dish that reflects the island's Portuguese colonial past and African roots. It is prepared for every important occasion, from family Sunday lunches to national independence celebrations.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the fish
steps.instruction: Soak smoked and dried fish separately in warm water for 30 minutes. Debone and break into large chunks.

steps.title: Layer the stew
steps.instruction: In a heavy pot, layer palm oil, onions, tomatoes, eggplant, okra, and both types of fish. Season each layer with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Layering builds complex flavours — do not stir at this stage.

steps.title: Slow cook
steps.instruction: Cover tightly. Cook on the lowest heat for 45 minutes, shaking the pot occasionally but not stirring.
steps.tip: The stew cooks in its own juices — do not add water.

steps.title: Check and stir
steps.instruction: After 45 minutes, gently stir from the bottom. The okra should have thickened the sauce. Simmer 10 more minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over mashed breadfruit or plantain (angú).

recipes.name: Sao Tomean Hot Chocolate
recipes.description: A thick, rich hot chocolate made from locally grown cacao beans. Sao Tome produces some of the world's finest cacao, and this drink showcases it beautifully.
recipes.story: Sao Tome and Principe was once the world's largest cacao producer. While production has decreased, the quality of Santomean cacao remains exceptional. This hot chocolate uses raw cacao for an authentic, intense flavour.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Heat the milk
steps.instruction: Pour milk into a saucepan with the cinnamon stick. Heat on medium until steaming but not boiling.

steps.title: Melt the chocolate
steps.instruction: Add chopped chocolate or cacao mass to the hot milk. Stir constantly until fully melted and smooth.
steps.tip: Use high-quality chocolate for the best result.

steps.title: Sweeten
steps.instruction: Add sugar and vanilla. Stir until dissolved. Continue heating for 5 minutes, whisking to create a frothy texture.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove cinnamon stick. Pour into cups and serve immediately while hot and frothy.
steps.tip: For extra richness, add a splash of coconut milk.

recipes.name: Thieboudienne
recipes.description: Senegal's national dish — rice cooked in a rich tomato sauce with stuffed fish and vegetables including cassava, eggplant, and cabbage.
recipes.story: Thieboudienne was created by Penda Mbaye of Saint-Louis and was inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2021. Families gather around a large communal platter.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Stuff the fish
steps.instruction: Blend parsley, garlic, scotch bonnet, and lime juice to make the rof. Fill the scored slits with this paste.
steps.tip: Pack the rof tightly into the fish cuts.

steps.title: Fry the fish
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Fry stuffed fish on both sides until browned. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Fry onions until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 5 minutes. Add water, tamarind, and dried fish for depth.

steps.title: Cook the vegetables
steps.instruction: Add cassava, eggplant, and cabbage. Simmer until tender, about 20 minutes. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Add washed rice to remaining sauce with enough water. Cover tightly and cook on low 25-30 minutes.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Mound rice on a large platter. Place fish in centre, surround with vegetables. Serve with lime wedges.

recipes.name: Cassava Leaf Stew
recipes.description: Sierra Leone's national dish — finely pounded cassava leaves simmered with palm oil, fish, and meat until thick and rich. A deeply savoury, earthy stew.
recipes.story: Cassava Leaf Stew is the soul of Sierra Leonean cooking. The laborious process of pounding the tough leaves until smooth is a communal activity, often done by women singing together. The dish is inseparable from Sierra Leonean identity.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: If using fresh leaves, pound or blend them until very fine. Boil in water for 20 minutes to soften and remove bitterness. Drain well.
steps.tip: Frozen pre-pounded cassava leaves save significant time.

steps.title: Cook the meat
steps.instruction: Season meat with onions, salt, and pepper. Boil until tender, about 20 minutes. Reserve the stock.

steps.title: Build the stew
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large pot. Add remaining onions and fry until soft. Add the drained cassava leaves, smoked fish, cooked meat, and ground peanuts.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add 2 cups of meat stock and scotch bonnets. Simmer on low heat for 30 minutes, stirring frequently, until thick and the oil rises to the surface.
steps.tip: The stew is ready when the palm oil pools on top.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve generous portions over fluffy white rice.

recipes.name: Bobotie
recipes.description: South Africa's beloved national dish — spiced curried mince baked under a creamy egg custard topping, with a sweet-savory flavor from raisins, chutney, and turmeric.
recipes.story: Bobotie traces its roots to the Cape Malay community of Cape Town, descendants of Southeast Asian workers brought to the Cape in the 17th century. The dish blends Indonesian-inspired spices with African and Dutch culinary traditions, creating something uniquely South African. It has been the country's national dish since 1999 and remains a staple at family gatherings across the rainbow nation.
recipes.best_served_with: Yellow rice with raisins and a green salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Soak the bread
steps.instruction: Tear the bread slices into pieces and soak in 100ml milk for 10 minutes. Squeeze out excess milk and set aside, reserving the milk.

steps.title: Cook the meat filling
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pan over medium heat. Fry the onions until golden, about 5 minutes. Add curry powder and turmeric, stirring for 1 minute. Add the mince and cook until browned, breaking it up with a wooden spoon.

steps.title: Add flavor
steps.instruction: Stir in the soaked bread, chutney, sultanas, salt, and pepper. Mix well and cook for another 3 minutes until everything is combined. Remove from heat.
steps.tip: The soaked bread helps bind the mixture and keeps it moist during baking.

steps.title: Assemble the dish
steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180°C. Spoon the meat mixture into a greased oven-safe dish and press down evenly. Tuck bay leaves into the top of the meat.

steps.title: Make the custard
steps.instruction: Whisk together the eggs and remaining milk with a pinch of turmeric. Pour evenly over the meat filling.
steps.tip: Adding a pinch of turmeric to the custard gives it a beautiful golden color.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Bake in the preheated oven for 35-40 minutes until the custard is set and golden brown on top.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Let the bobotie rest for 5 minutes, then serve with yellow rice, sambals, and a side salad.
steps.tip: Traditional accompaniments include banana slices, coconut flakes, and chutney as sambals.

recipes.name: Bunny Chow
recipes.description: A Durban original — a hollowed-out loaf of white bread filled with fragrant, slow-cooked lamb or chicken curry, eaten with your hands.
recipes.story: Bunny Chow originated in the Indian community of Durban in the 1940s. During apartheid, when non-white South Africans were barred from eating in restaurants, Indian merchants at the Grey Street area began serving curry inside hollowed-out bread loaves as a portable meal. The name's origin is debated, but the dish has become one of Durban's most iconic culinary exports.
recipes.best_served_with: Pickled vegetables and grated carrot salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over high heat. Season lamb with salt and brown in batches until deep golden on all sides. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the pot — browning in batches ensures a good sear.

steps.title: Cook the curry base
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add onions and cook until golden brown, about 8 minutes. Add curry powder, garam masala, and curry leaves, stirring for 2 minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer the curry
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes and return the lamb to the pot. Add enough water to just cover the meat. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for 40 minutes.

steps.title: Add potatoes
steps.instruction: Add the potato quarters to the curry. Continue simmering for another 20 minutes until the potatoes are tender and the sauce has thickened.
steps.tip: The potatoes should be soft enough to break apart slightly, thickening the curry.

steps.title: Prepare the bread
steps.instruction: Cut the bread loaf in half. Hollow out each half, leaving about 2cm of bread on the sides and bottom. Keep the scooped-out bread for dipping.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the hot curry into the hollowed-out bread. Place the bread lid on top. Serve immediately, eating with your hands — tear pieces of bread and scoop up the curry.
steps.tip: Authentic bunny chow is always eaten with your hands, never cutlery.

recipes.name: Ful Medames
recipes.description: Sudan's beloved slow-cooked fava beans mashed with sesame oil, lemon, and cumin — the undisputed national breakfast dish.
recipes.story: Ful is the beating heart of Sudanese mornings. From Khartoum street stalls to village homes along the Nile, the day begins with a plate of creamy fava beans drizzled with sesame oil and fresh lemon. Vendors slow-cook the beans overnight in tall, narrow pots called damasa, creating a uniquely smooth texture. Ful is more than food — it is a social ritual, eaten communally with bread torn and shared among friends and neighbours.
recipes.best_served_with: Warm flatbread (kisra or aish baladi), hard-boiled eggs, fresh salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Drain soaked fava beans, place in a large pot, and cover with fresh water by 5cm. Bring to a boil, reduce to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook for about 2 hours until very soft and breaking apart.
steps.tip: The longer and slower you cook, the creamier the result. Some Sudanese cooks simmer overnight on the lowest heat.

steps.title: Mash and season
steps.instruction: Drain most of the water, reserving some. Mash the beans roughly with a fork or potato masher — leave some texture. Stir in the cumin, salt, and half the lemon juice.
steps.tip: Sudanese ful is mashed, not pureed — you want chunky bits.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.instruction: Spoon into shallow bowls. Drizzle generously with sesame oil and remaining lemon juice. Top with diced onion, minced garlic, and sliced chilli. Serve immediately with warm bread.
steps.tip: In Sudan, the sesame oil is key — it gives ful its distinctive nutty flavour that sets it apart from Egyptian versions.

recipes.name: Koklo Meme
recipes.description: Togo's beloved grilled chicken marinated in a paste of ginger, garlic, chilli, and lime. Charred over wood charcoal for an unmistakable smoky flavour.
recipes.story: Koklo Meme is the king of Togolese street food. The name means "grilled chicken" in Ewe, and the charcoal-grilled birds with their spicy marinade are a fixture of every market and roadside stall in Lomé.
recipes.best_served_with: Piment sauce, yam fries, or rice
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.instruction: Blend ginger, garlic, scotch bonnets, lime juice, oil, salt, and pepper into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub marinade all over the chicken, under the skin and into every crevice. Refrigerate at least 2 hours.
steps.tip: Overnight gives the most flavour.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.instruction: Grill over medium charcoal heat, turning regularly, for 30-35 minutes until skin is crispy and charred and juices run clear.
steps.tip: Baste with remaining marinade while grilling.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest for 5 minutes, then cut into pieces. Serve with piment sauce and sides.

recipes.name: Lablabi
recipes.description: Tunisia's beloved chickpea soup poured over torn stale bread, loaded with harissa, cumin, garlic, and a drizzle of olive oil. A fiery, nourishing breakfast staple.
recipes.story: Lablabi is the ultimate Tunisian street food, served from early morning in small hole-in-the-wall shops across Tunis, Sousse, and Sfax. Workers and students line up for a warm bowl before starting their day. Each diner customises their bowl with as much harissa, cumin, capers, and olive oil as they like. It is simple, frugal cooking at its most satisfying — transforming humble chickpeas and stale bread into something extraordinary.
recipes.best_served_with: Crusty bread, pickled turnips, and olives
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chickpeas
steps.instruction: Drain the soaked chickpeas and place in a large pot. Cover with fresh water, add 1 tsp baking soda, and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer for 40 minutes until very tender.
steps.tip: The baking soda helps the chickpeas get extra soft and creamy.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Stir in the crushed garlic, harissa, cumin, salt, and pepper into the chickpea broth. Simmer for another 5 minutes to let the flavours infuse.
steps.tip: Adjust the harissa to your heat preference — Tunisians like it fiery.

steps.title: Prepare the bowls
steps.instruction: Place a generous handful of torn bread pieces in each serving bowl.
steps.tip: Day-old or stale bread works best as it absorbs the broth without disintegrating.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the hot chickpea broth and chickpeas over the bread. Drizzle generously with olive oil, squeeze lemon juice over the top, and scatter capers if using. Serve immediately.
steps.tip: Some like to crack a raw egg into the hot broth — the heat gently cooks it.
