recipes.name: Sahrawi Camel Meat Stew
recipes.description: A hearty nomadic stew of slow-cooked camel meat with onions, tomatoes, and warming spices. The signature protein of Sahrawi desert cuisine.
recipes.story: Camel meat is central to the diet and culture of the Sahrawi people, the nomadic inhabitants of Western Sahara. In the harsh desert environment, camels provide sustenance, transport, and livelihood. This stew represents the resourcefulness of Sahrawi cooking — simple ingredients transformed through slow cooking over low desert fire into a tender, deeply flavoured meal served during gatherings and celebrations.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over medium-high heat. Season the camel meat with salt, cumin, and coriander. Brown on all sides in batches, about 8 minutes total. Set aside.
steps.tip: Camel meat is lean — do not overcrowd the pot or it will steam instead of searing.

steps.title: Cook the onions
steps.instruction: In the same pot, add the sliced onions and cook over medium heat until deeply caramelised, about 12 minutes.
steps.tip: The caramelised onions provide the sweetness that balances the dish.

steps.title: Build the stew
steps.instruction: Return the meat to the pot. Add the chopped tomatoes, dried chilli, and enough water to just cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very low simmer. Cover and cook for 90 minutes.
steps.tip: Low and slow is essential — camel meat becomes wonderfully tender with patience.

steps.title: Add potatoes
steps.instruction: Add the potatoes and continue cooking for another 25 minutes until the potatoes are tender and the sauce has thickened.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve in a communal bowl with flatbread for scooping, alongside strong, sweet Sahrawi mint tea.
steps.tip: In Sahrawi tradition, this is eaten communally from a shared plate.

recipes.name: Sahrawi Mint Tea
recipes.description: A frothy, intensely sweet green tea with fresh mint, poured from height to create a signature foam. Prepared in three rounds, each with its own character and proverb.
recipes.story: Tea is the heartbeat of Sahrawi social life. The tea ceremony, called atay, is a sacred ritual of hospitality — refusing tea is considered deeply impolite. It is always served in three rounds: the first is strong and bitter like life, the second is balanced and sweet like love, and the third is gentle and mild like death. The tea master pours from great height to create the coveted froth, a sign of skill and respect for guests.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Rinse the tea
steps.instruction: Place the green tea in a small teapot. Add a splash of boiling water, swirl, and discard. This removes the bitterness and dust from the leaves.
steps.tip: This rinse is essential — never skip it.

steps.title: First brew (strong)
steps.instruction: Add the boiling water and a third of the sugar to the teapot. Let it steep for 5 minutes. Add some mint leaves and pour from a height into small glasses, then pour back into the pot. Repeat the pouring 3-4 times to create foam.
steps.tip: The higher you pour, the better the foam. This takes practice.

steps.title: Serve first round
steps.instruction: Pour the first round into the glasses from height, creating a frothy top. This round is the strongest and most bitter. Serve and drink.
steps.tip: The first glass is said to be bitter like life.

steps.title: Second and third rounds
steps.instruction: Add more water, sugar, and fresh mint for the second round. Steep and pour the same way. Repeat for the third round with even more sugar and mint. Each round gets sweeter and milder.
steps.tip: Always accept all three rounds — it is a sign of respect and friendship.

steps.title: Present with care
steps.instruction: Serve each glass on a small tray. The tea master always tastes first to ensure quality before serving guests.
steps.tip: In Sahrawi culture, the youngest person present usually prepares the tea as a sign of respect to elders.

recipes.name: Dovi
recipes.description: A hearty Zimbabwean peanut butter stew with chicken and vegetables, rich and creamy with a deep nutty flavor — true comfort food from the heart of Zimbabwe.
recipes.story: Dovi, meaning peanut butter in Shona, is one of Zimbabwe's most cherished dishes. Peanuts have been cultivated in the region for centuries and peanut butter stew appears in various forms across the country. The dish is hearty, affordable, and incredibly satisfying — the peanut butter melts into the broth to create a thick, creamy sauce that coats every piece of chicken and vegetable.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot over medium-high heat. Season chicken pieces with salt and brown on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook onions and tomatoes
steps.instruction: In the same pot, fry the onions until softened. Add the tomatoes and cook until they break down into a thick sauce, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Dissolve the peanut butter
steps.instruction: Mix the peanut butter with the water until smooth, ensuring there are no lumps. Pour this mixture into the pot with the tomato sauce and stir well.
steps.tip: Mixing the peanut butter with water separately first prevents clumping in the stew.

steps.title: Simmer with chicken
steps.instruction: Return the chicken to the pot. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce heat to low. Cover and simmer for 35-40 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce is thick and creamy.
steps.tip: Stir occasionally to prevent the peanut butter from sticking to the bottom.

steps.title: Add greens
steps.instruction: If using, add the pumpkin leaves or spinach in the last 10 minutes of cooking. Stir them into the stew and cook until wilted.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning with salt. Serve hot with sadza, scooping up the thick peanut gravy with each bite.
steps.tip: The stew thickens as it cools — add a splash of water when reheating leftovers.

recipes.name: Sadza neNyama
recipes.description: Zimbabwe's cornerstone meal — thick maize porridge served with slow-cooked beef stew in a rich tomato and onion gravy, eaten with the hands.
recipes.story: No Zimbabwean meal is complete without sadza. This thick maize porridge is the foundation of nearly every meal, and the phrase "I haven't eaten sadza today" is essentially saying you haven't eaten at all. Paired with nyama (meat stew), it represents the quintessential Zimbabwean dining experience — always shared, always eaten by hand.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the beef
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over high heat. Brown the beef cubes in batches until well-seared. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the stew base
steps.instruction: In the same pot, fry the onions until golden. Add the tomatoes and cook until they form a thick sauce, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Return the beef to the pot. Add water and salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer covered for 45 minutes until the beef is very tender and the gravy has thickened.
steps.tip: The stew should be saucy but not watery — the gravy is meant to soak into the sadza.

steps.title: Start the sadza
steps.instruction: In a separate large pot, bring the water to a boil. Mix a cup of maize meal with cold water to form a thin paste. Pour this paste into the boiling water, stirring constantly. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring.
steps.tip: This first step creates a smooth base that prevents lumps.

steps.title: Thicken the sadza
steps.instruction: Gradually add the remaining maize meal, stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon after each addition. The sadza is ready when it is very thick and pulls away cleanly from the pot.
steps.tip: Use a strong wooden spoon — a flat paddle called a mugoti works best.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl and scoop sadza into it, pressing to form a smooth dome. Invert onto a plate. Serve alongside the beef stew and greens.
steps.tip: Pinch off a small piece of sadza, flatten it in your palm, and use it to scoop up the stew.

recipes.name: Chakhchoukha
recipes.description: A rustic Algerian dish of hand-torn flatbread soaked in a rich, spiced tomato and lamb stew with chickpeas. A beloved comfort food from the eastern highlands.
recipes.story: Chakhchoukha hails from the Aures mountains and the high plateaux of eastern Algeria, particularly the cities of Biskra and Batna. It is the pride of the Chaoui Berber people. The dish gets its name from the sound of tearing the rougag flatbread into small pieces. Families prepare it for religious holidays, particularly Eid and Mawlid, gathering around a communal platter.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the rougag
steps.instruction: Mix semolina with salt and enough water to form a smooth dough. Roll very thin and cook on a dry griddle until lightly spotted. Tear into small bite-sized pieces and set aside.
steps.tip: The flatbread should be paper-thin for the best texture.

steps.title: Start the stew
steps.instruction: In a large pot, heat oil and brown the lamb pieces on all sides. Add onions and cook until softened, about 5 minutes.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomato paste, ras el hanout, chilli flakes, salt, and pepper. Stir well. Add the chickpeas and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 60 minutes until the lamb is very tender.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick and rich — reduce uncovered for the last 10 minutes if needed.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Place the torn flatbread pieces in a large serving dish. Ladle the hot stew and sauce over the bread, ensuring every piece is well soaked.
steps.tip: Let the bread absorb the sauce for a few minutes before serving.

steps.title: Steam and serve
steps.instruction: Cover the dish and let it sit for 5 minutes so the bread absorbs the broth. Serve on a communal platter with the meat arranged on top.
steps.tip: Traditionally eaten with spoons from a shared platter.

recipes.name: Makroud
recipes.description: Diamond-shaped semolina pastries filled with fragrant date paste and deep-fried until golden, then dipped in warm honey syrup. A treasured Algerian sweet.
recipes.story: Makroud is the jewel of Algerian patisserie, particularly associated with the city of Kairouan and the southern oasis towns. Every household prepares trays of makroud for Eid celebrations, weddings, and to welcome guests. The art lies in achieving the perfect ratio of crispy semolina shell to soft, spiced date filling, all glistening with honey.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the date filling
steps.instruction: Process the dates with cinnamon and orange blossom water until you have a smooth, thick paste. Roll into long logs about 1.5cm in diameter. Set aside.
steps.tip: If dates are dry, add a tablespoon of warm water while processing.

steps.title: Prepare the dough
steps.instruction: Combine semolina with melted butter, orange blossom water, a pinch of salt, and enough warm water to form a firm but pliable dough. Knead for 5 minutes.
steps.tip: The dough should hold together without being sticky.

steps.title: Shape the makroud
steps.instruction: Divide dough into portions. Roll each into a log and make a groove down the centre. Place a date paste log inside and seal the dough around it. Flatten slightly and cut into diamond shapes.
steps.tip: Use a fork to press a decorative pattern on each piece.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C (340F). Fry the makroud in batches for 3-4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden brown. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the pan — fry in small batches for even cooking.

steps.title: Dip in honey
steps.instruction: Warm the honey gently. Dip each fried makroud into the warm honey, turning to coat all sides. Place on a rack to let excess honey drip off.
steps.tip: Warm honey coats more evenly than cold. Let them cool completely before storing.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Arrange on a platter and allow to cool completely. Makroud keep well in an airtight container for up to a week.

recipes.name: Grilled Wagasi
recipes.description: Beninese soft white cheese grilled until golden and served with a spicy piment sauce. A unique West African cheese snack with a satisfying chewy texture.
recipes.story: Wagasi is one of the few traditional cheeses in West Africa, made by the Fula (Fulani) herders from fresh cow's milk. Grilled on roadside stalls across Benin, it is an important source of protein and a beloved snack.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the cheese
steps.instruction: Cut wagasi into slices about 2cm thick. Pat dry with paper towels.

steps.title: Grill the cheese
steps.instruction: Brush with oil and grill on a hot grill pan or barbecue for 3-4 minutes per side until golden brown with grill marks.
steps.tip: Do not move the cheese too early — let it develop a crust.

steps.title: Make piment sauce
steps.instruction: Mix blended scotch bonnets with chopped onion, a squeeze of lime, and salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve the grilled wagasi hot with the piment sauce for dipping.

recipes.name: Morogo
recipes.description: Traditional wild spinach greens cooked with tomatoes, onions, and peanut powder — a nutritious everyday dish that connects Batswana to their foraging heritage.
recipes.story: Morogo refers to a variety of wild leafy greens that have been gathered and eaten across Botswana for centuries. These nutrient-dense greens were traditionally foraged from the wild after the rains, dried for preservation, and cooked throughout the year. Today, morogo remains a staple in Batswana homes, valued for both its nutrition and its deep cultural significance.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sauté onion
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and cook until softened, about 3 minutes.

steps.title: Add tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add the diced tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce, about 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.

steps.title: Cook the greens
steps.instruction: Add the chopped morogo to the pot. Stir well, cover, and cook for 10 minutes until the greens are tender and wilted, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: If using regular spinach, reduce the cooking time to 5 minutes as it wilts faster.

steps.title: Add peanut powder
steps.instruction: Sprinkle the peanut powder over the greens and stir thoroughly. Cook for another 3 minutes to allow the peanut flavor to meld with the vegetables. Season with salt.
steps.tip: The peanut powder adds protein and a subtle nutty flavor that is characteristic of this dish.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve the morogo warm as a side dish alongside pap, bogobe, or seswaa.
steps.tip: Morogo can also be dried and stored for months — rehydrate in water before cooking.

recipes.name: Vetkoek with Mince
recipes.description: Golden deep-fried bread rolls with a crispy exterior and soft, fluffy interior, split open and filled with savory spiced mince — a beloved Southern African street food.
recipes.story: Known as magwinya in Botswana and vetkoek in South Africa, these deep-fried dough balls are one of the most popular street foods across Southern Africa. Vendors selling freshly fried magwinya can be found at bus stations, markets, and school gates. They can be filled with savory mince, jam, cheese, or simply eaten plain with butter.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Add warm water gradually, mixing to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
steps.tip: The dough should be softer than bread dough — slightly sticky is perfect.

steps.title: Let dough rise
steps.instruction: Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave in a warm place to rise for 1 hour until doubled in size.

steps.title: Prepare the filling
steps.instruction: While the dough rises, brown the mince with diced onion in a pan over medium-high heat. Season with salt, pepper, and a pinch of curry powder. Cook until done, about 10 minutes. Set aside.

steps.title: Shape the vetkoek
steps.instruction: Punch down the dough and divide into 8 equal portions. Roll each into a smooth ball. Let rest for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Deep fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170°C in a deep pot. Carefully lower 2-3 dough balls at a time into the oil. Fry for 3-4 minutes per side until deep golden brown and cooked through. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Do not let the oil get too hot or the outside will burn before the inside cooks through.

steps.title: Fill and serve
steps.instruction: Slice each vetkoek open along one side. Spoon in the warm mince filling. Serve immediately with chilli sauce or atchar on the side.
steps.tip: For a sweet version, fill with apricot jam or syrup instead of mince.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Babenda
recipes.description: A nourishing porridge of millet flour and leafy greens simmered with dawadawa for umami depth. A staple of everyday Burkinabé cooking.
recipes.story: Babenda is the soul food of Burkina Faso, eaten daily by millions. It celebrates the Sahelian pantry of millet, leafy greens, and fermented seasonings. Every family has their own version passed down through generations.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the greens
steps.instruction: Wash and chop the leafy greens. Boil in salted water for 10 minutes until tender. Drain, reserving the cooking liquid.

steps.title: Make millet paste
steps.instruction: Mix millet flour with 1 cup cold water to form a smooth paste.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: In a pot, heat shea butter and fry onion until soft. Add dawadawa and stir. Add 3 cups of the reserved greens liquid and bring to a boil.

steps.title: Combine and cook
steps.instruction: Pour in the millet paste, stirring constantly. Add the cooked greens and stir well. Cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring frequently, until thick and porridge-like.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot in bowls. Babenda should have the consistency of a thick, hearty porridge.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Poulet Bicyclette
recipes.description: Grilled free-range chicken marinated in a spicy mustard-lemon mix. Named after the free-range chickens that roam like bicycles through Burkinabé villages.
recipes.story: Poulet Bicyclette gets its name from the lean, free-range chickens that roam Burkinabé villages and towns on foot. Their meat is firmer and more flavourful than factory-farmed birds. Grilled over charcoal, it is the king of Ouagadougou's maquis (open-air bars).
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.instruction: Mix mustard, lime juice, garlic, cayenne, oil, salt, and pepper into a paste.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub the marinade all over the chicken, getting under the skin. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
steps.tip: Getting marinade under the skin is key to flavour.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.instruction: Grill over medium charcoal heat, turning regularly, for about 35-40 minutes until the skin is crispy and the juices run clear.
steps.tip: Keep the heat medium to cook through without burning.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the chicken rest for 5 minutes before cutting into pieces. Serve with your choice of sides.

recipes.name: Burundian Isombe
recipes.description: Burundi's version of pounded cassava leaves simmered with palm oil, onions, and a touch of dried fish.
recipes.story: Isombe is shared across Rwanda and Burundi but each country has its own twist. Burundian isombe often includes dried fish for an umami depth and is simmered longer until silky smooth. It is a dish of resilience, made from cassava leaves that grow abundantly even in difficult times, nourishing families across the country.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Leaves
steps.instruction: If using fresh cassava leaves, pound in a mortar to a fine paste. If using frozen, thaw and drain.
steps.tip: The finer you pound the leaves, the smoother and creamier the final dish.

steps.title: Cook Base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a pot. Saute onion and garlic until golden. Add tomatoes and eggplant, cook until softened.

steps.title: Add Leaves and Fish
steps.instruction: Add pounded cassava leaves, dried fish, and water. Stir well to combine.
steps.tip: The dried fish dissolves during cooking and adds deep savory flavor.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer, stirring occasionally, until leaves are very tender and sauce is thick.
steps.tip: Burundian cooks simmer isombe for at least 40 minutes — patience makes it silky.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Serve with ubugari (cassava or maize porridge) and beans.
steps.tip: Isombe and beans together provide complete protein — a traditional Burundian combination.

recipes.name: Mukeke
recipes.description: Lake Tanganyika's prized fish grilled whole with a zesty lemon-garlic marinade — Burundi's freshwater treasure.
recipes.story: Mukeke is a freshwater fish from Lake Tanganyika, the second deepest lake in the world. It is the most prized fish in Burundian cuisine, known for its firm white flesh and clean flavor. Fishermen bring their catch to the lakeside markets of Bujumbura where mukeke commands the highest prices.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate Fish
steps.instruction: Score fish deeply on both sides. Mix lemon juice, garlic, ginger, oil, salt, pepper, and paprika. Rub marinade all over and inside the fish.
steps.tip: Let the fish marinate at least 30 minutes for the lemon and garlic to penetrate.

steps.title: Heat Grill
steps.instruction: Heat charcoal grill until coals are white-hot. Oil the grate to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Grill Fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish over medium-hot coals, turning once carefully, until skin is charred and flesh is opaque and flakes easily.
steps.tip: Use a fish basket or two spatulas to flip — mukeke breaks apart if handled roughly.

steps.title: Prepare Garnish
steps.instruction: Arrange sliced tomatoes and onions on a plate. Season with a squeeze of lemon and salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place grilled fish on the bed of tomatoes and onions. Serve with ubugari or fried plantains.
steps.tip: In Bujumbura, grilled mukeke by the lake at sunset is a quintessential Burundian experience.

recipes.name: Ubugari
recipes.description: Burundi's cassava flour porridge — smooth, stretchy, and the daily staple that anchors every meal.
recipes.story: Ubugari is the daily bread of Burundi, made from cassava flour cooked into a thick, smooth porridge. It serves as the base of virtually every meal, paired with beans, isombe, or fish stew. In Burundian culture, refusing ubugari is considered rude, and the quality of one's ubugari is a mark of cooking skill.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Water
steps.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil in a heavy pot. Add salt.

steps.title: Cook Ubugari
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add cassava flour gradually, stirring constantly and vigorously with a wooden spoon until thick, smooth, and elastic.
steps.tip: Cassava flour thickens faster than maize — add it slowly to control the consistency.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Wet a plate and turn ubugari out in a dome shape. Cover to keep warm.

steps.title: Prepare Beans
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a pan. Saute onion until soft. Add tomatoes and cook until saucy. Add cooked beans and salt. Simmer 10 minutes.
steps.tip: Palm oil gives the beans their distinctive orange color and rich flavor.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place ubugari dome on plate with bean stew alongside. Eat by pinching off pieces and dipping.
steps.tip: In Burundi, ubugari and beans together are considered a complete, balanced meal.

collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

recipes.name: Eru
recipes.description: A hearty stew of shredded eru leaves and waterleaf cooked with palm oil, smoked fish, and cow skin. A signature dish of the South-West Cameroon region.
recipes.story: Eru is the soul food of the anglophone South-West region of Cameroon, especially among the Bayangi people. The wild eru vine (Gnetum africanum) grows in the dense equatorial forest and its leaves are hand-shredded into thin strips. Combined with the wilting waterleaf, generous palm oil, and smoked proteins, it creates a dish that is simultaneously light and deeply flavourful.
recipes.best_served_with: Garri (cassava granules soaked in water) or fufu corn
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: Wash the eru leaves thoroughly. If using fresh, shred into very thin strips. Wash and roughly chop the waterleaf.
steps.tip: Eru leaves should be cut as thinly as possible — this is key to the texture.

steps.title: Cook the waterleaf
steps.instruction: Place the waterleaf in a dry pot over medium heat. Cook, stirring occasionally, until it wilts and releases all its water, about 10 minutes. Drain off the excess liquid.
steps.tip: Waterleaf releases a lot of water — draining it prevents a watery final dish.

steps.title: Add palm oil and proteins
steps.instruction: Add palm oil to the wilted waterleaf. Stir in the smoked fish, cow skin, and ground crayfish. Cook for 5 minutes to meld the flavours.

steps.title: Add eru leaves
steps.instruction: Add the shredded eru leaves and stir to combine. The eru should absorb the palm oil and soften slightly. Cook on low heat for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: Do not add water — the dish should be dry and oily, not soupy.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt and stir. Serve hot with garri soaked in cold water or fufu corn.
steps.tip: Eru is traditionally eaten with the fingers, using garri as a scoop.

recipes.name: Canja de Galinha
recipes.description: A comforting Cape Verdean chicken and rice soup with vegetables and a squeeze of lime. The Cape Verdean version of the Portuguese classic, adapted with local spices.
recipes.story: Canja was brought to Cape Verde by Portuguese colonists and has become a cherished comfort food. It is the first dish offered to the sick, to new mothers, and to anyone in need of nourishment and warmth.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Place chicken in a pot with 6 cups water, onion, garlic, and salt. Boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Remove chicken, shred the meat, discard bones.

steps.title: Add rice and vegetables
steps.instruction: Add rice and carrots to the broth. Simmer for 15 minutes until rice is cooked and carrots are tender.

steps.title: Return chicken
steps.instruction: Add shredded chicken back to the soup. Season with salt, pepper, and a generous squeeze of lime.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls and serve hot with extra lime wedges and crusty bread.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Pastel com Diablo Dentro
recipes.description: Cape Verdean fried pastries stuffed with a fiery tuna and tomato filling. The name means "pastry with the devil inside" due to the spicy filling.
recipes.story: These fiery little pastries are a staple of Cape Verdean street food and celebrations. The "devil inside" is the combination of malagueta peppers and spiced tuna that gives an unexpected kick with every bite.
recipes.best_served_with: On its own as a snack
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, oil, salt, and warm water into a smooth dough. Knead for 5 minutes until elastic. Rest 30 minutes covered.

steps.title: Make the filling
steps.instruction: Fry onion until soft. Add tomatoes and peppers, cook 5 minutes. Add tuna and cook 5 more minutes until dry. Season well.
steps.tip: The filling must be dry — wet filling makes soggy pastels.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Roll dough thin. Cut into circles. Place a spoonful of filling in the centre, fold into half-moons, and seal edges with a fork.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Deep fry in oil at 180C until golden brown and crispy, about 3-4 minutes.
steps.tip: Fry in batches without crowding.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Serve hot.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Kanda ti Nyma
recipes.description: Savoury beef meatballs simmered in a thick peanut and tomato sauce. A hearty dish from the Central African Republic that is packed with protein and flavour.
recipes.story: Kanda ti nyma — meaning "meat balls" in Sango — is a festive dish reserved for special occasions and honoured guests. The meatballs are often made with hand-minced beef and bound with ground peanuts, which give them a distinctive nutty flavour and firm texture. Simmered in a rich tomato-peanut sauce, this is comfort food at its finest in the Central African Republic.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the meatballs
steps.instruction: Combine beef mince, ground peanuts, finely diced onion, and salt in a bowl. Mix well with your hands until the mixture holds together. Shape into golf ball-sized meatballs.
steps.tip: The ground peanuts act as a binder — no egg needed.

steps.title: Brown the meatballs
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pan and brown the meatballs on all sides, about 6 minutes total. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the pan — brown in batches for the best crust.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: In the same pan, sauté the diced onion until soft. Add the blended tomatoes and cook for 10 minutes until thickened. Stir in the peanut butter and 1 cup of water, mixing until smooth.

steps.title: Simmer meatballs in sauce
steps.instruction: Return the meatballs to the sauce. Cover and simmer gently for 25 minutes, turning the meatballs once halfway through.
steps.tip: Keep the heat low — vigorous boiling will break the meatballs apart.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste the sauce and adjust seasoning. Serve the meatballs and sauce over gozo or rice.

recipes.name: Sauce Graine
recipes.description: A rich palm fruit sauce simmered with smoked fish, tomatoes, and hot peppers. The quintessential accompaniment to gozo in the Central African Republic.
recipes.story: Sauce graine — literally "seed sauce" — is made from the pulp of palm fruits, which are boiled and pressed to extract a thick, rich cream. In the Central African Republic, this sauce is the most common pairing with gozo. The addition of smoked fish and chilli creates layers of flavour that make this simple sauce remarkably complex.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the palm cream
steps.instruction: If using fresh palm fruits, boil for 20 minutes, then pound and strain to extract the cream. If using canned palm cream, open and stir until smooth.
steps.tip: Canned palm cream is widely available and saves significant preparation time.

steps.title: Sauté the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat a tablespoon of palm oil in a pot. Cook the onion until soft and translucent, about 5 minutes. Add the chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down.

steps.title: Add the palm cream
steps.instruction: Stir in the palm cream and water. Bring to a simmer, stirring well to prevent separation.

steps.title: Simmer with fish
steps.instruction: Add the flaked smoked fish and whole scotch bonnet peppers. Cover and simmer for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is rich and thickened with orange oil floating on top.
steps.tip: The orange oil on top is a sign the sauce is ready.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the scotch bonnet peppers. Taste for salt and serve hot over gozo or rice.

recipes.name: Boule
recipes.description: A smooth, firm millet porridge shaped into balls and served as the staple accompaniment to sauces and stews. The foundation of every Chadian meal.
recipes.story: Boule is to Chad what bread is to France. This thick millet porridge is prepared daily in virtually every household, and eating without it feels incomplete. The millet is ground into flour and cooked with water into a firm, smooth paste. It is eaten by tearing off pieces and using them to scoop up stews and sauces — a communal act that defines Chadian mealtimes.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the porridge
steps.instruction: Bring 5 cups of water to a boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Mix 1 cup of millet flour with 1 cup of cold water to make a slurry. Pour the slurry into the boiling water, stirring constantly.
steps.tip: The slurry prevents lumps from forming in the final porridge.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Stir the mixture continuously over medium heat for 5 minutes until it begins to thicken. Add salt if using.

steps.title: Add remaining flour
steps.instruction: Gradually add the remaining 2 cups of millet flour, stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon. The mixture will become very thick and stiff.
steps.tip: Use a strong wooden spoon — metal spoons can bend under the pressure.

steps.title: Finish cooking
steps.instruction: Continue stirring for another 10 minutes over low heat until the boule pulls away from the sides of the pot and is completely smooth.
steps.tip: The more you stir, the smoother the boule will be.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or your hands and shape the boule into smooth rounds. Serve on a plate alongside stews and sauces.
steps.tip: Traditionally served in a communal bowl for everyone to share.

recipes.name: La Bouillie
recipes.description: A warm, sweet millet porridge flavoured with vanilla, lemon zest, and sugar. A cherished Chadian breakfast served to children and adults alike.
recipes.story: La Bouillie is the breakfast that fuels Chad from N'Djamena to the rural south. This thin, sweet millet porridge is the first food many Chadian children eat, and it remains a comfort throughout life. Street vendors sell it from large pots in the morning, ladling it into bowls for workers and students rushing to start their day.
recipes.best_served_with: Bread, beignets, or on its own
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the slurry
steps.instruction: Mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water until smooth, ensuring there are no lumps.

steps.title: Cook the porridge
steps.instruction: Bring the remaining 3 cups of water to a boil. Slowly pour in the millet slurry while stirring constantly. Reduce heat to medium-low and cook, stirring, for 10 minutes until thickened.
steps.tip: Constant stirring prevents lumps and scorching.

steps.title: Add sweetness and flavour
steps.instruction: Stir in the sugar, milk, vanilla extract, and lemon zest. Continue cooking for another 5 minutes until everything is well combined and the porridge reaches your desired consistency.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls and serve warm. The porridge can be made thinner or thicker by adjusting the water.
steps.tip: Add more milk for a creamier texture, or thin with water for a drinkable consistency.

recipes.name: Langouste a la Vanille
recipes.description: Comorian lobster in a luxurious vanilla-infused coconut cream sauce — the islands' most celebrated dish.
recipes.story: Comoros is one of the world's top vanilla producers, and this dish marries that prized spice with fresh Indian Ocean lobster. The combination of sweet vanilla and briny lobster in coconut cream seems unlikely but creates an unforgettable harmony. It is the signature dish of Comorian haute cuisine, served at celebrations and to honored guests.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sear Lobster
steps.instruction: Melt butter in a large pan over high heat. Sear lobster tails flesh-side down for 2 minutes until golden. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not overcook at this stage — the lobster will finish cooking in the sauce.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: In the same pan, saute shallots and garlic until soft. Add white wine and reduce by half.

steps.title: Make Sauce
steps.instruction: Add coconut cream, vanilla bean pods and seeds. Simmer gently until sauce thickens slightly.
steps.tip: Use real vanilla beans — extract cannot replicate the flavor in this dish.

steps.title: Finish Lobster
steps.instruction: Return lobster tails to the sauce. Simmer gently until lobster is just cooked through and opaque.
steps.tip: Lobster is done when the flesh is opaque and firm — overcooking makes it rubbery.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove vanilla pods. Add lime juice and season with salt and white pepper. Serve lobster with the sauce spooned over, alongside rice.
steps.tip: The vanilla aroma should be present but subtle — it should enhance the lobster, not dominate.

recipes.name: Mkatra Foutra
recipes.description: Comorian coconut bread baked with yeast and flavored with cardamom — dense, fragrant, and slightly sweet.
recipes.story: Mkatra Foutra is the ceremonial bread of the Comoros Islands, essential at weddings and the Grand Mariage — the most important social ceremony in Comorian culture. The bread is enriched with coconut milk and eggs, scented with cardamom, and baked until golden. No celebration is complete without stacks of mkatra foutra.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, cardamom, and salt. Add warm coconut milk, eggs, and butter. Knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
steps.tip: The dough should be soft and slightly sticky — do not add too much flour.

steps.title: First Rise
steps.instruction: Cover dough and let rise in a warm place for 1.5 hours until doubled.
steps.tip: Coconut milk enriches the dough but can slow rising — be patient.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Punch down dough and shape into a round flat disc about 2 inches thick. Place on a greased baking sheet.

steps.title: Second Rise
steps.instruction: Let rest 30 minutes. Brush top with egg yolk and sprinkle with sesame seeds.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Bake at 180C until golden brown and bread sounds hollow when tapped on the bottom.
steps.tip: The bread should be golden brown on top and have a dense, moist crumb inside.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Let cool slightly before slicing. Serve with butter, honey, or alongside fish curry.
steps.tip: Mkatra foutra stays fresh for several days — its density keeps it moist.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Pilao ya Nazi
recipes.description: Comorian coconut rice pilaf infused with cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon — fragrant and creamy.
recipes.story: Pilao ya Nazi showcases the Comorian love for coconut, which appears in nearly every dish on the islands. The rice absorbs the rich coconut milk and warm spices, becoming incredibly fragrant and creamy. It is the perfect accompaniment to the islands' seafood dishes and is served at every special occasion.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Saute
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Saute sliced onion until golden. Add cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and turmeric. Stir 1 minute.

steps.title: Toast Rice
steps.instruction: Add rinsed rice and stir to coat with the spiced oil. Toast for 2 minutes.
steps.tip: Toasting the rice before adding liquid gives each grain a nuttier flavor.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Pour in coconut milk and water. Add salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly and cook until rice is fluffy and liquid absorbed.
steps.tip: Do not stir or lift the lid — let the steam cook the rice perfectly.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let rest covered for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fluff gently with a fork. Remove whole spices if desired. Serve as a bed for fish, lobster, or meat stew.
steps.tip: The coconut milk makes the rice slightly sticky and creamy — this is the desired texture.

collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Liboke ya Mbika
recipes.description: Ground pumpkin seeds steamed in banana leaves with tomatoes, onions, and spices, creating a rich and fragrant vegetarian parcel. A classic Congolese preparation.
recipes.story: Liboke refers to any dish cooked in banana leaf parcels — a cooking technique central to Congolese cuisine. This version uses ground pumpkin seeds (mbika), which form a rich paste when steamed. The banana leaves impart a subtle grassy aroma while keeping everything moist. It is a favourite during Lent and among those who prefer plant-based meals.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Grind the seeds
steps.instruction: Toast pumpkin seeds in a dry pan for 3 minutes until fragrant. Grind to a fine paste in a blender or food processor, adding a splash of water if needed.
steps.tip: Do not over-toast — the seeds should be lightly golden, not dark.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.instruction: Combine the ground pumpkin seed paste with diced tomatoes, chopped onion, palm oil, minced scotch bonnet, and salt. Mix until everything is well incorporated.

steps.title: Wrap in banana leaves
steps.instruction: Soften banana leaves by briefly passing them over an open flame or blanching in hot water. Place a generous scoop of the mixture in the centre of each leaf and fold into a secure parcel, tying with kitchen string or strips of banana leaf.
steps.tip: Double-wrap the parcels to prevent leaking during steaming.

steps.title: Steam the parcels
steps.instruction: Place parcels in a steamer basket or in a pot with a small amount of water at the bottom. Cover and steam for 40-45 minutes until the filling is firm and cooked through.
steps.tip: Check water level halfway through — do not let the pot run dry.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Unwrap the parcels at the table for a dramatic presentation. Serve with fufu or boiled plantains.
steps.tip: The banana leaf aroma is part of the experience — serve immediately after unwrapping.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Saka-Saka
recipes.description: Finely shredded cassava leaves slow-cooked with palm oil, onions, and dried fish until meltingly tender. A beloved Congolese staple rich in iron and flavour.
recipes.story: Saka-Saka is eaten daily across the Congo, from the streets of Kinshasa to remote villages in the interior. Cassava leaves must be pounded and cooked for a long time to remove bitterness and release their nutrients. The addition of dried fish or smoked meat transforms simple greens into a deeply savoury dish that nourishes millions.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: If using fresh cassava leaves, wash thoroughly and pound in a mortar or blend until finely shredded. If using frozen, thaw and squeeze out excess water.
steps.tip: Frozen cassava leaves are widely available in African grocery stores and save significant prep time.

steps.title: Start the greens
steps.instruction: Place the cassava leaves in a large pot with water. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 30 minutes to begin softening the tough leaves.
steps.tip: Cassava leaves require long cooking — do not rush this step.

steps.title: Build the flavour base
steps.instruction: In a separate pan, heat palm oil and sauté the onion until golden. Add garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the dried fish and crumbled stock cube, stirring to combine.

steps.title: Combine and slow-cook
steps.instruction: Add the onion-fish mixture to the cassava leaves. Drop in the whole scotch bonnet. Stir well, cover, and simmer on low heat for another 45-60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the leaves are very tender and the sauce has thickened.
steps.tip: Add splashes of water if the pot gets dry. The final dish should be moist but not soupy.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.instruction: Remove the scotch bonnet. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt. Serve hot as a side dish alongside fufu or boiled cassava.

recipes.name: Djiboutian Laxoox
recipes.description: Djibouti's spongy fermented crepe made with sorghum and wheat flour — served with honey, ghee, or savory stews.
recipes.story: Laxoox is the daily bread of Djibouti, a spongy fermented crepe that bridges the gap between Ethiopian injera and Yemeni lahoh. Every morning, Djiboutian homes fill with the aroma of laxoox cooking on flat griddles. It is incredibly versatile — drizzled with honey and ghee for a sweet breakfast or used to scoop up spicy stews.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Mix Batter
steps.instruction: Combine both flours, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm water and whisk until smooth and lump-free.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover and let batter ferment for 2-3 hours until bubbly and slightly sour-smelling.
steps.tip: A warm spot speeds fermentation — near an oven or in sunlight works well.

steps.title: Cook Laxoox
steps.instruction: Heat a non-stick pan over medium heat. Pour a thin layer of batter, swirling to cover. Cook until surface is covered in bubbles and edges pull away. Do not flip.
steps.tip: The surface should be covered in tiny holes — this spongy texture is what makes laxoox special.

steps.title: Stack
steps.instruction: Remove carefully and stack on a plate. Repeat with remaining batter.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fold or roll laxoox. Drizzle with ghee and honey for sweet breakfast, or serve alongside fah-fah or stew.
steps.tip: Djiboutians often dip laxoox in sweet spiced tea for a quick morning meal.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Fah-Fah
recipes.description: Djibouti's hearty goat soup with vegetables, cumin, and chili — a restorative broth for all occasions.
recipes.story: Fah-Fah is a hearty goat bone soup that Djiboutians turn to for warmth, nourishment, and celebration. It is traditionally served to welcome guests, nourish new mothers, and break the Ramadan fast. The slow-simmered broth extracts every bit of flavor from the bones, creating a deeply satisfying soup.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start Broth
steps.instruction: Place goat pieces in a large pot with water. Bring to a boil, skimming any foam that rises to the surface.
steps.tip: Skimming the foam ensures a clear, clean-tasting broth.

steps.title: Add Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and whole chili. Reduce heat and simmer until meat is very tender.
steps.tip: The longer you simmer, the richer the broth — at least an hour for best results.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots and potatoes. Continue simmering until vegetables are tender.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Remove the whole chili. Season with salt. The broth should be richly flavored.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into deep bowls with meat and vegetables. Garnish with cilantro and a lime wedge. Serve with bread for dipping.
steps.tip: Fah-Fah is traditionally served in large communal bowls for sharing.

recipes.name: Skoudehkaris
recipes.description: Djibouti's national dish — spiced rice with lamb, caramelized onions, cardamom, and cumin, a one-pot feast.
recipes.story: Skoudehkaris is the national dish of Djibouti, a fragrant lamb and rice preparation that reflects the country's position at the crossroads of Somali, Afar, Yemeni, and French culinary influences. Served at every important occasion, it is the dish that unites Djibouti's diverse communities around one table.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Lamb
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Season lamb with salt and sear until deeply browned on all sides.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until caramelized. Add garlic, tomato, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, and turmeric. Cook 2 minutes.
steps.tip: Deeply caramelized onions are essential to the dish's rich color and sweetness.

steps.title: Simmer Lamb
steps.instruction: Add water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer until lamb is almost tender.

steps.title: Cook Rice
steps.instruction: Add drained rice to the pot with the lamb and broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly and cook until rice is fluffy and liquid absorbed.
steps.tip: Do not stir once the rice is added — let the steam do the work.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Let rest covered 10 minutes. Fluff gently. Mound on a platter with lamb arranged on top. Serve with banana and salad.
steps.tip: Like Somali bariis, skoudehkaris is traditionally served with a banana on the side.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Ful Medames
recipes.description: Slow-cooked fava beans mashed with garlic, lemon, and cumin, drizzled with olive oil and served with warm pita. Egypt's beloved everyday breakfast.
recipes.story: Ful medames may be the oldest dish still eaten in Egypt, tracing back to the pharaohs. Today it is the breakfast of millions — from street vendors serving it in paper-lined bowls at dawn to families enjoying it around the kitchen table. The word medames means buried, referring to the ancient practice of slow-cooking the beans in a pot buried in hot embers overnight. The ful cart is an institution in every Egyptian neighbourhood.
recipes.best_served_with: Warm pita bread, pickled turnips, and fresh vegetables
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Drain the soaked fava beans, place in a large pot, and cover with fresh water. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for about 1 hour until the beans are very soft and creamy. Reserve some cooking liquid before draining.
steps.tip: For an authentic texture, some beans should be falling apart while others stay whole.

steps.title: Mash and season
steps.instruction: Partially mash the beans with a fork or potato masher, leaving some whole for texture. Add garlic, cumin, lemon juice, and salt. Stir well, adding cooking liquid as needed to reach your desired consistency.
steps.tip: The ful should be creamy but not a smooth puree — texture is key.

steps.title: Warm through
steps.instruction: Return the mashed beans to low heat and warm through for 5 minutes, stirring gently to let the garlic and cumin infuse.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Spoon into shallow bowls. Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle with cumin and parsley, and serve with warm pita bread, pickled turnips, and sliced tomatoes.
steps.tip: Every diner should customise their bowl with extra lemon, cumin, or chilli to taste.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Molokhia
recipes.description: A vibrant green soup made from finely chopped jute leaves simmered in chicken broth, finished with a sizzling garlic and coriander dressing. Egypt's most iconic home-cooked meal.
recipes.story: Molokhia has been eaten in Egypt for thousands of years, with roots in the pharaonic era. The name comes from the Arabic word for kings — legend says it was once reserved for royalty. The signature moment is the tash-a — a sizzle of garlic and ground coriander fried in ghee and stirred into the pot, releasing an unmistakable aroma that signals dinner is ready in Egyptian households.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the broth
steps.instruction: Poach a whole chicken in water with an onion, bay leaves, and cardamom pods for about 40 minutes until cooked through. Remove the chicken and strain the broth. Shred the chicken meat and set aside.
steps.tip: A rich, flavourful broth is the foundation — do not skip the aromatics.

steps.title: Cook the molokhia
steps.instruction: Bring the chicken broth to a boil. Add the finely chopped molokhia leaves and stir well. Reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally. The soup should be thick and slightly viscous.
steps.tip: Never cover the pot while cooking molokhia — it can turn the leaves brown.

steps.title: Prepare the tash-a
steps.instruction: In a small pan, heat the ghee until very hot. Add the garlic and fry until golden, about 30 seconds. Add the ground coriander and stir for 10 seconds until fragrant — it should sizzle vigorously.
steps.tip: Watch carefully — the garlic burns quickly. It should be golden, not brown.

steps.title: Finish the soup
steps.instruction: Pour the sizzling tash-a directly into the molokhia pot. Stir well — it will bubble dramatically. This is the moment that defines Egyptian molokhia.
steps.tip: The dramatic sizzle when the garlic hits the soup is the hallmark of properly made molokhia.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the molokhia over white rice in deep plates. Serve the shredded chicken alongside, with lemon wedges and bread.
steps.tip: Some Egyptians eat molokhia with vermicelli rice and others with plain white rice — both are traditional.

recipes.name: Jollof Rice Equatoguineano
recipes.description: Equatorial Guinea's take on the iconic West African rice dish — fragrant long-grain rice cooked in a smoky tomato and pepper sauce with Spanish-influenced seasonings.
recipes.story: As the only Spanish-speaking country in Central Africa, Equatorial Guinea has a unique culinary identity that blends Fang and Bubi traditions with Spanish and broader West African influences. Their jollof rice uses smoked paprika alongside traditional African peppers, and is often prepared with palm oil rather than vegetable oil, giving it a distinctive orange hue and richer flavour.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantains, grilled chicken, or a fresh salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the tomato base
steps.instruction: Blend the tomatoes and one onion until smooth. Heat palm oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot. Sauté the sliced onion until golden. Add tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Cook the sauce
steps.instruction: Pour in the blended tomato-onion mixture. Add smoked paprika, bay leaves, and the whole scotch bonnet. Cook on medium heat for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is reduced and the oil floats on top.
steps.tip: The sauce must be well-reduced before adding rice — this is the key to flavourful jollof.

steps.title: Add rice and water
steps.instruction: Add the washed rice to the sauce and stir to coat every grain. Pour in 3 cups of hot water. Season with salt and stir once. Bring to a boil.
steps.tip: Use hot water to avoid shocking the rice and slowing the cooking.

steps.title: Steam the rice
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to the lowest setting. Cover the pot tightly with foil and then the lid. Cook without opening for 30 minutes until the rice is tender and has absorbed all the liquid.
steps.tip: Do not lift the lid or stir during this time — the steam does the work.

steps.title: Fluff and serve
steps.instruction: Remove from heat. Remove the bay leaves and scotch bonnet. Fluff the rice gently with a fork. Serve hot with fried plantains and grilled chicken.
steps.tip: The slightly crispy rice at the bottom of the pot is the best part — scrape it up and serve it.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Pepesoup
recipes.description: A fiery, aromatic fish soup spiced with calabash nutmeg, alligator pepper, and scotch bonnet. A beloved Central African restorative served at celebrations and as a hangover cure.
recipes.story: Pepesoup is the universal comfort food of Central and West Africa, and Equatorial Guinea's version has its own distinct character thanks to the Bubi people of Bioko Island. Fresh fish from the Gulf of Guinea is simmered in an intensely spiced broth featuring indigenous aromatics. It is served at celebrations, offered to new mothers for recovery, and consumed the morning after festivities as a restorative.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Rub the fish chunks with salt, a squeeze of lemon juice, and half the ground spices. Let sit for 10 minutes.
steps.tip: This brief marination firms up the fish and prevents it from falling apart in the soup.

steps.title: Build the broth
steps.instruction: Bring 6 cups of water to a boil in a large pot. Add the onion, garlic, ginger, and remaining spices. Boil for 10 minutes to create a fragrant broth.

steps.title: Add the fish
steps.instruction: Gently lower the seasoned fish into the broth. Add the whole scotch bonnet peppers. Reduce heat to medium and cook for 15 minutes until the fish is opaque and cooked through.
steps.tip: Do not stir vigorously — use a gentle swirling motion to prevent the fish from breaking.

steps.title: Adjust and serve
steps.instruction: Taste the broth and adjust salt and pepper. For more heat, gently press one of the scotch bonnets against the side of the pot. Ladle into bowls, ensuring each serving gets fish and broth. Serve hot.
steps.tip: The soup should be thin and brothy, not thick — it is meant to be sipped as well as eaten.

recipes.name: Eritrean Ful Medames
recipes.description: Slow-cooked fava beans mashed with olive oil, lemon, cumin, and chili — Eritrea's beloved breakfast dish.
recipes.story: Ful is the quintessential Eritrean breakfast, served at small restaurants called ful houses across Asmara. Each ful house has its own style — some add yogurt, others add berbere, some top with egg. The dish connects Eritrea to a broader Horn of Africa and Middle Eastern breakfast tradition while maintaining its distinctly Eritrean character.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Heat Beans
steps.instruction: Place fava beans with their liquid in a pot. Heat over medium, mashing some with a fork for a thick, chunky texture.
steps.tip: Mash about half the beans for texture — leave the other half whole.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Add garlic, cumin, berbere, and salt. Stir well and cook for 5 minutes until flavors meld.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Remove from heat. Stir in olive oil and lemon juice. The ful should be creamy but not soupy.
steps.tip: Add olive oil off the heat to preserve its flavor.

steps.title: Garnish
steps.instruction: Transfer to a bowl. Top with diced tomatoes, green chili, a drizzle of olive oil, and a hard-boiled egg if desired.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve with crusty bread rolls for scooping, or with injera. Accompany with sweet spiced tea.
steps.tip: In Asmara, ful is always served with a macchiato — the Italian coffee tradition lives on in Eritrea.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Eritrean Shiro
recipes.description: Silky Eritrean chickpea flour stew spiced with berbere, garlic, and niter kibbeh — creamy comfort in every bite.
recipes.story: Shiro is the everyday hero of Eritrean cuisine and the essential fasting food during Orthodox Christian Lent. Made from roasted chickpea flour, it comes together in under 30 minutes but tastes like it simmered for hours. It is the most affordable and beloved dish in the country, eaten by rich and poor alike.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Saute Base
steps.instruction: Heat niter kibbeh in a pot. Saute onion until softened. Add garlic, berbere, and jalapeno. Cook 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add Liquid
steps.instruction: Add tomato paste and water. Bring to a simmer.

steps.title: Add Shiro
steps.instruction: Gradually whisk in shiro powder, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. The mixture will thicken quickly.
steps.tip: Add shiro powder slowly while stirring — it thickens fast and can clump if added too quickly.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to low. Simmer, stirring frequently, until thick and creamy with a porridge-like consistency.
steps.tip: Shiro should be thick enough to hold its shape on injera but still pourable.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle shiro onto injera. Drizzle with a little extra niter kibbeh on top. Serve hot.
steps.tip: During fasting season, replace niter kibbeh with olive oil for a fully vegan version.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Zigini with Injera
recipes.description: Eritrea's fiery beef stew in berbere sauce served on tangy injera — the country's most iconic dish.
recipes.story: Zigini is Eritrea's national dish and a source of deep pride. While similar to Ethiopian dishes, Eritrean zigini has its own character — the berbere is often spicier and the niter kibbeh richer. It is the dish served to honored guests and at every celebration. Eritreans abroad say the smell of zigini cooking is the smell of home.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Dry-Cook Onions
steps.instruction: Place diced onions in a dry pot over medium heat. Cook, stirring frequently, until deeply browned and reduced. Do not add oil or water.
steps.tip: This step is non-negotiable — dry-cooked onions create the foundation of flavor.

steps.title: Build Sauce
steps.instruction: Add niter kibbeh, garlic, ginger, and berbere. Stir continuously for 5 minutes. Add tomato paste and water.
steps.tip: The berbere should toast in the fat until you can smell each individual spice.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add beef cubes to the sauce. Stir to coat completely. Cover and simmer on low heat until beef is very tender and sauce is thick.

steps.title: Adjust
steps.instruction: Check seasoning and add salt. The sauce should be thick enough to cling to the meat without being dry.
steps.tip: If sauce is too thick, add a splash of water. If too thin, simmer uncovered.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Spread injera on a large platter. Ladle zigini in the center. Serve with extra rolled injera on the side.
steps.tip: In Eritrean tradition, the eldest person eats first, and feeding someone by hand (suwa) is a sign of love.

recipes.name: Incwancwa
recipes.description: A tangy Swazi fermented maize porridge with a refreshing sour taste — served as a cooling, nutritious drink-like porridge often enjoyed during warm weather.
recipes.story: Incwancwa is a traditional fermented maize porridge that the Swazi people have enjoyed for generations. Unlike thick sishwala, incwancwa is thin enough to drink and has a pleasant sour tang from natural fermentation. It is particularly popular during the hot summer months as a refreshing, energy-giving drink, and is commonly served at communal work parties and gatherings.
recipes.best_served_with: Served as a standalone drink or light meal
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the porridge
steps.instruction: Mix the maize meal with 500ml of cold water to form a smooth paste. Bring the remaining litre of water to a boil. Gradually stir the paste into the boiling water. Cook for 15 minutes, stirring frequently, until thickened.

steps.title: Cool down
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let the porridge cool to lukewarm. It should be warm to the touch but not hot.
steps.tip: The temperature is important — too hot and it will kill the fermenting agents.

steps.title: Add malt and ferment
steps.instruction: Stir in the sorghum malt until well combined. Cover with a clean cloth and leave at room temperature for 24-48 hours, stirring once or twice during this time.
steps.tip: The longer you ferment, the more sour it becomes — 24 hours gives a mild tang, 48 hours is more pronounced.

steps.title: Strain and serve
steps.instruction: Once fermented to your liking, stir well and add water to thin to a drinkable consistency. Add sugar to taste if desired. Serve at room temperature or slightly chilled.
steps.tip: Incwancwa should be the consistency of a thin smoothie — pourable but with some body.

recipes.name: Injera
recipes.description: Ethiopia's signature sourdough flatbread made from teff flour — spongy, tangy, and used as both plate and utensil.
recipes.story: Injera is the foundation of Ethiopian cuisine, serving as plate, utensil, and flavor companion all at once. Made from teff, a tiny grain native to the Ethiopian highlands, the batter is fermented for days to achieve its signature sour tang. A meal without injera is not considered a meal in Ethiopia.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: sourdough starter
ingredients.prep_note: or reserved batter from previous batch

steps.title: Mix Batter
steps.instruction: Whisk teff flour and lukewarm water together until smooth with no lumps. Cover loosely with a cloth and leave at room temperature to ferment for 2-3 days.
steps.tip: The batter should bubble and develop a sour smell — that means fermentation is working.

steps.title: Make Absit
steps.instruction: On cooking day, mix half cup teff flour with one cup water in a small pot. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until it thickens into a paste. Let cool, then stir into the fermented batter.
steps.tip: Absit helps the injera develop its characteristic eye-holes on the surface.

steps.title: Adjust Batter
steps.instruction: The batter should be thin like crepe batter. Add water if too thick. Add salt and stir well. Pour off any liquid that accumulated on top during fermentation.

steps.title: Cook Injera
steps.instruction: Heat a large non-stick skillet or mitad over medium heat. Lightly oil. Pour batter in a spiral from outside to center. Cover and cook until eyes form on surface and edges lift from pan. Do not flip.
steps.tip: Only cook on one side — injera should be spongy on top and smooth on the bottom.

steps.title: Cool and Stack
steps.instruction: Carefully remove injera and place on a clean cloth to cool. Repeat with remaining batter, stacking cooled injera between layers of cloth.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Lay one large injera on a platter and spoon various wats and salads on top. Roll extra injera and serve alongside for tearing and scooping.
steps.tip: Fresh injera should be flexible enough to roll without cracking.

recipes.name: Kitfo
recipes.description: Ethiopian steak tartare — finely minced raw beef seasoned with mitmita spice and warm niter kibbeh, served with ayib cheese and gomen.
recipes.story: Kitfo originates from the Gurage people of southern Ethiopia and is considered one of the finest dishes in Ethiopian cuisine. Traditionally eaten raw (tere), it can also be served lightly warmed (leb leb) or fully cooked (yebesele). It is a festive dish often served at weddings and celebrations.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Beef
steps.instruction: Using a very sharp knife, mince the beef tenderloin as finely as possible. The meat should have an almost paste-like consistency.
steps.tip: Use the freshest, highest-quality beef you can find — this dish is traditionally served raw.

steps.title: Make Gomen
steps.instruction: Saute onion and garlic in oil until softened. Add collard greens and cook until tender but still green, about 8 minutes. Season with salt.

steps.title: Prepare Ayib
steps.instruction: Mix cottage cheese with lemon juice and a pinch of salt. Set aside at room temperature.

steps.title: Season Kitfo
steps.instruction: Warm niter kibbeh in a pan until just melted. In a bowl, combine minced beef, warm niter kibbeh, mitmita, cardamom, and salt. Mix gently with your hands until evenly combined.
steps.tip: For leb leb style, briefly warm the seasoned meat in a pan for 1-2 minutes — still pink inside.

steps.title: Plate and Serve
steps.instruction: Mound the kitfo in the center of a plate or injera. Place ayib on one side and gomen on the other. Serve immediately with extra injera for scooping.
steps.tip: Kitfo should be served immediately — it does not keep well once seasoned.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

recipes.name: Smoked Fish with Plantains
recipes.description: Flaked smoked fish stewed in a savoury tomato and onion sauce, served over golden fried plantains. A simple and satisfying Gabonese classic.
recipes.story: This dish reflects the abundant fishing culture of Gabon's coast and river systems. Smoked fish — preserved for longer shelf life in the tropical climate — is a pantry staple. Paired with sweet, ripe plantains and a quick tomato sauce, this meal comes together effortlessly and is a weeknight favourite across Libreville and beyond.
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed rice or on its own
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Fry the plantains
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Fry the plantain slices until golden brown on each side, about 3 minutes per side. Drain on paper towels and set aside.
steps.tip: Use ripe plantains — yellow with black spots — for natural sweetness.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: In the same pan, add a little more oil if needed. Sauté the sliced onion until soft. Add the chopped tomatoes and scotch bonnet, cooking for 10 minutes until the tomatoes break down into a sauce.

steps.title: Add the smoked fish
steps.instruction: Add the flaked smoked fish to the sauce and stir gently. Cook for 5 minutes to let the fish absorb the flavours of the sauce.
steps.tip: Be gentle when stirring to keep the fish in nice flakes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Arrange the fried plantains on a plate and spoon the smoked fish sauce over the top. Serve immediately.

recipes.name: Benachin
recipes.description: Gambia's one-pot rice dish — rice cooked in a rich tomato and vegetable sauce with fish or meat. Benachin means "one pot" in Wolof.
recipes.story: Benachin is the Gambian cousin of Senegalese thieboudienne. The name means "one pot" in Wolof, and the dish is a celebration of communal cooking where everything comes together in a single vessel.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Fry the fish
steps.instruction: Season fish and fry until browned on both sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Fry onions until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 5 minutes. Add 5 cups water and bring to a boil.

steps.title: Cook vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potato and cassava. Simmer 15 minutes until partly tender. Remove vegetables.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Add washed rice to the sauce. Arrange vegetables and fish on top. Cover tightly and cook on low 25-30 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not stir — let the rice absorb the sauce undisturbed.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve on a large platter with rice, fish, and vegetables arranged together.

recipes.name: Supakanja
recipes.description: A thick, slimy okra soup enriched with palm oil, dried fish, and seafood. The okra gives it a distinctive silky, mucilaginous texture.
recipes.story: Supakanja is the everyday okra soup of Gambia. The word "kanja" refers to okra, and this soup celebrates the vegetable in its full glory — the silky, slippery texture is considered a virtue, not a flaw.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the okra
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Add okra and fry, stirring, for 10 minutes until it becomes slimy and deep green.

steps.title: Add aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, scotch bonnets, and dried fish. Stir well and cook 5 minutes.

steps.title: Add liquid and shrimp
steps.instruction: Add 3 cups water and bring to a simmer. Add fresh shrimp and cook 15 minutes until the soup is thick and rich.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt and serve over white rice or with fufu.

recipes.name: Groundnut Soup
recipes.description: A rich, creamy soup from roasted groundnuts simmered with tomatoes and chilli. The natural peanut oils create a velvety broth.
recipes.story: Groundnut Soup is beloved comfort food across Ghana, particularly among the Ashanti people. It showcases the importance of groundnuts in West African cuisine.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, onion, and ginger. Boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve the stock.

steps.title: Prepare tomato base
steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes, scotch bonnet, and remaining onion. Cook until reduced, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Add groundnut paste
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in 2 cups reserved stock. Pour into the tomato base.
steps.tip: Mix peanut butter in stock first to prevent lumps.

steps.title: Simmer the soup
steps.instruction: Add cooked chicken and remaining stock. Simmer 20 minutes, stirring occasionally.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls with fufu or rice balls.

recipes.name: Kelewele
recipes.description: Spiced fried plantain cubes seasoned with ginger, chilli, and cloves. Crispy outside, sweet and soft inside — Ghana's favourite evening street snack.
recipes.story: Kelewele vendors are a common sight on Ghanaian streets at dusk. The combination of sweet ripe plantain with warming spices makes it irresistibly addictive.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season the plantain
steps.instruction: Combine plantain cubes with ginger, cayenne, cloves, nutmeg, and salt. Toss and let sit 10 minutes.
steps.tip: Plantains should be ripe with black spots.

steps.title: Heat the oil
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 180C. Test with a small piece — it should sizzle immediately.

steps.title: Fry the kelewele
steps.instruction: Fry in batches for 3-4 minutes per batch until golden brown and crispy.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Serve hot with roasted peanuts.
steps.tip: Best eaten immediately while still hot.

recipes.name: Fouti
recipes.description: Pounded fonio grain formed into a smooth, light ball and served with rich sauces. Fonio is an ancient grain native to West Africa, prized for its delicate flavour.
recipes.story: Fonio is one of the oldest cultivated grains in Africa, grown in the Fouta Djallon highlands of Guinea for thousands of years. The Fula people consider it the finest grain, reserving it for honoured guests.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Wash the fonio
steps.instruction: Rinse fonio in several changes of water until the water runs clear. Drain thoroughly.
steps.tip: Fonio is tiny — use a fine-mesh sieve.

steps.title: Cook the fonio
steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil with salt and butter. Add the fonio, stir once, cover, and reduce heat to low. Cook 5 minutes.

steps.title: Steam and fluff
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let steam covered for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork — grains should be separate and light.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Pack fonio into a wet bowl and turn out as a neat mound. Serve alongside sauces and stews.

recipes.name: Sauce Feuille
recipes.description: A hearty Guinean leaf sauce made with sweet potato leaves, palm oil, smoked fish, and groundnuts. Rich, nutritious, and deeply savory.
recipes.story: Sauce Feuille is the everyday sauce of Guinea, varying from region to region depending on which leaves are in season. Sweet potato leaves are the most common, valued for their mild flavour and nutritional density.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the leaves
steps.instruction: Boil chopped sweet potato leaves in salted water for 10 minutes. Drain and squeeze out excess water.

steps.title: Prepare the base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Fry onion until soft. Add the smoked fish and stir for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add peanuts and leaves
steps.instruction: Dissolve ground peanuts in 1 cup water and add to the pot. Add the cooked leaves and hot peppers. Stir well.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Simmer on low heat for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens and the flavours meld.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick enough to coat the leaves.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot over rice or with fouti.

recipes.name: Cashew Rice
recipes.description: Fragrant rice cooked with roasted cashew nuts, onions, and aromatic spices. A celebration of Guinea-Bissau's status as a major cashew producer.
recipes.story: Guinea-Bissau is one of the world's largest cashew producers, and cashews feature prominently in the cuisine. This rice dish is a simple yet flavourful showcase of the country's prized nut.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Toast the cashews
steps.instruction: Dry-toast cashew nuts in a pan over medium heat until golden and fragrant, about 5 minutes. Set aside.
steps.tip: Watch carefully — they can burn quickly.

steps.title: Cook the rice base
steps.instruction: Melt butter, fry onion until golden. Add rice and stir to coat. Pour in water or stock, season with salt.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, cover, reduce to low heat. Cook 18-20 minutes until rice is tender and liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Fluff rice with a fork, fold in toasted cashews. Serve as a side dish.
