recipes.name: Chabéu
recipes.description: Jollof-style rice from Guinea-Bissau, cooked in a palm oil and tomato base with dried fish and vegetables. The palm oil gives it a distinctive golden-red hue.
recipes.story: Chabéu is Guinea-Bissau's take on the West African one-pot rice tradition. The use of palm oil instead of vegetable oil distinguishes it from other jollof variations, giving it a unique colour and nutty flavour.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Fry the base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Fry onions until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes.

steps.title: Add fish and vegetables
steps.instruction: Add dried fish, eggplant, and 5 cups water. Bring to a boil and simmer 10 minutes.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Add washed rice. Stir once, cover tightly, and cook on low heat for 25-30 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not lift the lid during cooking.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fluff rice and serve with the fish and vegetables mixed through.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Alloco
recipes.description: Deep-fried ripe plantain slices served with a spicy onion and chilli sauce. The most popular street food snack across Ivory Coast.
recipes.story: Alloco vendors line the streets of Abidjan from late afternoon. The sweet, caramelised plantain paired with a fiery onion-tomato-chilli sauce is an irresistible combination that fuels the city.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Heat the oil
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pan to 180C. The oil should shimmer but not smoke.

steps.title: Fry the plantain
steps.instruction: Fry plantain slices in batches until deep golden brown on both sides, about 3-4 minutes per batch.
steps.tip: Use very ripe plantains — the skin should be mostly black.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: In a separate pan, sauté sliced onions until softened. Add diced tomatoes and chopped scotch bonnet. Cook 5 minutes until saucy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Pile fried plantain on a plate and spoon the spicy onion-tomato sauce over the top. Serve immediately.

recipes.name: Kenyan Pilau
recipes.description: Fragrant spiced rice with caramelized onions, pilau masala, and tender beef — a Swahili Coast masterpiece.
recipes.story: Pilau arrived on the Kenyan coast through centuries of trade with Arab, Indian, and Persian merchants. Every family has their own pilau masala blend and the dish is a centerpiece at weddings, Eid celebrations, and Sunday lunches. Deeply caramelized onions give it that signature dark color.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over high heat. Season beef with salt and sear until browned on all sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Caramelize Onions
steps.instruction: In the same pot, add sliced onions. Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden brown.
steps.tip: Do not rush — deeply browned onions give pilau its signature color and sweetness.

steps.title: Bloom Spices
steps.instruction: Add garlic, ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, cumin, and pilau masala. Stir 1 minute until fragrant. Return beef to pot.

steps.title: Cook Rice
steps.instruction: Add drained rice and stir to coat. Pour in hot stock. Bring to a boil, reduce to lowest heat, cover tightly and cook until rice is fluffy and liquid absorbed.
steps.tip: Place a clean towel under the lid to absorb excess steam for fluffier rice.

steps.title: Rest and Fluff
steps.instruction: Remove from heat, rest covered 10 minutes. Fluff gently with a fork.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound pilau on a platter, garnish with cilantro. Serve with kachumbari and lemon wedges.
steps.tip: Pilau is traditionally served at celebrations — make extra.

collections.name: Party Favorites
collections.description: Festive dishes that bring people together. These crowd-pleasers are served at celebrations across Africa.

recipes.name: Nyama Choma
recipes.description: Kenya's iconic grilled goat or beef marinated with salt and lemon, roasted over charcoal to smoky perfection.
recipes.story: Nyama Choma means "roasted meat" in Swahili and is more than food — it is a social ritual. Kenyans gather at open-air joints on weekends to share platters of smoky meat with cold Tusker beer. The best nyama choma is cooked slowly over hot coals with minimal seasoning.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate Meat
steps.instruction: Combine lemon juice, salt, pepper, garlic, ginger, and oil. Rub into goat pieces. Marinate at least 2 hours or overnight.
steps.tip: Overnight marination with lemon juice tenderizes goat meat.

steps.title: Prepare Charcoal
steps.instruction: Light charcoal grill and let coals burn until covered with white ash. Spread evenly for indirect heat.

steps.title: Grill Meat
steps.instruction: Place meat on grill over medium heat. Cook slowly, turning every 10 minutes, until deeply browned and cooked through.
steps.tip: Low and slow is the secret — resist high heat.

steps.title: Make Kachumbari
steps.instruction: Combine diced tomatoes, sliced onion, chili, cilantro, and lime juice. Season with salt and toss.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Remove meat from grill, rest 5 minutes, then chop into pieces on a wooden board. Serve with kachumbari and ugali.
steps.tip: In Kenya, nyama choma is always served on a wooden board with toothpicks.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Motoho
recipes.description: A tangy, slightly sour fermented sorghum porridge — Lesotho's traditional breakfast that warms the body against the cold highland mornings.
recipes.story: Motoho is the traditional breakfast porridge of the Basotho, made from fermented sorghum or maize. The fermentation gives it a distinctive sour tang that Basotho people love. In the mountain kingdom where winter temperatures drop well below freezing, a bowl of warm motoho is the perfect start to the day, providing sustained energy for long hours of work.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the base
steps.instruction: Mix the sorghum flour with 500ml of cold water to form a smooth paste. Bring the remaining 1 litre of water to a boil in a pot.

steps.title: Cook the porridge
steps.instruction: Gradually pour the sorghum paste into the boiling water, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes, stirring frequently.
steps.tip: Keep stirring to prevent the porridge from catching on the bottom of the pot.

steps.title: Add the malt
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let the porridge cool to lukewarm. Stir in the sorghum malt, mixing thoroughly. The malt will begin the fermentation process and thin the porridge slightly.
steps.tip: The porridge must be lukewarm, not hot, or the malt enzymes will be destroyed.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover the pot and leave at room temperature overnight or for at least 8 hours. The porridge will develop a pleasant sour tang.
steps.tip: In warmer weather, fermentation happens faster — check after 6 hours.

steps.title: Reheat and serve
steps.instruction: The next morning, gently reheat the motoho over low heat, stirring well. Add sugar or honey to taste. Serve warm in bowls, adding milk if desired.
steps.tip: The consistency should be pourable, like a thick smoothie — add water if too thick.

recipes.name: Nyekoe
recipes.description: A hearty Basotho bean and maize stew cooked together until thick and creamy — a protein-rich highland dish that fuels the people of the mountain kingdom.
recipes.story: Nyekoe is a simple but deeply satisfying combination of beans and maize kernels, slow-cooked until they merge into a thick, hearty stew. This dish has been a cornerstone of Basotho nutrition for centuries, providing essential protein and carbohydrates in a country where the harsh highland climate limits what can be grown. It is the kind of dish that grandmothers make and children remember forever.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Soak overnight
steps.instruction: Place the dried beans and samp in separate bowls, cover with water, and soak overnight or for at least 8 hours. Drain and rinse both.
steps.tip: Soaking significantly reduces cooking time and improves digestibility.

steps.title: Start cooking
steps.instruction: Place the drained samp in a large pot with 1.5 litres of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 30 minutes. Samp takes longer to cook than beans, so it gets a head start.

steps.title: Add beans
steps.instruction: Add the drained beans and chopped onion to the pot with the samp. Add more water if needed to keep everything covered. Simmer for another 45-60 minutes until both the samp and beans are completely tender.
steps.tip: Stir occasionally and add hot water as needed to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Mash and season
steps.instruction: Once everything is tender, use the back of a wooden spoon to roughly mash some of the beans against the side of the pot, creating a thick, creamy texture. Season with salt.
steps.tip: Do not mash everything — you want a mix of whole and broken beans for the best texture.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls and serve hot. Nyekoe can be eaten on its own or alongside moroho and fresh milk.
steps.tip: Leftovers thicken overnight and are even better the next day.

recipes.name: Liberian Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Liberia's distinctive jollof rice, often made with a mix of meats and seasoned with Liberian-style spice blends. Rich, tomatoey, and packed with protein.
recipes.story: Liberian Jollof has its own character, often incorporating more meats and a slightly sweeter tomato base. It is the centrepiece of every celebration and gathering.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the meats
steps.instruction: Season chicken and smoked turkey with salt, pepper, and onions. Boil until chicken is tender. Reserve stock.

steps.title: Fry the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry onions until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add blended tomatoes and cook until reduced.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Add rice and stir to coat. Pour in 4 cups meat stock. Add cooked meats. Cover tightly.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Cook on low heat for 30-35 minutes until rice is tender and has absorbed the sauce.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fluff and serve with fried plantain and pepper sauce.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Asida
recipes.description: A simple Libyan porridge-like dessert of wheat flour cooked into a smooth mound, served with a well of honey and melted butter in the centre. Sweet, warm, and ancient.
recipes.story: Asida is one of the oldest desserts in North Africa, served in Libya to celebrate the birth of a child, during Mawlid (the Prophet's birthday), and other joyful occasions. Its simplicity is its beauty — just flour, water, and butter, transformed through patient stirring into something comforting and celebratory. The crater of honey and butter in the centre is shared as everyone tears from the mound.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the water
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of water with a pinch of salt to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot.

steps.title: Add the flour
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to low. Gradually add the flour while stirring vigorously and continuously with a strong wooden spoon. Keep stirring for about 10 minutes until the mixture forms a thick, smooth, elastic dough that pulls away from the sides of the pot.
steps.tip: Stir with force — the dough needs to be completely lump-free and smooth.

steps.title: Shape the asida
steps.instruction: Wet a serving plate. Turn the asida out onto the plate and shape it into a smooth dome using a wet spoon or wet hands.
steps.tip: Wetting your tools prevents sticking.

steps.title: Create the well
steps.instruction: Make a deep well in the centre of the dome using the back of a spoon. Pour the melted butter into the well, then drizzle honey generously over the top.
steps.tip: The butter and honey pool should be generous — it is the soul of the dish.

steps.title: Serve communally
steps.instruction: Serve warm on a communal plate. Each person tears off a piece of the asida and dips it into the honey-butter well.
steps.tip: Asida is best eaten warm while the butter and honey are still flowing.

recipes.name: Sharba Libiya
recipes.description: A warming Libyan lamb and orzo soup spiced with cinnamon, turmeric, and mint. A deeply comforting bowl often served to break the Ramadan fast.
recipes.story: Sharba is the soup that greets every Libyan at the iftar table during Ramadan. Its fragrance fills Libyan kitchens in the late afternoon as families prepare for the evening meal. The combination of lamb, tomato, and small pasta with warm spices like cinnamon and mint is uniquely Libyan and instantly recognisable.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the lamb
steps.instruction: Heat 2 tbsp olive oil in a large pot. Add the lamb cubes and brown on all sides, about 5 minutes. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Add the onion to the pot and cook until soft. Stir in tomato paste, cinnamon, turmeric, dried mint, salt, and pepper. Cook for 2 minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer the soup
steps.instruction: Return the lamb to the pot. Add 6 cups of water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer for 40 minutes until the lamb is very tender.
steps.tip: Skim any foam for a clearer soup.

steps.title: Add the pasta
steps.instruction: Add the orzo to the soup and cook for 10-12 minutes until al dente. The soup should be slightly thick from the starch.
steps.tip: Add more water if the soup gets too thick — it should still be brothy.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.instruction: Stir in the fresh cilantro and a squeeze of lemon juice. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve hot in deep bowls.
steps.tip: A drizzle of olive oil on top adds richness.

recipes.name: Mofo Gasy
recipes.description: Traditional Malagasy rice flour pancakes cooked in special molds — crispy outside, soft inside, perfect for breakfast.
recipes.story: Mofo Gasy, meaning "Malagasy bread," is the quintessential Malagasy breakfast sold by street vendors every morning across the island. Cooked in special cast-iron molds called mofo gasy molds, these rice flour cakes have a distinctive crispy exterior and soft, slightly sweet interior. The sound of vendors calling "mofo gasy" is the alarm clock of Antananarivo.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Batter
steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast and sugar in lukewarm water. Let stand 5 minutes until foamy. Mix rice flour, coconut milk, salt, vanilla, and yeast mixture into a smooth batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover batter and let rest in a warm place for 1 hour until bubbly and slightly risen.
steps.tip: The fermentation gives mofo gasy its characteristic light tang.

steps.title: Heat Molds
steps.instruction: Heat a mofo gasy mold, aebleskiver pan, or mini muffin tin over medium heat. Brush each well generously with oil.
steps.tip: An aebleskiver pan is the closest substitute for a traditional mofo gasy mold.

steps.title: Cook Pancakes
steps.instruction: Fill each mold three-quarters full with batter. Cover and cook until bottoms are golden and tops are set.
steps.tip: Listen for a sizzle — if the oil is not hot enough, the mofo gasy will stick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Turn out onto a plate. Serve warm with coffee or tea for a traditional Malagasy breakfast.
steps.tip: Best eaten fresh and warm — mofo gasy lose their crispness as they cool.

recipes.name: Ravitoto
recipes.description: Pounded cassava leaves braised with pork and coconut milk — a rich, earthy Malagasy classic.
recipes.story: Ravitoto is a beloved Malagasy comfort dish made from pounded cassava leaves slow-cooked with fatty pork. The dish showcases the Malagasy genius for transforming humble ingredients into something deeply satisfying. It is often prepared for Sunday family gatherings and is considered one of the island's most traditional recipes.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Pork
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Brown pork belly pieces until golden and fat begins to render.
steps.tip: Rendering the pork fat first creates a flavorful base for the entire dish.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and ginger. Saute until softened. Add tomatoes and cook until broken down.

steps.title: Add Cassava Leaves
steps.instruction: Add pounded cassava leaves and water. Stir well to combine with the pork and aromatics.

steps.title: Braise
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer, stirring occasionally, until leaves are very tender and pork is falling apart.
steps.tip: Long, slow cooking is essential — cassava leaves need extended heat to become tender and safe to eat.

steps.title: Finish with Coconut
steps.instruction: Stir in coconut milk and cook uncovered for 10 more minutes until sauce thickens.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Serve hot over a generous mound of steamed white rice.
steps.tip: Ravitoto gets better as it sits — leftovers are even more flavorful the next day.

recipes.name: Romazava
recipes.description: Madagascar's national dish — a fragrant beef and mixed greens stew flavored with tomatoes, onions, and ginger.
recipes.story: Romazava is the national dish of Madagascar, eaten daily across the island. Its name means "clear broth" in Malagasy, referring to the light yet flavorful soup base. The combination of mixed greens (called bredes) with beef creates a nourishing everyday meal that reflects the island's unique blend of Southeast Asian and African culinary heritage.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Beef
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Brown beef cubes on all sides over high heat.

steps.title: Saute Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and ginger. Cook until softened. Add tomatoes and cook until they break down.

steps.title: Simmer Broth
steps.instruction: Add water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer until beef is tender.
steps.tip: The broth should remain relatively clear — this is a light stew, not a thick one.

steps.title: Add Greens
steps.instruction: Add mustard greens first and cook 5 minutes. Then add spinach and watercress. Cook until all greens are wilted.
steps.tip: Add hardier greens first, delicate ones last, so nothing is overcooked.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Ladle into bowls over steamed white rice. Serve with rougail (tomato chili condiment) on the side.
steps.tip: In Madagascar, romazava is always served with vary (rice) — the rice is the main event, the stew is the accompaniment.

recipes.name: Kondowole
recipes.description: Thick cassava porridge from northern Malawi — a starchy staple with a distinctive chewy texture.
recipes.story: Kondowole is the cassava-based staple of northern Malawi, where cassava grows abundantly. Unlike nsima, which is made from maize, kondowole has a distinctive sticky, chewy texture that northerners are deeply proud of. It is often the subject of friendly regional rivalries with maize-eating southerners.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Water
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of water to a boil. Add salt.

steps.title: Make Kondowole
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add cassava flour gradually, stirring vigorously. The mixture will become very thick and stretchy. Keep stirring until it forms a cohesive, elastic mass.
steps.tip: Kondowole requires more arm strength than nsima — the cassava makes it very sticky and resistant.

steps.title: Cook Greens
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onions until soft. Add tomatoes and cook down. Add greens and cook until wilted and tender.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Wet a plate and turn kondowole onto it. Shape into a dome with a wet spoon.
steps.tip: Wet everything — kondowole is much stickier than maize-based porridges.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve kondowole with the greens relish and dried fish if available.
steps.tip: Kondowole pairs especially well with dried fish (usipa) from Lake Malawi.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Mandasi
recipes.description: Malawian fried dough balls spiced with cardamom and coconut milk — a sweet breakfast treat or snack.
recipes.story: Mandasi are the East African answer to donuts — golden, puffy balls of fried dough found at every market and bus station in Malawi. Flavored with coconut milk and cardamom, they are the perfect breakfast paired with sweet tea. Their aroma wafting from roadside vendors is one of the defining scents of Malawian mornings.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, sugar, yeast, cardamom, nutmeg, and salt. Add warm coconut milk and oil. Knead into a soft, smooth dough.
steps.tip: The dough should be soft but not sticky — add flour if too wet.

steps.title: Rise
steps.instruction: Cover dough and let rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size.
steps.tip: Warm coconut milk activates the yeast faster for a better rise.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Punch down dough. Pull off golf ball-sized pieces and shape into balls or flatten into small discs.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C. Fry mandasi in batches, turning once, until golden brown on all sides. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the oil — fry 3-4 at a time for even cooking.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve warm with tea or coffee. Optionally dust with powdered sugar.
steps.tip: Mandasi are best eaten warm and fresh — they lose their puffiness as they cool.

recipes.name: Nsima with Chambo
recipes.description: Malawi's pride — firm maize porridge served with grilled chambo fish from Lake Malawi, the country's most prized catch.
recipes.story: Chambo is a tilapia species found only in Lake Malawi and is the national fish. Grilled whole over charcoal and served with nsima, it is the ultimate Malawian meal. Lake Malawi, the third largest lake in Africa, provides this cherished fish that is central to the country's identity and cuisine.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season Fish
steps.instruction: Score fish on both sides with deep diagonal cuts. Rub with lemon juice, garlic, salt, pepper, and oil. Let marinate 20 minutes.
steps.tip: Deep scores allow seasoning to penetrate and help the fish cook evenly.

steps.title: Grill Fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish over hot charcoal or broil in oven, turning once, until skin is crispy and flesh flakes easily.
steps.tip: Oil the grill grate first to prevent sticking — or use a fish grilling basket.

steps.title: Make Ndiwo
steps.instruction: Saute onions in oil until soft. Add tomatoes and cook into a thick relish. Season with salt.

steps.title: Cook Nsima
steps.instruction: Boil water, add maize flour gradually while stirring vigorously. Cook until thick and smooth, pulling away from sides.
steps.tip: Nsima should be smoother and slightly softer than Zambian nshima.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place nsima dome on plate with grilled chambo and tomato ndiwo alongside. Serve with a lemon wedge.
steps.tip: In Malawi, the fish head is considered the best part and is offered to the guest of honor.

recipes.name: Fakoye
recipes.description: A tangy okra and greens soup with dried fish and dawadawa (fermented locust beans). Deeply rooted in Malian tradition.
recipes.story: Fakoye is a beloved everyday soup of the Songhai people along the Niger River. The dawadawa gives it distinctive umami depth, while okra provides natural body.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Fry onions until softened. Add crumbled dawadawa and stir 1 minute.

steps.title: Add liquid and fish
steps.instruction: Add 4 cups water and dried fish. Boil and simmer 10 minutes.

steps.title: Add okra
steps.instruction: Add sliced okra, cook 10 minutes until silky.

steps.title: Add greens and finish
steps.instruction: Stir in greens, cook 3-5 minutes. Adjust salt and serve hot.

recipes.name: Toh
recipes.description: A firm, smooth millet porridge shaped into a mound and served as the base for sauces and stews. Mali's most fundamental staple.
recipes.story: Toh is the daily bread of Mali. Made from millet or sorghum flour, it is the foundation upon which most Malian meals are built.
recipes.best_served_with: Tigadegena, okra sauce, or any stew
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make a slurry
steps.instruction: Mix half the millet flour with 1 cup cold water to form a smooth paste.
steps.tip: Cold-water slurry prevents lumps.

steps.title: Boil the water
steps.instruction: Bring remaining 3 cups water to a rolling boil with salt.

steps.title: Add the slurry
steps.instruction: Pour slurry into boiling water, stirring constantly. Cook 5 minutes on medium heat.

steps.title: Add remaining flour
steps.instruction: Gradually add remaining flour, stirring vigorously. Mixture will become very thick.
steps.tip: Use a strong wooden spoon.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: When toh pulls from pot sides, wet a bowl, scoop in, smooth top, turn out as a mound.
steps.tip: Serve immediately — toh hardens as it cools.

recipes.name: Lakh
recipes.description: A sweet millet couscous porridge served with a fermented milk and sugar sauce. Mauritania's traditional dessert and breakfast, creamy and nourishing.
recipes.story: Lakh is one of the few sweet dishes in the Sahelian culinary tradition. The combination of steamed millet couscous with cool, tangy fermented milk is both refreshing and sustaining, making it popular as both a dessert and a breakfast.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.instruction: Steam millet couscous in a steamer basket over boiling water for 15 minutes until fluffy and tender.

steps.title: Enrich the couscous
steps.instruction: Toss the hot couscous with butter and a pinch of salt. Fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: Whisk fermented milk with sugar and vanilla until smooth and pourable.
steps.tip: The sauce should be cool — the contrast with warm couscous is the magic.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound couscous in bowls and pour the sweet milk sauce generously over the top. Serve immediately.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Mechoui
recipes.description: Slow-roasted whole lamb seasoned with cumin, coriander, and butter, cooked over embers until the meat falls off the bone. Mauritania's ultimate celebration dish.
recipes.story: Mechoui is reserved for the most important celebrations — weddings, religious holidays, and the arrival of honoured guests. In Moor culture, the ability to prepare a perfect mechoui is a mark of great hospitality.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the rub
steps.instruction: Mix softened butter with cumin, coriander, garlic, paprika, salt, and pepper into a paste.

steps.title: Prepare the lamb
steps.instruction: Score the lamb deeply. Rub the butter-spice paste into every crevice, under the skin and into the scores. Let marinate at least 2 hours.
steps.tip: Overnight marination is ideal.

steps.title: Slow roast
steps.instruction: Place in a roasting pan. Cover with foil. Roast at 150C for 3 hours, basting every 30 minutes with pan juices.
steps.tip: Low and slow is the key — the meat should fall off the bone.

steps.title: Crisp the exterior
steps.instruction: Remove foil. Increase oven to 220C and roast 15-20 minutes until the exterior is golden and crispy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Rest 15 minutes before carving. Serve on a large platter with couscous and bread.

recipes.name: Dholl Puri
recipes.description: Mauritius's beloved street food — soft flatbread stuffed with ground yellow split peas, served with curry and chutneys.
recipes.story: Dholl Puri is the undisputed king of Mauritian street food, with origins in the Indian indentured laborers who came to the island in the 19th century. Today it transcends all ethnic boundaries — every Mauritian, regardless of background, grows up eating dholl puri from roadside vendors. It is the great unifier of this multicultural island nation.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook Split Peas
steps.instruction: Boil soaked split peas until very soft. Drain well. Grind to a coarse powder with cumin, turmeric, and salt.
steps.tip: The split peas must be completely dry before grinding — excess moisture makes the dough soggy.

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, salt, oil, and water into a soft dough. Knead 5 minutes. Rest 30 minutes covered.

steps.title: Stuff
steps.instruction: Divide dough into balls. Flatten each, place a spoonful of dholl filling in center, fold edges over, and seal. Gently roll out into a thin circle.
steps.tip: Roll gently to avoid the filling breaking through — if it does, pinch it closed and try again.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Cook on a hot dry tawa or griddle until golden spots appear on both sides. Brush lightly with oil.
steps.tip: The puri should puff slightly and have golden-brown spots — do not overcook or it becomes crispy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place puri on paper, spread with curry, add coriander chutney and chili paste. Fold and eat with hands.
steps.tip: The authentic Mauritian way: butter bean curry, coriander chutney, and hot chili — all in one fold.

recipes.name: Gateau Piment
recipes.description: Mauritius's crispy chili lentil fritters — golden outside, soft inside, with a kick of green chili and cumin.
recipes.story: Gateau piment literally means "chili cake" in Mauritian Creole, and these addictive lentil fritters are found at every street corner on the island. Made from soaked yellow split peas ground with green chilies, cumin, and fresh herbs, they are deep-fried to golden perfection. They are Mauritius's answer to falafel and are typically tucked inside a buttered baguette.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Grind Peas
steps.instruction: Drain soaked split peas. Grind in a food processor to a coarse paste — not too smooth, some texture should remain.
steps.tip: Do not add water — the paste should be thick enough to shape. Some coarse bits add crunch.

steps.title: Mix
steps.instruction: Add chilies, onion, garlic, cumin, turmeric, cilantro, spring onions, salt, and baking soda to the paste. Mix well.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: With wet hands, form small balls slightly smaller than a golf ball.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in water frequently — the paste is sticky.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C. Fry fritters in batches until deep golden brown and crispy on the outside.
steps.tip: Fry on medium heat so the inside cooks through before the outside burns.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Serve hot, either plain as a snack or stuffed inside a buttered baguette.
steps.tip: The classic Mauritian way: split a baguette, butter it, stuff with gateau piment, and add chili sauce.

recipes.name: Rougaille
recipes.description: Mauritian sausage simmered in a spiced tomato sauce with thyme, ginger, and chili — the island's comfort classic.
recipes.story: Rougaille is the cornerstone of Mauritian Creole home cooking, a rich tomato-based sauce that can be made with sausages, fish, or salted fish. The sausage version (rougaille saucisse) is the most popular weeknight dinner on the island. Its French name betrays the colonial influence, but the bold use of ginger, chili, and thyme is purely Mauritian.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Sausages
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Brown sausage pieces on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: In the same pot, saute onions until translucent. Add garlic, ginger, and thyme. Cook 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add Tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add chopped tomatoes and whole scotch bonnet. Cook, stirring occasionally, until tomatoes completely break down into a thick sauce.
steps.tip: Crush the tomatoes with your spoon to help them break down faster.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Return sausages to the pot. Stir to coat in sauce. Cover and simmer until sauce is rich and thick.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick and coating, not watery — reduce uncovered if needed.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove scotch bonnet and thyme sprigs. Stir in parsley. Serve over steamed basmati rice.
steps.tip: Rougaille is even better the next day — make extra and reheat for lunch.

recipes.name: Harira
recipes.description: A hearty Moroccan tomato and lentil soup thickened with flour and brightened with lemon juice, herbs, and warming spices. A staple during Ramadan to break the fast.
recipes.story: Harira is the soul of Moroccan Ramadan. As the call to prayer sounds at sunset, families across the country break their fast with a steaming bowl of this nourishing soup, often accompanied by dates, chebakia pastries, and hard-boiled eggs. Each family has their own recipe, passed down through generations, but the tomato-lentil-chickpea base remains constant.
recipes.best_served_with: Dates, chebakia pastries, and crusty bread
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: In a large pot, brown the lamb cubes in a drizzle of olive oil over medium-high heat until golden on all sides, about 5 minutes.

steps.title: Sautee aromatics
steps.instruction: Add the onion and cook until softened, about 4 minutes. Stir in 1 tsp each of ground ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, and black pepper.

steps.title: Simmer the soup
steps.instruction: Add the crushed tomatoes, lentils, soaked chickpeas, and 8 cups of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer. Cover and cook for 50 minutes until the lentils and chickpeas are tender.
steps.tip: If using canned chickpeas, add them in the last 15 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken the soup
steps.instruction: Stir the flour-water mixture into the soup to thicken it. Add the cilantro and parsley. Simmer for another 10 minutes, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: The soup should coat the back of a spoon.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.instruction: Squeeze in the juice of one lemon, taste and adjust salt. Ladle into bowls and serve immediately with dates and bread.
steps.tip: A squeeze of fresh lemon just before serving brightens the whole bowl.

recipes.name: Msemen
recipes.description: Crispy, flaky Moroccan square flatbread made by folding layers of semolina dough with butter. Golden and crunchy on the outside, soft and chewy within.
recipes.story: Msemen is a morning ritual across Morocco, sold on every street corner alongside mint tea. Mothers teach daughters the folding technique, a rhythmic motion of stretching, buttering, and folding the dough into its signature square shape. Whether enjoyed plain with honey or stuffed with a spiced onion-tomato filling, msemen represents the warmth of a Moroccan breakfast.
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, honey, and soft cheese
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Combine the flour, semolina, salt, sugar, and yeast. Gradually add warm water and knead for 10 minutes until you have a smooth, slightly sticky dough.
steps.tip: The dough should be softer than bread dough — slightly sticky is perfect.

steps.title: Rest the dough
steps.instruction: Divide the dough into 8 equal balls. Oil each ball lightly and place on an oiled surface. Cover and let rest for 20 minutes.

steps.title: Shape the msemen
steps.instruction: Take a ball and flatten it on an oiled surface, stretching it as thin as possible with your fingers. Spread softened butter across the surface. Fold the left and right edges to the centre, then fold the top and bottom edges to form a square. Repeat for all balls.
steps.tip: Work on an oiled surface to prevent sticking. The thinner you stretch, the flakier the layers.

steps.title: Cook on griddle
steps.instruction: Heat a flat griddle or skillet over medium heat. Place a msemen square on the dry griddle and cook for 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown and crispy.
steps.tip: Press gently with a spatula while cooking to ensure even browning.

steps.title: Serve warm
steps.instruction: Serve the msemen warm, drizzled with honey or alongside soft cheese and mint tea.
steps.tip: Msemen are best eaten immediately while the layers are still crispy.

recipes.name: Caril de Camarao
recipes.description: Mozambican prawn curry simmered in coconut milk with tomatoes and warm spices — a coastal treasure.
recipes.story: Caril de Camarao reflects Mozambique's position at the crossroads of African, Portuguese, and Indian Ocean culinary traditions. The warm waters of the Mozambique Channel yield some of the world's finest prawns, and local cooks honor them with this lightly spiced coconut curry that lets the sweetness of the seafood shine through.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Saute Aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a wide pan. Saute onion, garlic, and ginger until soft and fragrant.

steps.title: Build Sauce
steps.instruction: Add curry powder, turmeric, and chili. Stir for 30 seconds. Add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce.
steps.tip: Toast the spices briefly before adding tomatoes to deepen their flavor.

steps.title: Add Coconut Milk
steps.instruction: Pour in coconut milk and stir well. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 5 minutes to meld flavors.

steps.title: Cook Prawns
steps.instruction: Add prawns to the sauce in a single layer. Cook for 2-3 minutes per side until pink and curled. Do not overcook.
steps.tip: Prawns cook quickly — remove from heat as soon as they turn pink to keep them tender.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Garnish with fresh cilantro and lime wedges. Serve over steamed rice or with crusty bread to soak up the sauce.
steps.tip: This curry is best served immediately — prawns toughen if left sitting in hot sauce.

recipes.name: Matapa
recipes.description: Creamy Mozambican stew of cassava leaves pounded with ground peanuts, coconut milk, and garlic.
recipes.story: Matapa is a cornerstone of Mozambican cuisine from the southern provinces. Made from young cassava leaves pounded with ground peanuts and simmered in coconut milk, it represents the ingenious use of local ingredients. Traditionally served with rice and shrimp, it is a dish that connects Mozambicans to their agricultural roots.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook Leaves
steps.instruction: Boil cassava leaves in water for 20 minutes until tender. Drain well.
steps.tip: Cassava leaves require thorough cooking — boiling removes natural compounds and makes them safe and delicious.

steps.title: Saute Aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a pot. Saute onion and garlic until soft and golden.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add cooked cassava leaves, ground peanuts, and water. Stir well to combine. Simmer for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Add Coconut Milk
steps.instruction: Pour in coconut milk and stir until the stew is creamy and thick. If adding shrimp, add them now and cook until pink.
steps.tip: Stir frequently to prevent the peanut mixture from sticking to the bottom.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt and lemon juice. Serve matapa over steamed white rice.
steps.tip: Matapa pairs beautifully with grilled prawns for a Mozambican feast.

recipes.name: Piri Piri Chicken
recipes.description: Mozambique's fiery grilled chicken marinated in piri piri chili sauce with citrus, garlic, and paprika.
recipes.story: Piri piri chicken is Mozambique's gift to the world, born from the marriage of African bird's eye chilies and Portuguese colonial cooking techniques. The small but fiery piri piri pepper is native to Mozambique, and local cooks have perfected the art of balancing its heat with citrus and garlic into a marinade that has become globally famous.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Piri Piri Sauce
steps.instruction: Blend piri piri chilies, garlic, lemon juice, roasted pepper, paprika, oregano, vinegar, oil, and salt into a smooth sauce.
steps.tip: Wear gloves when handling piri piri chilies — they are extremely hot.

steps.title: Marinate Chicken
steps.instruction: Score the chicken deeply on both sides. Rub two-thirds of the sauce all over and under the skin. Reserve remaining sauce. Marinate at least 4 hours or overnight.
steps.tip: Deep scores let the marinade penetrate — the longer the marination, the more flavorful.

steps.title: Grill Chicken
steps.instruction: Grill chicken over medium-hot charcoal, skin side down first, basting with reserved sauce every 10 minutes. Turn occasionally.
steps.tip: Start skin side down to render fat and get crispy skin.

steps.title: Check Doneness
steps.instruction: Chicken is done when juices run clear when pierced at the thigh and internal temperature reaches 75C. Rest for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Cut into pieces and squeeze fresh lime over the top. Serve with rice, fries, or matapa on the side.
steps.tip: Serve extra piri piri sauce on the side for those who like more heat.

recipes.name: Kapana
recipes.description: Namibia's iconic street food — beef strips grilled over open coals and tossed with a fresh chilli-tomato salsa, sold at bustling open-air markets.
recipes.story: Kapana is the heartbeat of Namibian street food culture, most famously found at the Kapana market in Windhoek's Katutura township. Vendors grill strips of fresh beef on open-air drum grills, and customers choose their toppings from an array of fresh chilli salsas. It brings communities together daily around the smoke and sizzle of the grill.
recipes.best_served_with: Fresh bread rolls and chilli salsa
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the salsa
steps.instruction: Combine the diced tomatoes, onion, green chillies, and vinegar in a bowl. Season with salt and mix well. Set aside to let the flavors meld.
steps.tip: Make the salsa first so it has time to develop flavor while you grill the meat.

steps.title: Prepare the beef
steps.instruction: Rub the beef strips with oil and season generously with salt. Let them come to room temperature for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Grill the meat
steps.instruction: Place the beef strips on a very hot grill or cast-iron pan. Cook for 2-3 minutes per side for medium, getting a good char on the outside while keeping the inside juicy.
steps.tip: The grill or pan must be smoking hot for the best charred crust.

steps.title: Slice and toss
steps.instruction: Transfer the grilled beef to a cutting board. Slice into bite-sized pieces and toss with a generous spoonful of the fresh chilli salsa.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Pile the kapana onto plates or into bread rolls. Serve immediately with extra salsa on the side.
steps.tip: At the market, kapana is served on newspaper or brown paper — keep it casual.

recipes.name: Oshifima
recipes.description: A smooth, firm porridge made from pearl millet flour — the staple starch of northern Namibia, served with stews, greens, or grilled meat.
recipes.story: Oshifima is the daily bread of the Owambo people in northern Namibia, where pearl millet (mahangu) has been the principal crop for centuries. The dish is prepared by stirring millet flour into boiling water until it forms a thick, smooth mass. It is eaten with the hands — a small piece is pinched off, shaped into a scoop, and used to pick up stew or vegetables.
recipes.best_served_with: Oshikundu (fermented drink), stews, or grilled meat
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring the water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add salt if using.

steps.title: Add millet flour gradually
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Sprinkle in about a third of the millet flour while stirring constantly with a strong wooden spoon or oshini (wooden paddle) to prevent lumps.
steps.tip: A flat wooden paddle gives you more leverage for stirring the thick porridge.

steps.title: Build thickness
steps.instruction: Continue adding the millet flour in batches, stirring vigorously after each addition. The mixture will become increasingly thick and heavy. Keep stirring to work out any lumps.
steps.tip: This requires real arm strength — the thicker it gets, the harder you stir.

steps.title: Cook through
steps.instruction: Once all the flour is incorporated and the mixture pulls away from the sides of the pot, reduce heat to low. Cover and steam for 5-8 minutes.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a wooden spoon and shape the oshifima into a smooth mound. Turn out onto a plate. Serve alongside stew, greens, or grilled meat.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in water when eating to prevent the oshifima from sticking to your fingers.

recipes.name: Kilishi
recipes.description: Paper-thin dried beef jerky coated in a spicy groundnut and chilli paste, then sun-dried until crispy. Niger's premium dried meat snack.
recipes.story: Kilishi is the gourmet beef jerky of the Sahel, perfected by the Hausa people. The process of slicing beef paper-thin, coating it in a spiced groundnut paste, and drying it in the sun requires great skill. It is a prized gift and travel food.
recipes.best_served_with: On its own as a snack
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Slice the beef
steps.instruction: Using a very sharp knife, slice the beef into paper-thin sheets, as large as possible. The thinner the better.
steps.tip: Partially freeze the beef for 30 minutes to make slicing easier.

steps.title: First drying
steps.instruction: Lay the beef slices on wire racks in the sun or in an oven set to 70C. Dry until firm but still pliable, about 3-4 hours in sun or 2 hours in oven.

steps.title: Make the labu paste
steps.instruction: Combine groundnut paste, cayenne, ginger, onion powder, seasoning cubes, and enough water to make a smooth, spreadable paste.
steps.tip: The paste should be thick enough to coat the meat without dripping.

steps.title: Coat and re-dry
steps.instruction: Coat each dried beef slice generously with the labu paste on both sides. Return to the racks and dry again for 2-3 hours until crispy.

steps.title: Quick grill
steps.instruction: Briefly grill each piece over low heat for 1-2 minutes per side to toast the coating. Store in airtight containers.
steps.tip: Kilishi keeps for weeks at room temperature when properly dried.

recipes.name: Tuwo Masara
recipes.description: A smooth, firm maize pudding served as the starchy base for soups and stews across Niger. Made by stirring maize flour into boiling water until thick.
recipes.story: Tuwo Masara is Niger's answer to the universal West African swallow. Made from locally grown maize, it provides the caloric foundation for the hearty stews and soups of the Sahel region.
recipes.best_served_with: Miyan kuka (baobab soup) or any stew
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make slurry
steps.instruction: Mix 1 cup maize flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth.

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring remaining 3 cups water to a boil with salt.

steps.title: Cook the tuwo
steps.instruction: Pour slurry into boiling water, stirring vigorously. Cook on medium heat for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: Gradually add remaining maize flour, stirring hard. The mixture will become very thick and pull away from the pot. Cook 10 more minutes.
steps.tip: Consistent stirring prevents lumps.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl, scoop tuwo in, and shape into a smooth mound. Turn out onto a plate and serve with soup.

recipes.name: Egusi Soup
recipes.description: A thick, hearty soup made from ground melon seeds, leafy greens, and assorted meats. The egusi forms satisfying clumps in a rich, peppery palm oil broth.
recipes.story: Egusi Soup is a staple across southern Nigeria. The Igbo lumpy style uses balls of egusi paste, while the Yoruba style fries it smooth. It is always served with a swallow like pounded yam.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the meats
steps.instruction: Season beef and tripe with onions, seasoning cubes, and salt. Boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve the stock.
steps.tip: The meat stock is the flavour backbone of the soup.

steps.title: Prepare egusi paste
steps.instruction: Mix ground egusi with a small amount of warm water to form a thick paste.

steps.title: Build the soup base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a pot. Add chopped onions and fry until softened. Add blended peppers and cook for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Add egusi
steps.instruction: Scoop egusi paste in spoonfuls into the pot forming lumps. Add reserved meat stock and stir gently. Cover and simmer for 15 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not stir too much — let the egusi clumps set.

steps.title: Add meats and greens
steps.instruction: Add cooked meats and stockfish. Stir gently and cook for 5 minutes. Add chopped greens and cook 3-5 minutes until wilted.
steps.tip: Add greens last so they keep their colour.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning and serve hot alongside pounded yam or any preferred swallow.

recipes.name: Suya
recipes.description: Thinly sliced beef skewers coated in a fiery, nutty spice blend called yaji, grilled over open charcoal until charred and smoky.
recipes.story: Suya is the pride of the Hausa people of northern Nigeria. As evening falls across Nigerian cities, the glow of suya grills and the aroma of smoking yaji spice fill the air.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the yaji spice
steps.instruction: Combine groundnut powder, cayenne, ginger, onion powder, paprika, and salt. Mix thoroughly.
steps.tip: Toast the groundnuts in a dry pan first for deeper flavour.

steps.title: Prepare the meat
steps.instruction: Thread thinly sliced beef onto wooden skewers. Brush with oil and coat generously with yaji spice on all sides.
steps.tip: Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes to prevent burning.

steps.title: Marinate
steps.instruction: Let coated skewers rest for at least 30 minutes, or refrigerate up to 2 hours.

steps.title: Grill the suya
steps.instruction: Grill over high charcoal heat, turning every 2-3 minutes, until charred on edges but juicy inside. About 8-10 minutes total.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Sprinkle extra yaji spice over the grilled suya. Serve with sliced onions, tomatoes, and fresh cabbage.

recipes.name: Mbisi ye Kalou
recipes.description: Fresh river fish grilled over charcoal and served with a fiery pili-pili sauce. A simple but spectacular dish from the banks of the Congo River.
recipes.story: Along the Congo River, fishing is a way of life. Freshly caught tilapia or capitaine are scored, seasoned simply, and grilled over wood charcoal until the skin is crispy and the flesh flakes easily. The pili-pili sauce served alongside is the real star — a fiery blend of fresh chillies, lemon, and onion that cuts through the richness of the fish.
recipes.best_served_with: Boiled plantains, fried plantains, or fufu with a green salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: tilapia
ingredients.prep_note: cleaned and scored on both sides

steps.title: Prepare the fish
steps.instruction: Rinse the tilapia and pat dry. Make 3-4 diagonal slashes on each side. Rub with oil and season generously with salt inside and out.
steps.tip: Scoring helps the heat penetrate evenly and lets the seasoning flavour the flesh.

steps.title: Make the pili-pili sauce
steps.instruction: Combine the chopped chillies, diced onion, lemon juice, oil, and a pinch of salt in a bowl. Mix well and set aside to let the flavours meld.
steps.tip: Make this at least 15 minutes ahead for the best flavour.

steps.title: Grill the fish
steps.instruction: Heat a charcoal grill or grill pan to high heat. Grill the fish for 8-10 minutes per side until the skin is charred and crispy and the flesh is opaque and flakes easily.
steps.tip: Oil the grill grate well to prevent sticking. Do not move the fish until it releases naturally.

steps.title: Flip and finish
steps.instruction: Carefully flip the fish and grill the other side for another 8 minutes until cooked through.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Transfer the grilled fish to a platter and spoon the pili-pili sauce generously over the top. Serve immediately with boiled or fried plantains.

recipes.name: Saka-Saka au Poisson Fumé
recipes.description: Cassava leaves slow-cooked with smoked fish, palm oil, and peanut butter, creating a deeply savoury and nutritious green stew beloved across the Republic of Congo.
recipes.story: The Brazzaville version of saka-saka distinguishes itself with the addition of peanut butter, which rounds out the bitterness of the cassava leaves and adds creaminess. Smoked fish from the Congo River provides a deep umami backbone. This is a dish of sustenance and community, often prepared in large batches and shared among neighbours.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: cassava leaves
ingredients.prep_note: frozen and thawed, or fresh and pounded

steps.title: Cook the cassava leaves
steps.instruction: Place cassava leaves in a large pot with 2 cups of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook for 30 minutes until softened.
steps.tip: Fresh leaves need longer — up to 60 minutes. Frozen leaves are already partially cooked.

steps.title: Prepare the base
steps.instruction: In a separate pan, heat palm oil and sauté the onion until golden. Add garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add peanut butter and stir until melted and combined.

steps.title: Combine everything
steps.instruction: Add the onion-peanut mixture and flaked smoked fish to the cassava leaves. Crumble in the stock cube. Stir well to combine.

steps.title: Slow-cook
steps.instruction: Cover and cook on low heat for 40-45 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes to prevent sticking. The dish is ready when the leaves are very tender and the sauce has thickened.
steps.tip: The peanut butter will thicken the sauce as it cooks — add water if too thick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste and adjust salt. Serve hot alongside fufu or chikwangue.

recipes.name: Igisafuria
recipes.description: A hearty Rwandan one-pot stew of plantains, vegetables, and meat simmered until everything melds together.
recipes.story: Igisafuria literally means "the pot" in Kinyarwanda, reflecting its one-pot cooking method. This practical and nourishing dish was born from the Rwandan tradition of combining whatever ingredients are available into a single pot. Every family has their own version, making it both a unifying and deeply personal dish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Season beef with salt and sear until browned on all sides.

steps.title: Build Stew
steps.instruction: Add onions and garlic, cook until softened. Add tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add plantains, potatoes, carrots, green beans, and water. Drop in the whole scotch bonnet. Bring to a boil.
steps.tip: Keep the scotch bonnet whole for gentle heat — piercing it will make the stew very spicy.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Reduce heat, cover, and simmer until all vegetables are tender and the stew has thickened naturally from the plantains breaking down.
steps.tip: The plantains will partially dissolve and thicken the stew — this is desired.

steps.title: Season and Serve
steps.instruction: Remove scotch bonnet. Adjust salt to taste. Serve hot in deep bowls — this is a complete meal in itself.
steps.tip: Igisafuria improves with reheating the next day as flavors deepen overnight.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Isombe
recipes.description: Rwandan cassava leaf stew pounded smooth and simmered with palm oil, eggplant, and spinach.
recipes.story: Isombe is one of Rwanda's most cherished dishes, made from pounded cassava leaves simmered until silky smooth. It is a staple across all regions and a source of deep nutritional value. Rwandan grandmothers say the secret is in the pounding — the leaves must be ground to a fine paste for the best texture.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Leaves
steps.instruction: If using fresh cassava leaves, pound them in a mortar until they form a fine green paste. If using frozen pre-pounded leaves, thaw completely.
steps.tip: Cassava leaves must be cooked thoroughly — never eat them raw as they contain naturally occurring compounds that break down with heat.

steps.title: Saute Base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a heavy pot. Saute onions and garlic until soft and fragrant.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the eggplant and cook until softened. Add the pounded cassava leaves and water. Stir well to combine.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Add scotch bonnet whole (for mild heat). Simmer, stirring occasionally, until leaves are very tender and sauce is thick.
steps.tip: The longer you simmer, the smoother and more flavorful the isombe becomes.

steps.title: Add Spinach
steps.instruction: Stir in chopped spinach and cook for 5 more minutes until wilted. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the whole scotch bonnet. Serve isombe with boiled plantains, rice, or ubugari (maize porridge).
steps.tip: Isombe tastes even better the next day after the flavors have melded.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Rwandan Brochettes
recipes.description: Charcoal-grilled goat meat skewers marinated with garlic and served with fried plantains — Rwanda's favorite barbecue.
recipes.story: Brochettes are the social food of Rwanda, found at every bar, restaurant, and street corner in Kigali. Goat is the most traditional and popular meat, skewered and grilled over charcoal while friends share Primus beers. The simplicity of the seasoning lets the quality of the meat speak for itself.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate Meat
steps.instruction: Combine garlic, ginger, oil, lemon juice, salt, pepper, and cumin. Toss goat cubes in marinade. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
steps.tip: Goat benefits from longer marination — overnight gives the best flavor.

steps.title: Skewer
steps.instruction: Thread marinated meat onto metal or soaked wooden skewers, leaving small gaps between pieces for even cooking.

steps.title: Grill
steps.instruction: Grill skewers over hot charcoal, turning every 3-4 minutes, until charred on the outside and cooked through.
steps.tip: Keep a spray bottle of water nearby to control flare-ups from dripping fat.

steps.title: Fry Plantains
steps.instruction: While meat grills, slice plantains and fry in oil until golden on both sides. Drain on paper towels.

steps.title: Make Fresh Sauce
steps.instruction: Mix diced tomatoes and onions with a pinch of salt and squeeze of lemon for a simple fresh condiment.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Arrange skewers on a platter with fried plantains and fresh tomato-onion sauce. Serve hot off the grill.
steps.tip: In Kigali, brochettes are always served with toothpicks and cold beer.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Angú de Banana
recipes.description: Mashed green banana with palm oil, creating a smooth, savoury side dish. The traditional accompaniment to calulu and other Santomean stews.
recipes.story: Angú de banana is as fundamental to Santomean cuisine as rice is to much of West Africa. The green bananas are boiled and mashed with palm oil to create a smooth, starchy base that perfectly absorbs rich stews.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the bananas
steps.instruction: Place peeled green bananas in salted boiling water. Cook until very soft, about 15-20 minutes.
steps.tip: They should be completely soft — undercooked bananas won't mash smoothly.

steps.title: Mash
steps.instruction: Drain the bananas. Mash with a potato masher or wooden pestle until completely smooth with no lumps.

steps.title: Add palm oil
steps.instruction: Stir in palm oil and salt. Mix until the angú is smooth, golden, and uniform.
steps.tip: The palm oil gives it a beautiful golden colour and rich flavour.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape into a mound and serve alongside calulu or other stews.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Chicken Yassa
recipes.description: Grilled chicken smothered in a tangy caramelised onion and lemon sauce. The sharp citrus and sweet onion combination is distinctly Senegalese.
recipes.story: Yassa originates from the Casamance region in southern Senegal. It was traditionally prepared for celebrations and has become one of Senegal's most popular dishes internationally.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice and a simple salad
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Combine chicken with lemon juice, half the onions, mustard, garlic, salt, and pepper. Refrigerate at least 2 hours.
steps.tip: Overnight marination is best.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.instruction: Remove chicken from marinade (reserve it). Grill until golden and slightly charred, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.instruction: Heat oil. Add all onions and cook on medium-low until deeply caramelised, about 25 minutes.
steps.tip: Low and slow is the key.

steps.title: Combine and simmer
steps.instruction: Add grilled chicken, reserved marinade, and whole scotch bonnet. Cover and simmer 20 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve chicken smothered in onion sauce over fluffy white rice.

recipes.name: Mafe
recipes.description: A hearty groundnut stew with tender meat and root vegetables in a thick, creamy peanut and tomato sauce.
recipes.story: Mafe is a Mandinka dish now a staple across Senegal and West Africa. The creamy groundnut sauce celebrates the peanut, one of the region's most important crops.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil. Season beef and brown on all sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Fry onions until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add garlic and ginger.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in 3 cups warm water. Pour into pot with browned meat. Simmer.
steps.tip: Dissolve fully to avoid lumps.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potatoes, carrots. Simmer until tender and sauce is thick, 35-40 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle over fluffy white rice.

recipes.name: Grilled Fish Creole
recipes.description: Seychellois grilled red snapper topped with a vibrant Creole sauce of tomatoes, ginger, chili, and fresh herbs.
recipes.story: Fish is the lifeblood of Seychellois cuisine, and this Creole-style preparation is the islands' most iconic dish. The Creole sauce — a zingy mix of tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and chili — is spooned over perfectly grilled fresh-caught fish. With 115 islands surrounded by the Indian Ocean, every Seychellois family has a fisherman and a Creole sauce recipe.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season Fish
steps.instruction: Rub fish fillets with olive oil, lemon juice, and salt. Let rest 15 minutes.

steps.title: Make Creole Sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onion, garlic, and ginger until fragrant. Add tomatoes, scotch bonnet, and thyme. Cook until tomatoes break down into a chunky sauce.
steps.tip: The sauce should be chunky, not smooth — the texture is part of the Creole style.

steps.title: Grill Fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish fillets over high heat or in a hot pan for 3-4 minutes per side until skin is crispy and flesh flakes easily.
steps.tip: Do not move the fish too early — let the skin crisp and release naturally from the grill.

steps.title: Finish Sauce
steps.instruction: Stir parsley into the Creole sauce. Season with salt to taste.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place grilled fish on plates and spoon Creole sauce generously over the top. Serve with rice and fried plantains.
steps.tip: In Seychelles, this dish is enjoyed with your feet in the sand and the ocean in view.

recipes.name: Ladob
recipes.description: Seychellois sweet dessert of ripe plantains and sweet potatoes simmered in vanilla-scented coconut milk.
recipes.story: Ladob is the beloved Seychellois dessert that comes in both sweet and savory versions. The sweet version features ripe plantains and sweet potatoes bathed in vanilla-infused coconut milk. Seychelles is one of the few places that grows its own vanilla, and ladob showcases this precious ingredient in its most comforting form.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Place coconut milk, water, sugar, vanilla bean and seeds, nutmeg, and salt in a pot. Bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potato chunks first as they take longer to cook. Simmer until slightly softened.

steps.title: Add Plantains
steps.instruction: Add plantain halves. Continue simmering gently until both are tender but still holding their shape.
steps.tip: Do not stir too vigorously — you want the plantains and sweet potatoes to stay intact.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: If sauce is too thin, gently remove fruit and reduce the coconut sauce until creamy. Return fruit.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve warm in bowls with plenty of the vanilla coconut sauce spooned over. Remove vanilla pod before serving.
steps.tip: Ladob is equally delicious served cold the next day — the flavors deepen overnight.

collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Shark Chutney
recipes.description: Seychellois condiment of boiled shark meat mashed with bilimbi, lime, onion, and turmeric — tangy and bold.
recipes.story: Shark chutney (satini reken) is one of the most distinctive dishes in Seychellois cuisine. Shark meat is boiled, skinned, and mashed with bilimbi (a sour tropical fruit), lime, and onion to create a tangy, textured condiment. It is a testament to the Seychellois tradition of using every part of the ocean's bounty.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Shark
steps.instruction: Place shark fillet in a pot of salted water. Boil until cooked through and firm. Drain, remove any skin, and flake the meat finely.
steps.tip: Boiling removes any strong flavor from the shark — drain well.

steps.title: Cook Bilimbi
steps.instruction: In a pan, heat oil and saute onion, garlic, and ginger until soft. Add bilimbi and cook until soft and pulpy.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add flaked shark meat, turmeric, scotch bonnet, and lime juice to the pan. Mash and stir until well combined and mixture has a rough paste-like consistency.
steps.tip: The chutney should be chunky, not smooth — the texture is important.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Cook 2 more minutes, stirring constantly, to meld flavors.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Let cool to room temperature. Serve as a condiment alongside rice, lentils, or grilled fish.
steps.tip: Shark chutney keeps well refrigerated for 3-4 days and the flavor improves over time.

recipes.name: Akara
recipes.description: Crispy deep-fried bean fritters made from black-eyed pea batter seasoned with onions and peppers. A beloved breakfast and snack across Sierra Leone.
recipes.story: Akara was brought to Sierra Leone by the Krio people, descendants of freed slaves who returned from the Americas and Britain. These golden fritters are a bridge between African and diaspora foodways.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend soaked, skinned black-eyed peas with onion, scotch bonnet, salt, and just enough water to form a thick, smooth batter.
steps.tip: The batter should be thick — too thin and the akara won't hold shape.

steps.title: Whip the batter
steps.instruction: Beat the batter vigorously with a wooden spoon for 3-5 minutes until it becomes light and airy.
steps.tip: Whipping incorporates air for a fluffy texture.

steps.title: Heat oil
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pan to 180C.

steps.title: Fry the akara
steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls of batter into the hot oil. Fry in batches until golden brown on all sides, about 3-4 minutes per batch.
steps.tip: Turn once for even browning.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Serve hot with pap or bread.

collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Groundnut Stew
recipes.description: A thick, creamy peanut stew with chicken simmered in a tomato and onion base. Sierra Leone's most comforting one-pot meal.
recipes.story: Groundnut stew is the second most popular dish in Sierra Leone after cassava leaf. The creamy, nutty broth is the ultimate comfort food, especially during the rainy season when warmth and sustenance are needed most.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken and boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve stock.

steps.title: Fry the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry onions until golden. Add blended tomatoes and cook 10 minutes until reduced.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in 2 cups chicken stock. Pour into the tomato base and stir well.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Add chicken pieces and scotch bonnets. Simmer 20 minutes until thick and creamy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over white rice or with fufu.

recipes.name: Bariis Iskukaris
recipes.description: Somali spiced rice with tender goat, raisins, and a fragrant blend of cumin, cardamom, and cinnamon.
recipes.story: Bariis Iskukaris is the centerpiece of Somali feasts, prepared for Eid celebrations, weddings, and Friday lunches. The name means "mixed rice" and reflects the Somali genius for spice blending, influenced by centuries of trade across the Indian Ocean. Each family guards their xawaash spice blend recipe as a treasured secret.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Brown goat meat on all sides. Add one diced onion, garlic, and half the xawaash. Add water to cover and simmer until tender. Reserve broth.
steps.tip: Cook the meat until very tender — the broth becomes the flavorful liquid for the rice.

steps.title: Caramelize Onions
steps.instruction: In a separate pot, heat oil and caramelize the remaining onion until golden brown.

steps.title: Toast Spices
steps.instruction: Add remaining xawaash, cumin, cardamom, and cinnamon to the onions. Stir for 1 minute until fragrant.

steps.title: Cook Rice
steps.instruction: Add drained rice and raisins. Pour in 4 cups of the reserved meat broth. Bring to boil, reduce to lowest heat, cover tightly, and cook until rice is fluffy.
steps.tip: Use the meat broth instead of water — this is what makes Somali rice so flavorful.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fluff rice and gently fold in the cooked meat. Let rest covered for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound on a large platter, garnish with cilantro. Serve with banana and salad on the side.
steps.tip: Somalis traditionally eat bariis with a banana — the sweetness complements the spices.

recipes.name: Canjeero
recipes.description: Somali fermented pancake — spongy, slightly sour, and served for breakfast with butter, sugar, or stew.
recipes.story: Canjeero (also called laxoox or lahoh) is the Somali breakfast staple, similar to Ethiopian injera but lighter and thinner. The batter ferments overnight to develop its sour tang and bubbly texture. Every Somali morning begins with canjeero drizzled with butter and sugar, or topped with liver and onions for a hearty start.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Mix Batter
steps.instruction: Combine flour, cornmeal, sugar, yeast, and salt. Add warm water and whisk until smooth with no lumps. The batter should be thinner than pancake batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover batter loosely and let ferment at room temperature for at least 3 hours, or overnight for best flavor.
steps.tip: Overnight fermentation gives the best sour flavor and bubbly texture.

steps.title: Stir Batter
steps.instruction: The batter will be bubbly. Give it a gentle stir. It should pour easily — add water if too thick.

steps.title: Cook Canjeero
steps.instruction: Heat a non-stick pan over medium heat. Pour batter in a thin layer, swirling to cover. Cook until the surface is covered in holes and edges lift. Do not flip.
steps.tip: Like injera, canjeero is only cooked on one side — the top should be spongy with many eyes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fold canjeero into quarters. Drizzle with melted butter and sprinkle with sugar. Or serve alongside stew for dipping.
steps.tip: For a traditional Somali breakfast, serve with suqaar (sauteed meat) and a cup of shaah (spiced tea).

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Suqaar
recipes.description: Somali sauteed meat with peppers, onions, and aromatic spices — a quick, flavorful everyday dish.
recipes.story: Suqaar is the everyday Somali dish that appears at breakfast, lunch, and dinner in different forms. The word means "small cuts" in Somali, referring to the finely diced meat. It showcases the Somali preference for lean, aromatic meat dishes seasoned with cumin and coriander. Quick to prepare, it fuels the nation.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sear Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan over high heat. Add diced beef and sear until browned, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: Keep the heat high so the meat sears rather than steams — work in batches if needed.

steps.title: Add Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and jalapeno. Cook until softened.

steps.title: Spice
steps.instruction: Add cumin, coriander, and turmeric. Stir to coat the meat evenly.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add peppers and tomatoes. Cook until peppers are tender and tomatoes have broken down into a sauce.
steps.tip: The tomatoes should cook down to a thick coating, not a watery sauce.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Serve suqaar with canjeero for breakfast, or with rice or pasta for lunch and dinner.
steps.tip: Somalis love suqaar with spaghetti — a legacy of Italian colonial influence.

recipes.name: Chakalaka
recipes.description: A vibrant, spicy vegetable relish made with beans, tomatoes, peppers, and carrots — a staple side dish at every South African braai and gathering.
recipes.story: Chakalaka was born in the gold mining townships of Johannesburg, where workers from across Southern Africa combined whatever vegetables and spices they had on hand to create a fiery relish. The dish quickly spread beyond the mines and became an essential part of South African food culture, especially at braais where no spread is complete without a bowl of chakalaka.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sauté aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the onions and fry until translucent, about 4 minutes. Add garlic and curry powder, stirring for 1 minute until fragrant.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the grated carrots and diced peppers. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the vegetables begin to soften.

steps.title: Add tomatoes and beans
steps.instruction: Stir in the chopped tomatoes and chilli peppers. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens and the vegetables are tender.
steps.tip: Adjust the chilli to your heat preference — some like it fiery, others mild.

steps.title: Add beans and finish
steps.instruction: Add the baked beans, stir gently, and simmer for another 10 minutes. Season with salt and pepper to taste.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let sit for 5 minutes before serving. Chakalaka can be served warm or at room temperature alongside pap and braai meat.
steps.tip: Chakalaka tastes even better the next day as the flavors develop overnight.

recipes.name: Asida
recipes.description: South Sudanese smooth wheat porridge shaped into a dome, served with stew poured around it — simple and filling.
recipes.story: Asida is a smooth, dense porridge made from wheat or sorghum flour that is a staple across South Sudan and the broader Sahel region. Its simplicity belies its importance — asida has sustained communities through times of plenty and hardship alike. The dome shape and the crater for stew make it both beautiful and functional.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Water
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of water to a boil in a heavy pot. Add salt and oil.

steps.title: Add Flour
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium-low. Gradually add flour while stirring constantly and vigorously with a wooden spoon. The mixture should become very smooth with no lumps.
steps.tip: Stir in one direction only to develop a smooth, cohesive texture.

steps.title: Cook Through
steps.instruction: Continue stirring until the asida is very thick, smooth, and pulls cleanly from the sides of the pot.
steps.tip: The asida is ready when it holds its shape and has a shiny, smooth surface.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Wet a deep plate. Turn asida onto it and shape into a smooth dome using a wet spoon. Make a well in the center.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Pour stew around the base and into the well. Drizzle with ghee or honey. Eat by pinching off pieces and dipping into the stew.
steps.tip: Asida can be served sweet with honey and ghee for breakfast, or savory with mulah for dinner.
