collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Bamia
recipes.description: South Sudanese okra and lamb stew with tomatoes, onions, and garlic — thick, hearty, and rich in flavor.
recipes.story: Bamia is the classic okra stew of South Sudan, a dish shared across the Nile Valley cuisines. The okra gives the stew its characteristic thick, silky texture that South Sudanese cooks treasure. Bamia is the kind of dish grandmothers make to feed their families — simple, nourishing, and deeply comforting.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Lamb
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over high heat. Season lamb with salt, pepper, cumin, and coriander. Sear until browned on all sides.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and garlic. Cook until softened. Add tomato paste and stir for 1 minute. Add chopped tomatoes.

steps.title: Simmer Meat
steps.instruction: Add water. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer until lamb is nearly tender.
steps.tip: Lamb should be almost falling apart before you add the okra.

steps.title: Add Okra
steps.instruction: Add sliced okra to the stew. Stir gently and continue simmering until okra is tender and has thickened the stew.
steps.tip: The okra releases a natural thickener — stir gently to keep the rounds intact.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning with salt. Garnish with cilantro. Serve hot with kisra, asida, or rice.
steps.tip: Bamia should be thick and rich — if too thin, simmer uncovered until it reaches the right consistency.

recipes.name: Kisra with Mulah
recipes.description: South Sudan's staple fermented sorghum flatbread served with a rich meat and okra stew.
recipes.story: Kisra is the daily bread of South Sudan, a thin fermented sorghum crepe that is the foundation of every meal. Mulah, meaning stew, varies by region and season but always features locally available ingredients. Together, kisra and mulah represent the culinary identity of the world's youngest nation, carrying forward food traditions that predate its borders.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Ferment Kisra Batter
steps.instruction: Mix sorghum flour with warm water to form a thin batter. Cover and ferment in a warm place for at least 8 hours or overnight.
steps.tip: The fermentation gives kisra its signature sour taste — do not skip this step.

steps.title: Cook Mulah
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Brown beef on all sides. Add onions and garlic, cook until softened. Add tomatoes and water. Simmer until beef is tender.

steps.title: Add Okra
steps.instruction: Add sliced okra and peanut butter to the stew. Stir well and simmer until okra is tender and stew is thick.
steps.tip: The okra and peanut butter together create a thick, silky sauce.

steps.title: Cook Kisra
steps.instruction: Stir fermented batter and add salt. Heat a non-stick pan. Pour a thin layer of batter, tilting to spread. Cook until set and edges lift. Do not flip.
steps.tip: Kisra should be paper-thin — pour as little batter as possible.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Roll or fold kisra and arrange on a plate. Ladle mulah alongside or on top. Eat by tearing kisra and scooping the stew.
steps.tip: In South Sudan, meals are communal — everyone eats from one shared platter.

recipes.name: Basbousa
recipes.description: A sweet, syrup-soaked semolina cake scented with rose water and topped with almonds — Sudan's favourite celebratory dessert.
recipes.story: Basbousa is the dessert that marks every Sudanese celebration, from Eid feasts to weddings and births. The semolina gives it a unique grainy texture that absorbs the fragrant sugar syrup like a sponge. Every household has a slightly different recipe, but the rose water-scented syrup poured over the hot cake is the constant. The diamond-shaped cuts with an almond on each piece make basbousa as beautiful as it is delicious.
recipes.best_served_with: Sudanese coffee (jabana) or tea with milk
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the syrup
steps.instruction: Combine 1.5 cups sugar and 1 cup water in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, add lemon juice, and simmer for 8 minutes until slightly thickened. Stir in rose water and set aside to cool completely.
steps.tip: The syrup MUST be cold when poured on the hot cake — this is the secret to proper absorption.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.instruction: Combine the semolina, 1 cup sugar, yoghurt, melted butter, baking powder, and coconut. Mix well and let rest for 30 minutes so the semolina absorbs the moisture.
steps.tip: Resting is essential — it softens the semolina for a smoother cake.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Spread the batter evenly in a greased 23×33cm baking dish. Score into diamond shapes and press an almond into each piece. Bake for 25-30 minutes until golden.
steps.tip: Score before baking so the syrup penetrates deeply into each piece.

steps.title: Soak with syrup
steps.instruction: Remove the hot cake from the oven and immediately pour the cold syrup evenly over the entire surface. Let it sit for at least 30 minutes to absorb.
steps.tip: You will hear the satisfying sizzle when the cold syrup hits the hot cake.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Re-cut along the scored lines and serve at room temperature. Basbousa keeps well covered at room temperature for 2-3 days.
steps.tip: In Sudan, basbousa is always served with strong coffee or sweet tea with milk.

recipes.name: Kisra with Mullah Tagalia
recipes.description: Thin, fermented sorghum crepes (kisra) served with Sudan's aromatic dried meat and onion stew — the daily meal of millions.
recipes.story: Kisra is to Sudan what injera is to Ethiopia — the essential bread that defines the cuisine. Made from fermented sorghum batter, it is cooked paper-thin on a wide, hot plate. Mullah tagalia is a robust stew built on dried meat (sharmout), caramelised onions, and peanut butter, creating layers of smoky, nutty, and savoury flavours. Together, they form the foundational meal of Sudanese home cooking.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare kisra batter
steps.instruction: Mix sorghum flour with warm water and a pinch of yeast to form a thin, pourable batter (thinner than crepe batter). Cover and let ferment at room temperature for 12-24 hours until slightly sour.
steps.tip: Traditional fermentation uses no yeast — just time. The natural bacteria create the sour tang.

steps.title: Cook the kisra
steps.instruction: Heat a large flat pan or griddle (doka). Pour a thin layer of batter and quickly spread it in a circular motion using a palette or the back of a spoon. Cook until set and edges lift, about 1 minute. Do not flip — kisra is cooked on one side only.
steps.tip: Sudanese women are expert at spreading kisra paper-thin with a special gourd tool called a mafrash.

steps.title: Build the mullah
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pot. Fry the onions over medium heat, stirring often, until deeply caramelised and dark brown, about 20 minutes. Add the dried meat, tomatoes, coriander, and 2 cups of water. Simmer for 20 minutes.
steps.tip: The dark onions are the soul of tagalia — don't rush this step.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Stir in the peanut butter until fully dissolved. Simmer for another 10 minutes until the stew is thick and rich. Season with salt.
steps.tip: The peanut butter thickens the stew and adds a nutty depth.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Lay kisra on a large platter or individual plates. Ladle the mullah over the top. Eat by tearing pieces of kisra and scooping the stew.
steps.tip: In Sudanese tradition, everyone eats from one large shared platter.

recipes.name: Chipsi Mayai
recipes.description: Tanzania's beloved street food — a crispy french fry omelette, golden on the outside and loaded with chips inside.
recipes.story: Chipsi Mayai, literally "chips eggs" in Swahili, is Tanzania's most iconic street food. Found at every corner food stall in Dar es Salaam, this indulgent combination of french fries and eggs is the ultimate comfort food. It is the go-to meal for students, workers, and anyone needing a quick, affordable, and filling bite.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Fry Chips
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pan to 170C. Fry potato chips in batches until golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Double frying makes crispier chips — fry once at lower heat, rest, then fry again at higher heat.

steps.title: Prepare Egg Mix
steps.instruction: Beat eggs with salt, pepper, diced onion, tomato, and chili.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Place fried chips in a hot oiled skillet. Pour beaten egg mixture over the chips, making sure eggs fill all the gaps.
steps.tip: Press chips down so they are fully coated in egg.

steps.title: Cook Omelette
steps.instruction: Cook on medium heat until the bottom is golden and set. Carefully flip using a plate and cook the other side until golden.
steps.tip: Use a plate larger than the pan to flip — slide omelette onto plate, then invert back into pan.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Slide onto a plate and cut into wedges. Serve hot with ketchup and pili pili sauce on the side.
steps.tip: Best eaten immediately while chips are still crispy inside the omelette.

recipes.name: Tanzanian Pilau
recipes.description: Zanzibar-influenced spiced rice with meat, potatoes, and a blend of whole spices cooked in caramelized onions.
recipes.story: Tanzanian Pilau reflects the Zanzibari spice trade that shaped East African cuisine for centuries. The island of Zanzibar, once the world's largest clove producer, infused mainland cooking with cardamom, cinnamon, and cumin. This pilau is distinguished from its Kenyan cousin by the addition of potatoes and a slightly different spice balance.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Toast Spices
steps.instruction: Dry-toast cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, cumin, and peppercorns in a hot pan until fragrant. Set aside.
steps.tip: Toasting whole spices releases their essential oils for deeper flavor.

steps.title: Caramelize Onions
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Add onions and cook until deeply browned and caramelized.

steps.title: Brown Meat
steps.instruction: Add beef and garlic to onions. Sear until browned. Add toasted spices and potatoes. Stir to coat.

steps.title: Cook Pilau
steps.instruction: Add drained rice and hot stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly and cook until rice is tender and liquid absorbed.
steps.tip: Do not lift the lid during cooking — the steam does the work.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Remove from heat. Let rest covered for 10 minutes, then fluff with a fork. Serve garnished with fresh lime wedges.

collections.name: Party Favorites
collections.description: Festive dishes that bring people together. These crowd-pleasers are served at celebrations across Africa.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Ugali na Nyama
recipes.description: Tanzanian staple of firm maize porridge paired with slow-simmered beef stew in a rich tomato sauce.
recipes.story: Ugali na Nyama is the everyday meal of Tanzania, from Dar es Salaam to the slopes of Kilimanjaro. The beef stew (mchuzi wa nyama) varies by region — coastal versions add coconut milk, while inland versions use tomatoes and peppers. It is the dish that every Tanzanian mother teaches first.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Beef
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Season beef with salt and pepper, then sear until browned on all sides.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and garlic, cook until softened. Add bell peppers, tomatoes, and curry powder. Stir well.
steps.tip: Crush the tomatoes with your spoon to release their juices.

steps.title: Simmer Stew
steps.instruction: Pour in beef stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer until beef is fork-tender.
steps.tip: Low heat and patience make the beef meltingly tender.

steps.title: Make Ugali
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups water to a boil. Gradually add maize flour, stirring vigorously until firm and pulling from sides of pot.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Turn ugali onto a wet plate in a dome shape. Ladle the beef stew alongside. Serve hot.
steps.tip: Tanzanians say the stew should be thick enough to cling to the ugali, not soupy.

recipes.name: Akoumé
recipes.description: A firm corn flour dough similar to polenta, steamed until solid and served as the starchy base for Togolese soups and stews.
recipes.story: Akoumé is Togo's everyday staple, the equivalent of fufu but made from corn flour. Its neutral flavour makes it the perfect vehicle for the bold, spicy stews and sauces of Togolese cuisine.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make slurry
steps.instruction: Mix 1 cup corn flour with 1 cup cold water to a smooth paste.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups water to boil with salt. Pour in slurry, stirring constantly. Cook 5 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: Add remaining flour gradually, stirring vigorously until very thick and smooth. Cook 10 more minutes.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl, scoop akoumé in, smooth top, turn out as a mound. Serve with stew.

recipes.name: Fufu with Gboma Dessi
recipes.description: Smooth pounded yam fufu served with gboma dessi — a rich spinach and tomato stew with smoked fish and crab. A classic Ewe combination.
recipes.story: Gboma Dessi is the quintessential Ewe stew, made with gboma (a local variety of spinach) and enriched with smoked fish and crab. Paired with smooth pounded yam fufu, it is the ultimate comfort meal in Togo.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the fufu
steps.instruction: Boil yam pieces until very soft, about 20 minutes. Pound in a mortar or mash until completely smooth with no lumps.
steps.tip: Alternatively, use instant pounded yam flour.

steps.title: Prepare the stew base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Fry onions until soft. Add blended tomatoes and cook until reduced and oil separates, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Add fish and greens
steps.instruction: Add smoked fish and stir. Add the chopped greens and cook for 10 minutes until wilted and integrated into the sauce.

steps.title: Season and simmer
steps.instruction: Season with salt and pepper. Simmer 5 more minutes until thick. The stew should coat a spoon.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape fufu into smooth balls and serve in a bowl alongside the gboma dessi.

recipes.name: Brik a l'Oeuf
recipes.description: A crispy Tunisian pastry turnover filled with a whole egg, tuna, capers, and parsley, fried until the paper-thin malsouka shell shatters at the first bite.
recipes.story: Brik is a Tunisian national treasure, particularly the classic version with a runny egg sealed inside a crispy malsouka (or warka) pastry. Eating brik is a test of skill — the goal is to bite in without the egg yolk dripping down your chin. It is a staple of Ramadan tables, street stalls, and family dinners across the country.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the filling base
steps.instruction: Mix together the flaked tuna, sauteed onion, capers, parsley, salt, and pepper in a bowl.

steps.title: Assemble the brik
steps.instruction: Lay a sheet of malsouka pastry on a flat surface. Spoon a quarter of the tuna mixture onto one half. Make a small well in the centre and crack a whole egg into it. Season the egg with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Work quickly — the pastry dries out fast.

steps.title: Fold and seal
steps.instruction: Carefully fold the pastry over to form a half-moon shape, pressing the edges to seal. Be gentle so the egg yolk stays intact.
steps.tip: A dab of egg white along the edges helps seal the pastry.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep skillet to 180C (355F). Carefully slide the brik into the hot oil and fry for about 1-2 minutes per side until deep golden and crispy.
steps.tip: The egg should remain runny inside — do not overcook.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on paper towels. Serve immediately with lemon wedges and harissa.
steps.tip: Eat immediately — brik waits for no one.

recipes.name: Ojja with Merguez
recipes.description: A spicy Tunisian egg and tomato skillet loaded with harissa, merguez sausage, and peppers. Tunisia's answer to shakshuka, with a bolder, fierier personality.
recipes.story: Ojja is a staple of Tunisian home cooking, particularly popular as a quick lunch or brunch. Unlike its milder cousins across the Mediterranean, Tunisian ojja is unapologetically spicy thanks to a generous dose of harissa. The addition of merguez sausage makes it a hearty, satisfying one-pan meal that comes together in minutes.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the merguez
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a deep skillet over medium heat. Add the merguez slices and cook until browned and slightly crispy, about 5 minutes. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: In the same skillet, cook the green pepper and garlic for 2 minutes. Add the harissa, caraway, and a pinch of salt. Stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add the chopped tomatoes and simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens and reduces. Return the merguez to the skillet.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick, not watery.

steps.title: Add the eggs
steps.instruction: Make 4 wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each. Cover the skillet and cook for 5-6 minutes until the egg whites are set but the yolks are still runny.
steps.tip: For set yolks, cook a minute or two longer with the lid on.

steps.title: Serve in the skillet
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and serve straight from the skillet with plenty of crusty bread for dipping.
steps.tip: Bring the skillet to the table for a rustic, communal meal.

recipes.name: Luwombo
recipes.description: A royal Ugandan dish of chicken, beef, or groundnut sauce steamed in banana leaves, locking in extraordinary flavor.
recipes.story: Luwombo was created in 1887 by the personal chef of Kabaka Mwanga II, the king of Buganda. It remains the most prestigious dish in Ugandan cuisine, served at weddings, coronations, and important gatherings. The banana leaf wrapping steams the ingredients to incredible tenderness while infusing a subtle earthy aroma.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Banana Leaves
steps.instruction: Pass banana leaves briefly over an open flame to soften them and prevent tearing. Wipe clean with a damp cloth.
steps.tip: The leaves should become pliable and darker green — do not char them.

steps.title: Mix Filling
steps.instruction: In a large bowl, combine chicken, ground peanuts, onions, tomatoes, bell peppers, garlic, ginger, mushrooms, salt, pepper, and water. Mix thoroughly.
steps.tip: Mixing the ground peanuts into the raw sauce ensures they cook into a rich, thick gravy.

steps.title: Wrap Parcels
steps.instruction: Place a portion of the mixture onto each banana leaf. Fold the leaf into a secure parcel, tying with banana fiber or kitchen string.
steps.tip: Ensure parcels are tightly sealed so steam cannot escape during cooking.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Place parcels in a large steamer or pot with a raised rack and water in the bottom. Cover tightly and steam over medium heat until chicken is very tender.

steps.title: Check Doneness
steps.instruction: Carefully open one parcel to check that chicken is cooked through and sauce is thick. If needed, reseal and steam longer.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place unopened parcels on plates — let guests unwrap their own. Serve with matooke (steamed green bananas) or posho.
steps.tip: The aroma when opening the parcel at the table is part of the experience.

collections.name: Sunday Specials

recipes.name: Matoke
recipes.description: Steamed and mashed green bananas cooked in a savory sauce — Uganda's national dish and daily staple.
recipes.story: Matooke is the soul food of the Baganda people and the undisputed national dish of Uganda. Green cooking bananas are steamed in their own leaves until soft, then mashed to a smooth golden paste. In Buganda culture, a meal without matooke is not a real meal, and a woman's cooking skills are often judged by the quality of her matooke.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Bananas
steps.instruction: Peel green bananas and place in a pot lined with banana leaves. The sap can stain, so oil your hands before peeling.
steps.tip: Rub cooking oil on your hands before peeling to prevent the sticky sap from staining.

steps.title: Make Sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onions until soft, add garlic, tomatoes, bell peppers, and curry powder. Cook until tomatoes break down into a sauce.

steps.title: Combine and Steam
steps.instruction: Pour the sauce over the bananas. Add water. Wrap banana leaves over the top to seal. Cover pot with a tight lid and steam over low heat.
steps.tip: The banana leaves create a steam chamber that gives matooke its distinctive flavor.

steps.title: Mash
steps.instruction: Once bananas are completely soft, mash them in the pot using a wooden spoon, mixing with the sauce until smooth and golden.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound the matooke on a platter, still in its banana leaf. Serve with groundnut sauce, beans, or meat stew.
steps.tip: Matooke should be smooth and lump-free — keep mashing until it reaches a uniform consistency.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Rolex
recipes.description: Uganda's famous street food — a chapati rolled around an egg omelette with fresh vegetables, eaten on the go.
recipes.story: The Rolex is Uganda's most beloved street food, found on virtually every street corner in Kampala. The name is a playful contraction of "rolled eggs." Born from the creativity of street vendors, this simple wrap has become a national icon and was even considered for UNESCO cultural heritage recognition.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, salt, oil, and warm water into a soft dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Rest 15 minutes covered.

steps.title: Roll and Fry Chapati
steps.instruction: Divide dough into 2 balls. Roll each into a thin circle. Fry in an oiled pan until golden on both sides. Set aside.
steps.tip: A good chapati should be flaky — brush with oil between layers as you cook.

steps.title: Make Omelette
steps.instruction: Beat eggs with salt. Pour into a hot oiled pan, adding onions, tomato, cabbage, and pepper on top. Cook until just set.

steps.title: Roll
steps.instruction: Place the omelette on top of a chapati. Roll tightly into a cylinder, tucking the edges in.
steps.tip: Roll while the omelette is still warm so it molds to the chapati.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Wrap the bottom half in paper or foil for easy handling. Serve immediately while hot.
steps.tip: A true Kampala Rolex is eaten standing at the street vendor's stall.

recipes.name: Sahrawi Vegetable Couscous
recipes.description: A simple, nourishing Sahrawi couscous steamed with onions, turnips, carrots, and squash in a light broth. Desert cooking at its most pure and satisfying.
recipes.story: Couscous is a staple across the Sahrawi diet, prepared more simply than in neighbouring Morocco or Algeria due to the limited ingredients available in the desert environment. Sahrawi women prepare couscous by hand-rolling semolina grains, a skill passed from mother to daughter. This vegetable version is the everyday meal, served family-style on a large communal platter.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the vegetable broth
steps.instruction: Place the onion, carrots, and turnips in the bottom of a couscoussier or large pot. Add 1.5 litres of water, olive oil, and salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer.

steps.title: First steam of couscous
steps.instruction: Moisten the couscous with salted water, raking it with your fingers to separate the grains. Place in the steamer basket over the simmering vegetables. Steam uncovered for 15 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not cover the steamer — the steam needs to pass through freely.

steps.title: Add squash and rest couscous
steps.instruction: Remove the couscous and spread on a tray. Sprinkle with water and rake again to break clumps. Add the pumpkin to the broth below. Return the couscous to the steamer for a second steam of 15 minutes.
steps.tip: Two steamings give the lightest, most separate grains.

steps.title: Final steam
steps.instruction: Repeat the moistening process and steam a third time for 10 minutes. The couscous should be fluffy, light, and each grain separate.
steps.tip: Three steamings is traditional and makes a real difference in texture.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound the couscous on a large communal platter. Arrange the vegetables on top and ladle broth over everything. Serve from the shared platter.
steps.tip: Sahrawi custom is to eat from the edges of the platter, working inward.

recipes.name: Chikanda
recipes.description: Zambian "African polony" — a firm savory cake made from wild orchid tubers and ground peanuts, sliced and served cold.
recipes.story: Chikanda is one of Africa's most unique foods, made from the tubers of wild terrestrial orchids (chikanda tubers) mixed with ground peanuts and chili. Often called "African polony" for its firm, sliceable texture, it is a beloved snack in Zambia. The orchid tubers give it a distinctive bouncy texture unlike any other food.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Mixture
steps.instruction: Combine chikanda powder, ground peanuts, baking soda, chili flakes, salt, and grated onion in a bowl. Mix thoroughly.
steps.tip: The chikanda powder must be very finely ground for a smooth final texture.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Add water gradually while stirring to form a thick paste. Transfer to an oiled pot. Cook over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens and begins to pull away from the pot.
steps.tip: Stir constantly to prevent burning — the mixture thickens significantly as it cooks.

steps.title: Set
steps.instruction: Pour the cooked mixture into an oiled loaf pan or mold. Press down firmly to remove air pockets. Let cool completely at room temperature.

steps.title: Chill
steps.instruction: Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until firm and set.
steps.tip: Chikanda firms up as it cools — it should slice cleanly like polony.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Turn out of the mold and slice into rounds or squares. Serve cold as a snack or appetizer.
steps.tip: Chikanda is traditionally sold sliced on the street — it is a grab-and-go Zambian snack.

recipes.name: Kapenta
recipes.description: Tiny dried sardines fried with tomatoes and onions — a protein-packed Zambian staple from Lake Kariba.
recipes.story: Kapenta are tiny freshwater sardines from Lake Kariba and Lake Tanganyika that have become one of Zambia's most important protein sources. Sun-dried and sold in markets across the country, these little fish are an affordable nutritional powerhouse. The fishing boats that catch kapenta at night using bright lights are a magical sight on the lake.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Rinse Kapenta
steps.instruction: Rinse dried kapenta in cold water to remove excess salt and any grit. Drain well.
steps.tip: Some cooks soak kapenta for 10 minutes to reduce saltiness — taste first and decide.

steps.title: Fry Kapenta
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Add kapenta and fry, stirring frequently, until lightly crispy.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and green pepper. Saute until softened. Add tomatoes and curry powder.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Cook until tomatoes break down and form a thick sauce coating the kapenta. Season with salt if needed.
steps.tip: Kapenta are already salty from drying — taste before adding extra salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot alongside nshima. Kapenta is also delicious with rice or as a snack on its own.
steps.tip: Kapenta are eaten whole — head, bones, and all — which makes them rich in calcium.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

recipes.name: Nshima with Ifisashi
recipes.description: Zambia's staple maize porridge served with a rich peanut and greens sauce — hearty, creamy, and satisfying.
recipes.story: Nshima is the cornerstone of Zambian cuisine — no meal is complete without it. Ifisashi, a peanut-based vegetable relish, is one of the most popular accompaniments. The combination provides a complete protein and is eaten daily in homes across the country. Zambians say you have not eaten until you have eaten nshima.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook Greens
steps.instruction: Boil rape leaves in salted water with a pinch of baking soda until tender. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make Ifisashi Sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Saute onion until soft. Add tomatoes and cook until broken down. Stir in peanut butter and water. Simmer until thick and creamy.
steps.tip: Stir constantly when adding peanut butter to prevent lumps and sticking.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add cooked greens to the peanut sauce. Stir well and simmer together for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Make Nshima
steps.instruction: Bring 4 cups water to a boil. Add a third of the maize meal, stirring to make a thin porridge. Cook 5 minutes, then gradually add remaining meal, stirring vigorously until very thick and pulling from pot sides.
steps.tip: A proper nshima should be firm enough to shape with a wet spoon.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Scoop nshima into a wet bowl and turn onto a plate in a smooth dome. Serve ifisashi alongside. Eat by pinching nshima and dipping.
steps.tip: Zambians eat nshima with the right hand — roll a ball, press a dip in the center, and scoop the relish.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Dovi
recipes.description: Zimbabwean peanut butter chicken stew — tender chicken simmered in a rich, creamy peanut and tomato sauce.
recipes.story: Dovi is one of Zimbabwe's most beloved stews, showcasing the country's love affair with peanut butter (dovi literally means peanut butter in Shona). This creamy, rich stew is comfort food at its finest, served at family dinners across the country. The peanut sauce transforms simple chicken into something extraordinary.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Chicken
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Season chicken with salt and pepper, then brown on all sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook Base
steps.instruction: Saute onion and garlic until softened. Add tomatoes and curry powder. Cook until tomatoes break down.

steps.title: Add Peanut Butter
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in water until smooth. Pour into the pot and stir well. Return chicken.
steps.tip: Mix peanut butter with water separately first to prevent lumps in the stew.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer until chicken is tender and sauce is thick.
steps.tip: Stir occasionally to prevent the peanut sauce from sticking to the bottom.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve dovi over sadza or rice. The thick peanut sauce should coat the back of a spoon.
steps.tip: Dovi is even better reheated the next day as the peanut sauce intensifies.

recipes.name: Madora
recipes.description: Crispy fried mopane worms sauteed with tomatoes, onions, and peanuts — Zimbabwe's protein-rich delicacy.
recipes.story: Madora (mopane worms) are the caterpillars of the emperor moth, harvested from mopane trees in the bushveld. They are one of southern Africa's most important indigenous food sources, packed with more protein than beef per gram. Once a rural subsistence food, madora are now a prized delicacy served even in upscale restaurants.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Soak Madora
steps.instruction: Soak dried mopane worms in warm water for 15 minutes until slightly softened. Drain and squeeze out excess water.
steps.tip: Do not over-soak — they should be pliable but still have some chewiness.

steps.title: Fry Madora
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Fry the madora until crispy on the outside, stirring frequently.
steps.tip: Fry in a single layer for maximum crispiness.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and chili. Cook until softened. Add tomatoes and cook until they form a thick sauce.

steps.title: Combine and Finish
steps.instruction: Toss the fried madora in the tomato sauce. Add crushed peanuts. Cook 3 more minutes to meld flavors.
steps.tip: The peanuts add a complementary crunch and nuttiness.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot alongside sadza as a protein-rich relish, or enjoy on their own as a snack.
steps.tip: Madora are a sustainable protein source — embrace this ancient food tradition.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Mapopo Candy
recipes.description: Crystallized papaya pieces slow-cooked in sugar syrup until translucent and jewel-like — a traditional Zimbabwean sweet treat made from green papaya.
recipes.story: Mapopo candy is a treasured confection in Zimbabwe, passed down through generations of home cooks. Made from unripe green papaya that is peeled, sliced, and slowly candied in sugar syrup, the resulting translucent, jewel-toned sweets are a point of pride at church gatherings, weddings, and holiday celebrations. The process requires patience but the result is a unique treat found nowhere else.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the papaya
steps.instruction: Peel the green papaya, cut in half, and scoop out the seeds. Cut the flesh into 2cm cubes. Soak in salted water for 30 minutes, then drain and rinse well.
steps.tip: The salt soak helps firm up the papaya so it holds its shape during cooking.

steps.title: Pre-boil
steps.instruction: Place the papaya cubes in a pot, cover with water, and boil for 10 minutes until slightly softened. Drain thoroughly.

steps.title: Make the syrup
steps.instruction: In a clean pot, combine the sugar and water. Heat over medium, stirring until the sugar dissolves completely. Add lemon juice and food coloring if using.

steps.title: Candy the papaya
steps.instruction: Add the drained papaya cubes to the syrup. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook on low heat for 60-75 minutes, stirring very gently every 15 minutes, until the papaya turns translucent and the syrup thickens.
steps.tip: Do not stir too vigorously or the pieces will break apart.

steps.title: Dry the candy
steps.instruction: Using a slotted spoon, remove the candied papaya pieces and place on a wire rack or parchment paper. Let them dry for several hours or overnight until they develop a slightly firm, crystallized exterior.
steps.tip: In Zimbabwe, these are often dried in the sun for the best texture.

steps.title: Store and serve
steps.instruction: Once dried, toss lightly in extra sugar if desired. Store in an airtight container. Serve as a sweet snack with tea.
steps.tip: Mapopo candy keeps well for weeks in a cool, dry place.

recipes.name: Sadza with Muriwo
recipes.description: Zimbabwe's national dish — thick maize porridge served with sauteed leafy greens in a tomato and onion base.
recipes.story: Sadza is to Zimbabwe what rice is to Asia — the foundation of every meal. Made from finely ground white maize, it is eaten at least twice a day by most Zimbabweans. Muriwo (leafy greens) is the most common relish, and a meal of sadza nemuriwo connects every Zimbabwean to home, no matter where they are in the world.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start Sadza
steps.instruction: Bring 4 cups water to a boil. Mix a cup of maize meal with cold water to form a slurry. Pour into boiling water, stirring. Cook thin porridge for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken Sadza
steps.instruction: Gradually add remaining maize meal, stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon until very thick and smooth. Cook 5 more minutes.
steps.tip: The sadza is ready when it cleanly pulls away from the pot and holds its shape.

steps.title: Cook Muriwo
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onion and garlic until soft. Add tomatoes and cook down. Add greens and cook until wilted and tender.
steps.tip: For extra richness, stir a spoonful of peanut butter into the greens.

steps.title: Shape Sadza
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl, scoop sadza into it, and turn onto a plate to form a smooth dome.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place sadza dome on plate with muriwo alongside. Eat by pinching off pieces of sadza and scooping up the greens.
steps.tip: In Shona culture, washing hands before and after eating sadza is essential etiquette.
